name: MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB

topics.title: Implement and demonstrate the FIND-S algorithm for finding the most specific hypothesis based on a given set of training data samples. Read the training data from a .CSV file.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL508 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement and demonstrate the FIND-S algorithm for finding the most specific hypothesis based on a given set of training data samples. Read the training data from a .CSV file.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Learning (Mitchell, 1997):** The inferential task of acquiring a general rule (Boolean-valued function) from a set of input-output training examples, where the output is restricted to a discrete set of values (typically `yes` / `no`, or `+` / `−`).

**FIND-S** is a *concept learning algorithm* that searches the hypothesis space of attribute-based conjunctive descriptions, traversing it along the **general-to-specific** ordering, and converges on the **Most Specific Hypothesis (MSH)** that is *consistent* with all observed positive training instances.

**Hypothesis Representation:**
A hypothesis $h$ in FIND-S is a conjunction of constraints on $n$ attributes:

$$h = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n \rangle$$

where each constraint $a_i$ may take one of three forms:

| Constraint Symbol | Meaning |
|:-----------------:|:--------|
| `?` (wildcard) | Any value is acceptable for this attribute |
| Specific value $v$ | The attribute must take exactly value $v$ |
| `Ø` (null) | No instance satisfies this hypothesis |

**Hypothesis Space $\mathcal{H}$:** The Cartesian product of all legal constraints across all attributes.

$$|\mathcal{H}| = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( \vert D_i \vert + 1 \right) + 1$$

where $\vert D_i \vert$ is the number of distinct values attribute $i$ can take. The trailing `+1` accounts for the `Ø` (null) hypothesis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Resume Shortlister" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> Imagine you are an HR recruiter asked to shortlist candidates for a *single specific role* — "Senior Python Developer with AWS experience, willing to relocate, and who knows TensorFlow." You have a pile of resumes (training examples). You start with a maximally restrictive blank template that says *nothing* about the candidate. As you read each resume that was **hired** (positive example), you start *relaxing* the template just enough to include that person. By the time you finish, the template describes the **most specific profile** that covers every successful hire but excludes every rejected candidate. That final template is the **Most Specific Hypothesis** returned by FIND-S.

## 1.3 Why "Most Specific" and Not "Most General"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> FIND-S deliberately picks the **most specific** consistent hypothesis because, under the **Closed-World Assumption**, anything that is not provably positive is treated as negative. The most specific hypothesis is the *smallest* region in instance space that still covers every positive example, and therefore the *strongest inductive bias* against unseen negatives.

## 1.4 Formal Preliminaries

- **Instance space $\mathcal{X}$** — the set of all possible attribute vectors.
- **Target concept $c : \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \{0, 1\}$** — the unknown Boolean function being learned.
- **Training example** — a pair $\langle x, c(x) \rangle$.
- **Positive example** — an instance $x$ for which $c(x) = 1$ (e.g., `EnjoySport = yes`).
- **Negative example** — an instance $x$ for which $c(x) = 0$ (e.g., `EnjoySport = no`).
- **Consistency** — A hypothesis $h$ is *consistent* with a training example $\langle x, c(x) \rangle$ iff $h(x) = c(x)$.
- **Satisfies:** $x$ satisfies $h$ iff $h(x) = 1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The General-to-Specific Ordering ($\geq_g$)

A partial order $\geq_g$ (pronounced "more general than or equal to") is defined on the hypothesis space such that:

$$h_1 \geq_g h_2 \iff \forall x \in \mathcal{X} : \big[ h_2(x) = 1 \Rightarrow h_1(x) = 1 \big]$$

Equivalently, $h_1$ is more general than $h_2$ if and only if $h_1$'s constraint set is a **superset** of the instances that $h_2$ covers.

**Lattice Intuition:** The hypothesis space forms a *lattice* in which:

- The **maximally specific** hypothesis is $\langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \ldots, \emptyset \rangle$ — no instance satisfies it.
- The **maximally general** hypothesis is $\langle ?, ?, \ldots, ? \rangle$ — every instance satisfies it.

FIND-S performs a *breadth-climbing search* that starts at the bottom (most specific) and walks only **upward** through this lattice, never sideways or downward.

## 2.2 FIND-S Algorithm — Operational Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Inputs:** A set of positive training examples $D^+$, attributes $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$.  
> **Output:** A hypothesis $h \in \mathcal{H}$ that is consistent with all positive examples in $D$.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Initialization** — Initialize $h$ to the *most specific* hypothesis in $\mathcal{H}$:
   $$h \leftarrow \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \ldots, \emptyset \rangle$$
2. **Iterate** over each training example $x = \langle a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n, c(x) \rangle$.
3. **Filter** — if $x$ is a *negative* example, discard it (FIND-S ignores negatives entirely).
4. **Consistency check** — if $h$ already satisfies $x$ (i.e., $h(x) = 1$), do nothing; proceed to the next example.
5. **Generalize minimally** — for each attribute $a_i$ in $h$:
   - If the constraint $h_i$ is *satisfied* by $x_i$ (i.e., $h_i = x_i$ or $h_i = ?$), leave it unchanged.
   - Otherwise, replace $h_i$ with the wildcard `?` (the minimum relaxation that accommodates $x$).
6. **Termination** — return $h$ after processing all training examples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalization rule (minimum generalization):** When a constraint $h_i$ must be relaxed, the *least* relaxation is the wildcard `?`. This preserves the conjunction semantics and ensures the resulting hypothesis remains the *most specific* hypothesis still consistent with all observed positives.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:---------------|:------|
| 1 | Hypothesis size (instance count) | $\vert \mathcal{H} \vert = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left( \vert D_i \vert + 1 \right) + 1$ | Add 1 for the null hypothesis `Ø` |
| 2 | General-to-specific | $h_1 \geq_g h_2 \iff (\forall x)\, h_2(x)=1 \Rightarrow h_1(x)=1$ | Partial order on $\mathcal{H}$ |
| 3 | Satisfaction | $h(x) = 1$ iff $\forall i \in \{1,\ldots,n\} : (h_i = ? \;\vert\; h_i = x_i)$ | Conjunctive evaluation |
| 4 | Minimal generalization | $h_i^{\text{new}} = ?$ when $h_i^{\text{old}} \neq x_i$ and $h_i^{\text{old}} \neq ?$ | Only on positive mismatches |
| 5 | Initialization | $h \leftarrow \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \ldots, \emptyset \rangle$ | Most specific in $\mathcal{H}$ |
| 6 | Update trigger | $h \leftarrow h$ if $h(x) = 1$; else apply rule (4) | Skip negatives |
| 7 | Output invariance | Final $h$ is **unique** w.r.t. $D^+$ if $D^+$ is non-contradictory | FIND-S returns *the* MSH |
| 8 | Time complexity | $O(\vert D^+ \vert \cdot n)$ | Linear in positives and attributes |
| 9 | Space complexity | $O(n)$ | Single hypothesis vector |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> - **Rule-extraction shells** in early expert systems used FIND-S-like logic to build diagnostic rules from examples.
> - **Fraud-detection seed rules** in financial ML are often bootstrapped from a few confirmed fraud cases using the same generalization principle.
> - **Biomedical feature selection** historically used FIND-S to derive the *minimum conjunction of symptoms* that uniquely characterizes a disease cohort.
> - In the modern era, FIND-S is the **pedagogical foundation** for understanding how learners like **ID3, C4.5, and Candidate-Elimination** generalize from examples.

## 2.5 Critical Limitations (Frequently Tested)

| # | Limitation | Implication |
|:-:|:-----------|:------------|
| 1 | Ignores negative examples | Cannot detect over-generalization |
| 2 | No backtracking | Cannot undo a wrong generalization |
| 3 | Output is *the* MSH, not *all* consistent hypotheses | No measure of uncertainty |
| 4 | Depends on attribute ordering | Path-dependent on the data presentation order in some edge cases |
| 5 | Sensitive to noise | A single mislabeled positive can over-generalize the hypothesis |
| 6 | Fails on disjunctive concepts | Cannot learn $A \lor B$ style target concepts |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Reference Dataset (EnjoySport — Mitchell, 1997)

For the exhaustive walkthrough, we adopt the canonical *EnjoySport* dataset stored in a CSV file (`enjoysport.csv`):

| # | Sky | AirTemp | Humidity | Wind | Water | Forecast | EnjoySport |
|:-:|:---:|:-------:|:--------:|:----:|:-----:|:--------:|:----------:|
| 1 | Sunny | Warm | Normal | Strong | Warm | Same | **Yes ( + )** |
| 2 | Sunny | Warm | High | Strong | Warm | Same | **Yes ( + )** |
| 3 | Rainy | Cold | High | Strong | Warm | Change | **No ( − )** |
| 4 | Sunny | Warm | High | Strong | Cool | Change | **Yes ( + )** |

The attribute set is $\mathcal{A} = \{\text{Sky}, \text{AirTemp}, \text{Humidity}, \text{Wind}, \text{Water}, \text{Forecast}\}$, so $n = 6$.

**Hypothesis Initialization:**

$$h^{(0)} = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle$$

## 3.2 Iteration 1 — Process Example $x_1 = \langle Sunny, Warm, Normal, Strong, Warm, Same, + \rangle$

**Satisfaction test:**

$$h^{(0)}(x_1) = 1 \iff \forall i : h_i = x_i \lor h_i = ?$$

Since every $h_i^{(0)} = \emptyset \neq x_i$, we have $h^{(0)}(x_1) = 0$. Generalization is required.

**Component-wise update (apply Rule 4 to every position):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1^{(1)} &= ? \quad \text{(Sky: } \emptyset \neq \text{Sunny)} \\
h_2^{(1)} &= ? \quad \text{(AirTemp: } \emptyset \neq \text{Warm)} \\
h_3^{(1)} &= ? \quad \text{(Humidity: } \emptyset \neq \text{Normal)} \\
h_4^{(1)} &= ? \quad \text{(Wind: } \emptyset \neq \text{Strong)} \\
h_5^{(1)} &= ? \quad \text{(Water: } \emptyset \neq \text{Warm)} \\
h_6^{(1)} &= ? \quad \text{(Forecast: } \emptyset \neq \text{Same)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$h^{(1)} = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$$

> **Insight:** The first positive example always lifts the hypothesis to the *maximally general* point in the lattice, because the initial `Ø` hypothesis cannot satisfy anything.

## 3.3 Iteration 2 — Process Example $x_2 = \langle Sunny, Warm, High, Strong, Warm, Same, + \rangle$

**Satisfaction test:** $h^{(1)}(x_2) = 1$ because every $h_i = ?$ trivially accepts $x_2$. No update is required.

$$h^{(2)} = h^{(1)} = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$$

## 3.4 Iteration 3 — Process Example $x_3 = \langle Rainy, Cold, High, Strong, Warm, Change, - \rangle$

**Action:** Because $x_3$ is a **negative** example, FIND-S **skips it entirely** (Rule 6 in the cheat sheet).

$$h^{(3)} = h^{(2)} = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$$

> This is a key algorithmic decision. Negative examples are never used to *restrict* the hypothesis in FIND-S, which is precisely why the Candidate-Elimination algorithm is required when one needs to bound the hypothesis from both sides.

## 3.5 Iteration 4 — Process Example $x_4 = \langle Sunny, Warm, High, Strong, Cool, Change, + \rangle$

**Satisfaction test:** Compare each $x_4[i]$ with $h^{(3)}[i]$:

| $i$ | Attribute | $h^{(3)}_i$ | $x_{4,i}$ | Match? |
|:--:|:---------|:-----------:|:--------:|:------:|
| 1 | Sky | ? | Sunny | ✅ |
| 2 | AirTemp | ? | Warm | ✅ |
| 3 | Humidity | ? | High | ✅ |
| 4 | Wind | ? | Strong | ✅ |
| 5 | Water | ? | Cool | ✅ |
| 6 | Forecast | ? | Change | ✅ |

Since $h^{(3)}(x_4) = 1$, no update is required.

$$h^{(4)} = h^{(3)} = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$$

## 3.6 The Counter-Intuitive Result

The final hypothesis is:

$$\boxed{\,h_{\text{FIND-S}} = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle \equiv \text{``EnjoySport always''}\,}$$

**Why is this happening?** The dataset is *too easy* — every positive example is fully absorbed by the wildcard template produced from the first instance. This is one of the most commonly tested pitfalls in the KTU lab examination.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Many students submit a hypothesis with concrete values like $\langle Sunny, Warm, ?, Strong, ?, ? \rangle$ after Example 1 and never update it. While that hypothesis is *also* consistent with all four training examples, FIND-S as defined in Mitchell's textbook performs the **uniform wildcard replacement** shown above. The algorithmic **final hypothesis is $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$** for this dataset. Always trace the algorithm step-by-step rather than writing a "common-sense" rule.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Hypotheses Generated at Each Step

| Step | Input Example | Class | Action | Resulting Hypothesis |
|:----:|:--------------|:-----:|:-------|:--------------------|
| Init | — | — | $h \leftarrow \langle \emptyset^6 \rangle$ | $\langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle$ |
| 1 | $x_1$ | + | Generalize all 6 attrs | $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ |
| 2 | $x_2$ | + | No-op (already satisfied) | $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ |
| 3 | $x_3$ | − | Skip (negative ignored) | $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ |
| 4 | $x_4$ | + | No-op (already satisfied) | $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ |

## 3.8 Reference Implementation (Python with `csv` Module)

```python
import csv

# ---------- 1. Load the CSV ----------
def load_csv(path):
    with open(path, newline='') as f:
        reader = csv.reader(f)
        rows = [r for r in reader if r]          # drop blank lines
    header = rows[0]                              # ['Sky','AirTemp',...,'EnjoySport']
    data   = rows[1:]
    return header, data

# ---------- 2. Initialize hypothesis to the most specific ----------
def initialize_hypothesis(num_attrs):
    return ['∅'] * num_attrs                     # <∅, ∅, …, ∅>

# ---------- 3. Test if an example satisfies the current hypothesis ----------
def satisfies(h, x):
    for h_i, x_i in zip(h, x):
        if h_i != '?' and h_i != x_i:
            return False
    return True

# ---------- 4. FIND-S core ----------
def find_s(data):
    num_attrs = len(data[0]) - 1                  # last col is the label
    h = initialize_hypothesis(num_attrs)
    for row in data:
        x, label = row[:-1], row[-1].strip().lower()
        if label not in ('yes', 'y', '+', '1', 'true'):
            continue                              # ignore negatives
        if satisfies(h, x):
            continue                              # already consistent
        for i in range(num_attrs):
            if h[i] != '?' and h[i] != x[i]:
                h[i] = '?'                        # minimal generalization
    return h

# ---------- 5. Driver ----------
if __name__ == '__main__':
    header, data = load_csv('enjoysport.csv')
    final_h = find_s(data)
    print('Final Hypothesis:', final_h)
```

**Expected console output for `enjoysport.csv`:**

```
Final Hypothesis: ['?', '?', '?', '?', '?', '?']
```

## 3.9 Using the `pandas` Alternative (Industry Standard)

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv('enjoysport.csv')
positives = df[df['EnjoySport'].str.lower().isin(['yes','y','+','1','true'])]

h = ['∅'] * (df.shape[1] - 1)
for _, row in positives.iterrows():
    x = row.iloc[:-1].tolist()
    if all(hi == '?' or hi == xi for hi, xi in zip(h, x)):
        continue
    h = ['?' if (hi != '?' and hi != xi) else hi for hi, xi in zip(h, x)]

print('Most Specific Hypothesis:', h)
```

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FIND-S Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load CSV] --> B[Read Header and Data Rows]
    B --> C[Initialize h to most specific: <empty, empty, ...>]
    C --> D[For each training example x in D]
    D --> E{Is x a negative example?}
    E -- Yes --> D
    E -- No --> F{Does h already satisfy x?}
    F -- Yes --> D
    F -- No --> G[For each attribute position i from 1 to n]
    G --> H{Is constraint h_i not equal to ? and not equal to x_i?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Set h_i to wildcard ?]
    H -- No --> J[Keep h_i unchanged]
    I --> K{All attribute positions processed?}
    J --> K
    K -- No --> G
    K -- Yes --> D
    D --> L[No more examples]
    L --> M[Output final hypothesis h]
    M --> N[End]
```

## 4.2 General-to-Specific Hypothesis Lattice (n = 2 attributes)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Top["<?, ?>\n(Most General)"]:::top
    MidL["Sunny, ?"]:::mid
    MidR["?, Warm"]:::mid
    MidA["Sunny, Warm"]:::mid
    MidB["Rainy, Warm"]:::mid
    MidC["Sunny, Cold"]:::mid
    MidD["Rainy, ?"]:::mid
    MidE["?, Cold"]:::mid
    Bot["<empty, empty>\n(Most Specific)"]:::bot

    Top --- MidL
    Top --- MidR
    Top --- MidD
    Top --- MidE
    MidL --- MidA
    MidL --- MidC
    MidR --- MidA
    MidR --- MidB
    MidA --- Bot
    MidB --- Bot
    MidC --- Bot
    MidD --- Bot
    MidE --- Bot

    classDef top fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef mid fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#78350f
    classDef bot fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
```

**Reading the Lattice:** FIND-S begins at `Bot` and climbs **upward** (never sideways, never downward). Each upward edge corresponds to replacing a single `empty` constraint with a specific value, or relaxing a specific value to `?`. The algorithm terminates at the **lowest node in the lattice that is consistent with every positive example**.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Lab Program

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IN[Input Layer]
        CSV[enjoysport.csv]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Processing Layer]
        L[CSV Loader\nstdlib csv / pandas]
        I[Initialiser\nh = empty x n]
        IT[Iterator over D+]
        SAT[Satisfaction Test\nh and x]
        GEN[Generaliser\nreplace mismatched attr with ?]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output Layer]
        RES[Final Hypothesis h]
        LOG[Console Trace\nstep by step dump]
    end

    CSV --> L --> I
    I --> IT
    IT --> SAT
    SAT -- "h and x already consistent" --> IT
    SAT -- "mismatch detected" --> GEN --> IT
    IT -- "no more rows" --> RES
    IT -. "optional verbose flag" .-> LOG
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define a hypothesis in the context of concept learning. List the three legal forms of an attribute constraint in the FIND-S hypothesis representation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *hypothesis* in concept learning is a candidate Boolean-valued function $h : \mathcal{X} \to \{0, 1\}$ that purports to approximate an unknown target concept $c$ over the instance space $\mathcal{X}$. In FIND-S, a hypothesis is represented as a **conjunction of constraints**, one per attribute:

1. **A specific value** $v$ — the attribute must take exactly the value $v$ for $h$ to fire.
2. **The wildcard `?`** — any value is acceptable for the attribute (no constraint).
3. **The null `Ø`** — the hypothesis is unsatisfiable by any instance; this is the **initial** state of FIND-S.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three forms with one-line explanation each.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Why does FIND-S ignore negative training examples? Mention one consequence of this design choice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

FIND-S operates by *climbing* the hypothesis lattice from the most specific point upwards. Negative examples can only force the hypothesis to become *more specific* (i.e., move **downward** in the lattice), but the algorithm has no descent operator. Therefore, processing negatives would require a backtracking or alternative-hypothesis mechanism that FIND-S simply does not possess.

**Consequence (1 mark):** The final hypothesis can become *over-general* if the training data contains labeling noise or if the positive set is not a representative sample — a critical limitation that motivates the **Candidate-Elimination** algorithm.

---

## Part B — Full-Stack Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) (7 marks)** Trace the execution of the **FIND-S** algorithm on the following training data. Show the hypothesis after every positive example and state the final hypothesis.

| Sky | AirTemp | Humidity | Wind | Water | Forecast | EnjoySport |
|:---:|:-------:|:--------:|:----:|:-----:|:--------:|:----------:|
| Sunny | Cool | High | Strong | Warm | Same | Yes |
| Rainy | Cool | Normal | Light | Warm | Change | No |
| Sunny | Cool | High | Strong | Cool | Change | Yes |

**(b) (7 marks)** Write a Python program (using the `csv` module) that implements FIND-S, reads the above data from `weather.csv`, and prints the most specific hypothesis.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Trace (7 marks)**

**Initial hypothesis:**

$$h^{(0)} = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle$$

**Process $x_1 = \langle Sunny, Cool, High, Strong, Warm, Same, + \rangle$ (1 mark):**

Since $h^{(0)}$ is unsatisfiable, generalize every attribute. Result:

$$h^{(1)} = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$$

**Process $x_2 = \langle Rainy, Cool, Normal, Light, Warm, Change, - \rangle$ (1 mark):**

Negative example → **skip**. No update:

$$h^{(2)} = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$$

**Process $x_3 = \langle Sunny, Cool, High, Strong, Cool, Change, + \rangle$ (3 marks):**

Check each attribute against $h^{(2)}$:

| $i$ | $h^{(2)}_i$ | $x_{3,i}$ | Action |
|:--:|:----------:|:---------:|:-------|
| 1 (Sky) | ? | Sunny | keep `?` |
| 2 (AirTemp) | ? | Cool | keep `?` |
| 3 (Humidity) | ? | High | keep `?` |
| 4 (Wind) | ? | Strong | keep `?` |
| 5 (Water) | ? | Cool | keep `?` |
| 6 (Forecast) | ? | Change | keep `?` |

$h^{(2)}(x_3) = 1$ → no update. **Final hypothesis (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{\,h_{\text{FIND-S}} = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle\,}$$

**Part (b) — Python Program (7 marks)**

```python
import csv

def load_data(path):
    with open(path, newline='') as f:
        rows = [r for r in csv.reader(f) if r]
    return rows[0], rows[1:]

def find_s(rows):
    n = len(rows[0]) - 1
    h = ['∅'] * n
    for row in rows:
        x, label = row[:-1], row[-1].strip().lower()
        if label not in ('yes', 'y', '+', '1', 'true'):
            continue
        if all(hi == '?' or hi == xi for hi, xi in zip(h, x)):
            continue
        for i in range(n):
            if h[i] != '?' and h[i] != x[i]:
                h[i] = '?'
    return h

if __name__ == '__main__':
    header, data = load_data('weather.csv')
    print('Final Hypothesis:', find_s(data))
```

*(2 marks for correct CSV loading, 2 marks for the FIND-S logic, 2 marks for negative-example handling, 1 mark for clean output.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **general-to-specific ordering** $\geq_g$ on the hypothesis space. With $n = 2$ attributes where each attribute can take values from $\{A, B\}$, enumerate the size of the hypothesis space and list at least 4 hypotheses that are more general than $\langle A, B \rangle$ but more specific than $\langle ?, ? \rangle$.

**(b) (7 marks)** Discuss **three limitations** of the FIND-S algorithm. For each limitation, suggest **one** improvement or alternative algorithm that addresses it.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — General-to-Specific Ordering (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):** Given two hypotheses $h_1$ and $h_2$ over the same attribute set, $h_1$ is *more general than or equal to* $h_2$, written $h_1 \geq_g h_2$, iff for every instance $x \in \mathcal{X}$:

$$h_2(x) = 1 \;\Longrightarrow\; h_1(x) = 1$$

In plain words: every instance that satisfies $h_2$ also satisfies $h_1$. The relation is a *partial order* (reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive).

**Hypothesis space size (2 marks):** With $n = 2$ attributes, each having 2 values, plus the wildcard and the null:

$$|\mathcal{H}| = (2 + 1)^2 + 1 = 9 + 1 = 10 \text{ hypotheses}$$

The 10 hypotheses are: $\langle \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle$, $\langle A, \emptyset \rangle$, $\langle B, \emptyset \rangle$, $\langle \emptyset, A \rangle$, $\langle \emptyset, B \rangle$, $\langle A, A \rangle$, $\langle A, B \rangle$, $\langle B, A \rangle$, $\langle B, B \rangle$, $\langle ?, ? \rangle$. *(Some texts count `Ø` as the 10th.)*

**Hypotheses strictly between $\langle A, B \rangle$ and $\langle ?, ? \rangle$ (3 marks):** A hypothesis is more general than $\langle A, B \rangle$ iff it covers all instances that $\langle A, B \rangle$ covers (i.e., the single instance $(A, B)$) *and possibly more*. The four valid answers are:

1. $\langle A, ? \rangle$ — covers $(A, A)$ and $(A, B)$.
2. $\langle ?, B \rangle$ — covers $(A, B)$ and $(B, B)$.
3. $\langle ?, A \rangle$ — wait, this does **not** cover $(A, B)$. ❌ — corrected: the correct set is $\{\langle A, ? \rangle, \langle ?, B \rangle, \langle B, ? \rangle \text{ (if it covers } (A,B) \text{? no) }\}$. Final correct list:
   - $\langle A, ? \rangle$
   - $\langle ?, B \rangle$
   - $\langle ?, ? \rangle$ (the maximum)
   - $\langle B, ? \rangle$ covers $(B, A), (B, B)$ — does **not** cover $(A, B)$, so it is **not** more general than $\langle A, B \rangle$.

The strictly intermediate valid hypotheses are: $\langle A, ? \rangle$, $\langle ?, B \rangle$, and $\langle ?, ? \rangle$. *(Examiner will accept any 4 from the strict chain between the two; partial credit for plausible candidates.)*

**Part (b) — Limitations and Improvements (7 marks)**

| # | Limitation (1 mark) | Improvement (1+ mark) |
|:-:|:--------------------|:----------------------|
| 1 | FIND-S ignores **negative examples** | Use the **Candidate-Elimination** algorithm which maintains both the most-specific and most-general consistent boundary sets |
| 2 | Output is a **single hypothesis**; no uncertainty estimate | Use **Bayesian** or **ensemble** learners (e.g., Naive Bayes) to obtain a probabilistic concept description |
| 3 | Cannot learn **disjunctive** target concepts (e.g., $A \lor B$) | Move to a **rule-induction** learner such as **CN2** or **RIPPER** that handles disjuncts |
| 4 | No **noise tolerance** | Apply **probabilistic** learners (e.g., logistic regression) or **pruning** techniques in decision-tree learning |

*(Allocate ~2 marks per limitation + 1 mark for the improvement; distribute remaining marks for completeness.)*

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marks lost on this experiment:**
> 1. **Skipping the initial `Ø` step** — students often start with `<?, ?, …, ?>` and lose **1 mark** for the initialization.
> 2. **Writing the wrong constraint symbol** — using `0` (zero) or `None` instead of `Ø` or `'∅'` may be considered correct *only if* the program still functions; otherwise, deduct **1 mark** for typographical confusion.
> 3. **Confusing `pandas.read_csv` with `csv.reader`** — both are valid, but the program must explicitly demonstrate the *generalization step*, not merely print a `DataFrame`. Deduct **2 marks** if the candidate does not implement the actual hypothesis-update logic.
> 4. **Treating the output as a "rule" rather than a hypothesis vector** — write it as a tuple/list of constraints, not as an English sentence. Deduct **1 mark** for missing formal notation.
> 5. **Not handling the case where there are no positive examples** — the final hypothesis would remain `Ø`; the code should not crash. Bonus **1 mark** for graceful handling.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **FIND-S** is a *concept learning* algorithm that finds the **most specific hypothesis** consistent with all **positive** training examples.
- Hypothesis format: **conjunction of attribute constraints**, each being either a specific value, a `?` (wildcard), or `Ø` (null).
- Initialize $h$ to the most specific hypothesis: $h \leftarrow \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \ldots, \emptyset \rangle$.
- Iterate over the dataset; **skip every negative example**.
- For each positive example not already satisfied, apply the **minimum generalization** rule: replace every mismatched non-wildcard constraint with `?`.
- The algorithm performs a **general-to-specific** search along the partial order $\geq_g$.
- The hypothesis space size is $|\mathcal{H}| = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \big( |D_i| + 1 \big) + 1$.
- The output is **unique** for any non-contradictory positive training set.
- Time complexity: $O(|D^+| \cdot n)$; space: $O(n)$.
- **Major limitations:** ignores negatives, no backtracking, single-hypothesis output, no noise tolerance, fails on disjunctive concepts.
- **Mitigations:** Candidate-Elimination (handles negatives), Bayesian learners (uncertainty), rule-induction systems (disjunctions), probabilistic models (noise).
- The **EnjoySport** dataset is the canonical KTU textbook example — master its CSV layout and step-by-step trace.
- In Python, the implementation must show: (i) CSV reading, (ii) initialization, (iii) negative skipping, (iv) satisfaction test, (v) generalization step, (vi) final print.
- For KTU lab records, **always include**: aim, algorithm, dataset, code, output, and a 2–3 line conclusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: For a given set of training data examples stored in a .CSV file, implement and demonstrate the Candidate-Elimination algorithm to output a description of the set of all hypotheses consistent with the training examples.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL508 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** For a given set of training data examples stored in a .CSV file, implement and demonstrate the Candidate-Elimination algorithm to output a description of the set of all hypotheses consistent with the training examples.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Candidate-Elimination Algorithm** is a *concept learning* algorithm in Machine Learning that incrementally computes the **Version Space** — the set of *all hypotheses* in the hypothesis space that are *consistent* with the observed training examples.

Formally, given:
- A set of training examples $D$ partitioned into **Positive Examples** ($D^+$) and **Negative Examples** ($D^-$)
- A hypothesis space $H$ consisting of conjunctions of attribute constraints drawn from the set $\{\emptyset, ?, \text{Specific Value}\}$

The algorithm maintains two boundary sets:
- **S (Specific Boundary):** The set of *most specific* hypotheses that cover all positive examples and exclude all negative examples.
- **G (General Boundary):** The set of *most general* hypotheses that cover all positive examples and exclude all negative examples.

The **Version Space** $VS_{H,D}$ is defined as:

$$
VS_{H,D} = \{ h \in H \mid \text{Consistent}(h, D) \}
$$

where *Consistent* means $h$ classifies every positive example as positive and every negative example as negative.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Detective's Notebook Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a detective trying to identify a *mystery suspect* based on witness reports. The detective keeps two notebooks:
>
> - **Notebook S (Specific):** Lists only what is *definitely true* about the suspect. Initially just "the suspect exists" (the most specific empty constraint). As positive reports come in ("the suspect was a tall man wearing a red hat"), the notebook is refined — but never too loosely.
> - **Notebook G (General):** Lists what is *possibly true* — the broadest suspects that fit. As negative reports come in ("it was NOT a short woman"), the notebook tightens to exclude innocent parties.
>
> At the end, every hypothesis **between S and G** (specific enough to match S, but general enough to match G) is a *plausible suspect*. The Candidate-Elimination algorithm mechanically maintains these two notebooks for any concept learning problem.

## 1.3 Key Vocabulary & Constants

- **Hypothesis** $h$: A candidate rule expressed as a conjunction over attributes.
- **Most General Hypothesis:** $\langle ?, ?, \dots, ? \rangle$ — accepts every instance.
- **Most Specific Hypothesis:** $\langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \dots, \emptyset \rangle$ — accepts no instance.
- **Specialization Operator:** Replaces `?` with a specific value, or `∅` with `?` or a specific value.
- **Generalization Operator:** Replaces a specific value with `?`.
- **Consistency:** $h$ is consistent with example $x$ if $h(x) = \text{label}(x)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hypothesis Representation

For an instance space with $n$ attributes $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n$, every hypothesis $h$ is a vector:

$$
h = \langle c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_n \rangle
$$

where each constraint $c_i$ belongs to the set:

$$
c_i \in \{ \emptyset \} \cup \{?\} \cup \{ \text{value}_1, \text{value}_2, \dots \}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Effect on Classification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Specific value` (e.g., `Red`) | Attribute must equal this value | Accepts only matching instances |
| `?` | Any value is acceptable | Wildcard — does not restrict |
| `∅` (null) | No value is acceptable | Rejects **every** instance |

## 2.2 Operational Concept: The "More General-Than" Relation

A hypothesis $h_i$ is **more general than** $h_j$ (denoted $h_i \geq_g h_j$) iff every instance accepted by $h_j$ is also accepted by $h_i$, and they are not equal.

- Example: $\langle \text{Sunny}, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle \geq_g \langle \text{Sunny}, \text{Hot}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$

This partial order forms the **Generalization Hierarchy**, the lattice on which the algorithm searches.

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Initialize $S_0$** with the **most specific hypothesis** $\langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \ldots, \emptyset \rangle$.
2. **Initialize $G_0$** with the **most general hypothesis** $\langle ?, ?, \ldots, ? \rangle$.
3. For each training example $d = \langle x, c \rangle$:
   - If $c = \text{Yes}$ (positive): Generalize $S$ minimally, and remove from $G$ any hypothesis that does not cover $x$.
   - If $c = \text{No}$ (negative): Specialize $G$ minimally so it excludes $x$, and remove from $S$ any hypothesis that incorrectly covers $x$.
4. **Termination:** Return the final sets $S$ and $G$ — the version space is the bounded set of all consistent hypotheses.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Version Space | $VS = \{ h \in H \mid \text{Consistent}(h, D) \}$ | Bounded by $S$ and $G$ |
| Lower Bound | $S = \max_g \{ h \in VS \}$ | Most specific boundary |
| Upper Bound | $G = \min_g \{ h \in VS \}$ | Most general boundary |
| Coverage Test | $h \text{ covers } x$ iff $\forall i: h_i = x_i \text{ or } h_i = ?$ | — |
| Generalization | Replace specific value with `?` in $S$ for positive example | Minimal change only |
| Specialization | Replace `?` with specific value in $G$ for negative example | Minimal change only |
| Consistency Check | $\text{Consistent}(h, D) = \forall \langle x,c\rangle \in D: h(x) = c$ | Required for all examples |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Candidate-Elimination algorithm underpins:
- **Rule-based diagnostic systems** in medical AI.
- **Decision support engines** in industrial fault classification.
- **Foundational concept learning** — basis for advanced inductive logic programming (e.g., FOIL, Progol).
- **Explainable AI (XAI):** Generates human-readable rule sets, vital where transparency is mandated.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Worked Example — "EnjoySport" Dataset

Consider the classic Mitchell (1997) EnjoySport problem with **6 attributes**: `Sky, AirTemp, Humidity, Wind, Water, Forecast`. Target concept: days on which my friend enjoys water sports (`Yes`).

### Training Data (stored in `enjoysport.csv`)

| Example | Sky | AirTemp | Humidity | Wind | Water | Forecast | EnjoySport |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Sunny | Warm | Normal | Strong | Warm | Same | **Yes** |
| 2 | Sunny | Warm | High | Strong | Warm | Same | **Yes** |
| 3 | Rainy | Cold | High | Strong | Warm | Change | **No** |
| 4 | Sunny | Warm | High | Strong | Cool | Change | **Yes** |

### Step 0: Initialization

$$
S_0 = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle
$$
$$
G_0 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle
$$

### Step 1: Process Example 1 — $\langle \text{Sunny, Warm, Normal, Strong, Warm, Same}, \text{Yes} \rangle$

Because it is a **positive** example, $S$ must be generalized to cover it, and $G$ is checked for consistency.

**Updating $S$:** Replace each `∅` with the corresponding attribute value from the positive example:

$$
S_1 = \langle \text{Sunny, Warm, Normal, Strong, Warm, Same} \rangle
$$

**Updating $G$:** $G_0 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ covers the example, so it remains unchanged.

$$
G_1 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle
$$

### Step 2: Process Example 2 — $\langle \text{Sunny, Warm, High, Strong, Warm, Same}, \text{Yes} \rangle$

This positive example differs from Example 1 only in the `Humidity` attribute. $S$ must be generalized *minimally* to cover both positive examples.

**Generalize $S$:** For each position, if $S_1$ and Example 2 differ, replace with `?`:

$$
S_2 = \langle \text{Sunny, Warm, ?, Strong, Warm, Same} \rangle
$$

**$G$ remains unchanged:** $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ still covers the example.

$$
G_2 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle
$$

### Step 3: Process Example 3 — $\langle \text{Rainy, Cold, High, Strong, Warm, Change}, \text{No} \rangle$

This is a **negative** example. $G$ must be specialized to exclude it, and $S$ is checked.

**Check $S$:** Does $S_2$ cover the negative example? Sky=`Sunny` vs `Rainy` → No. So $S$ is consistent, no change.

**Specialize $G$:** $G_2 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ currently *covers* the negative example — this is a contradiction. We must specialize $G$ to exclude it while still covering Example 1 and Example 2.

The minimal specializations of $G$ that exclude Example 3 are obtained by replacing `?` with the *value of $G$ that contradicts the example*:

$$
G_3 = \{ \langle \text{Sunny, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Warm, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, \text{Warm}, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, \text{Same} \rangle \}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Each specialization "pins down" one attribute to a value it must NOT equal — effectively ruling out the negative example along that single dimension.

### Step 4: Process Example 4 — $\langle \text{Sunny, Warm, High, Strong, Cool, Change}, \text{Yes} \rangle$

This is a positive example, differing from $S_2$ in `Water` (Warm → Cool) and `Forecast` (Same → Change).

**Update $S$:** Generalize those differing positions to `?`:

$$
S_4 = \langle \text{Sunny, Warm, ?, Strong, ?, ? \rangle
$$

**Update $G$:** Remove any $g \in G_3$ that does *not* cover this positive example:
- $\langle \text{Sunny, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ — covers Example 4? Yes (Sky matches). **Keep.**
- $\langle ?, \text{Warm, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ — covers Example 4? Yes (AirTemp matches). **Keep.**
- $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, \text{Warm}, ? \rangle$ — covers Example 4? Water=Cool ≠ Warm. **Discard.**
- $\langle ?, ?, ?, ?, ?, \text{Same} \rangle$ — covers Example 4? Forecast=Change ≠ Same. **Discard.**

$$
G_4 = \{ \langle \text{Sunny, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Warm, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle \}
$$

### Final Result — Version Space Boundaries

$$
\boxed{S_4 = \langle \text{Sunny, Warm, ?, Strong, ?, ? \rangle}
$$

$$
\boxed{G_4 = \{ \langle \text{Sunny, ?, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Warm, ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle \}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Any hypothesis that is more specific than $S_4$ *and* more general than (or equal to) some member of $G_4$ is consistent with all four training examples.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flow — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Candidate Elimination]) --> loadA[Load CSV Training Data]
    loadA --> initS[Initialize S = phi, phi, phi]
    initS --> initG[Initialize G = question, question, question]
    initG --> loopStart{For each training example}
    loopStart --> checkLabel{Is label == Yes}
    checkLabel -- Yes positive --> posProcess[Generalize S minimally to cover x]
    posProcess --> filterG[Remove g in G that do not cover x]
    filterG --> checkG{G has members with no attribute specific to x}
    checkG -- Yes --> forAllAtt[For every attribute: add minimal generalization to G]
    forAllAtt --> loopStart
    checkG -- No --> loopStart
    checkLabel -- No negative --> negProcess[Check if S covers x]
    negProcess --> checkS{S covers x}
    checkS -- Yes --> remS[Remove s from S]
    remS --> loopStart
    checkS -- No --> specG[Specialize G minimally to exclude x]
    specG --> forEachG{For each g in G}
    forEachG --> canSpec{g can be specialized to exclude x}
    canSpec -- Yes --> addSpec[Add minimal specializations to G]
    addSpec --> forEachG
    canSpec -- No --> remG[Remove g from G]
    remG --> forEachG
    forEachG --> loopStart
    loopStart --> done([Output final S and G sets])
```

## 4.2 Concept Learning Version Space — Boundary Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VS[VERSION SPACE - Bounded by S and G]
        mostGen["Most General = question, question, question, question, question, question"]
        g1["g1: Sunny, question, question, question, question, question"]
        g2["g2: question, Warm, question, question, question, question"]
        middle["Hypotheses in between - consistent with all data"]
        sFinal["S final: Sunny, Warm, question, Strong, question, question"]
        mostSpec["Most Specific = phi, phi, phi, phi, phi, phi"]
    end
    mostGen --> g1
    mostGen --> g2
    g1 --> middle
    g2 --> middle
    middle --> sFinal
    sFinal --> mostSpec
```

## 4.3 Data Processing Pipeline — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CSV File: enjoysport.csv"] --> B["Pandas read_csv Module"]
    B --> C["Separate Attributes X and Labels y"]
    C --> D{"Iterate through examples"}
    D -- Positive --> E["Generalize S boundary"]
    D -- Negative --> F["Specialize G boundary"]
    E --> G["Apply consistency pruning"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Display final version space"]
    H --> I["Hypotheses between S and G"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`
**Define the Version Space in concept learning. State its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Version Space, $VS_{H,D}$, is the set of all hypotheses in hypothesis space $H$ that are consistent with the training examples $D$. It represents the *learner's uncertainty* about the true target concept. Significance: (1) It is a compact representation of all valid rules, (2) It is bounded efficiently by the $S$ and $G$ boundary sets, and (3) Any new instance is classified by majority vote over the version space.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]`
**Differentiate between the Specific Boundary (S) and General Boundary (G) in the Candidate-Elimination algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Specific Boundary $S$ contains the **most specific** hypotheses in the version space — they cover no negative examples but must cover all positive examples. The General Boundary $G$ contains the **most general** hypotheses in the version space — they cover all positive examples but must exclude all negative examples. Together, $S$ and $G$ delimit the entire version space, with $S \leq_g G$ for all $s \in S, g \in G$.

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** For the dataset below, apply the **Candidate-Elimination algorithm** step by step, and derive the final version space (S, G). **(7 marks)**

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| D1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| D2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| D3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| D4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| D5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| D6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| D7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialize:**
$$S_0 = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle, \quad G_0 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$$

**Step 2 — D1: $\langle \text{Sunny, Hot, High, Weak}, \text{No} \rangle$ (Negative):**
- $S_0 = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset, \emptyset \rangle$ does not cover D1 → no change.
- $G_0$ *does* cover D1 → must specialize. For each `?`, replace with D1's value to exclude it. Result:

$$
G_1 = \{ \langle \text{Sunny}, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Hot}, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Weak} \rangle \}
$$

**Step 3 — D2: $\langle \text{Sunny, Hot, High, Strong}, \text{No} \rangle$ (Negative):**
- $S_0$ still does not cover D2 → no change.
- $G_1$ covers D2; only $\langle \text{Sunny}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ covers D2 (others don't match all attributes). So we specialize that:

$$
G_2 = \{ \langle \text{Sunny}, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Hot}, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle \}
$$

(Note: $\langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Weak} \rangle$ doesn't cover D2's Strong wind, so it is already consistent — kept as is.)

**Step 4 — D3: $\langle \text{Overcast, Hot, High, Weak}, \text{Yes} \rangle$ (Positive):**
- $S$ must generalize to cover D3:
$$S_1 = \langle \text{Overcast, Hot, High, Weak} \rangle$$
- $G$ — remove any $g$ that does *not* cover D3:
  - $\langle \text{Sunny}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ — Outlook mismatch → **Discard**.
  - $\langle ?, \text{Hot}, ?, ? \rangle$ — covers D3 → **Keep**.
  - $\langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle$ — covers D3 → **Keep**.

$$
G_3 = \{ \langle ?, \text{Hot}, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle \}
$$

**Step 5 — D4: $\langle \text{Rain, Mild, High, Weak}, \text{Yes} \rangle$ (Positive):**
- Generalize $S$ — differs from $S_1$ in Outlook, Temp, Wind:

$$S_2 = \langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle$$

- $G$ — remove any $g$ not covering D4:
  - $\langle ?, \text{Hot}, ?, ? \rangle$ — Temp=Mild ≠ Hot → **Discard**.
  - $\langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle$ — covers D4 → **Keep**.

$$
G_4 = \{ \langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle \}
$$

**Step 6 — D5: $\langle \text{Rain, Cool, Normal, Weak}, \text{Yes} \rangle$ (Positive):**
- Generalize $S$ — $S_2 = \langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle$ does NOT cover D5 (Humidity=Normal ≠ High). Therefore we must generalize:

$$S_3 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$$

- $G$ — remove any $g$ not covering D5: $\langle ?, ?, \text{High}, ? \rangle$ does not cover D5 → **Discard**.

$$
G_5 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle
$$

**Step 7 — D6: $\langle \text{Rain, Cool, Normal, Strong}, \text{No} \rangle$ (Negative):**
- $S_3 = \langle ?, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ covers D6 → **S becomes empty (contradiction — no consistent hypothesis!)** but in a relaxed implementation we keep $S_3$ as the boundary.
- $G_5$ covers D6 → specialize:

$$
G_6 = \{ \langle \text{Rain}, ?, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, \text{Cool}, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, \text{Normal}, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Strong} \rangle \}
$$

**Step 8 — D7: $\langle \text{Overcast, Cool, Normal, Strong}, \text{Yes} \rangle$ (Positive):**
- Generalize $S$ to include D7's attributes:

$$S_4 = \langle ?, \text{Cool}, \text{Normal}, \text{Strong} \rangle$$

- $G$ — remove any $g$ not covering D7:
  - $\langle \text{Rain}, ?, ?, ? \rangle$ — Outlook mismatch → **Discard**.
  - $\langle ?, \text{Cool}, ?, ? \rangle$ — covers D7 → **Keep**.
  - $\langle ?, ?, \text{Normal}, ? \rangle$ — covers D7 → **Keep**.
  - $\langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Strong} \rangle$ — covers D7 → **Keep**.

$$
\boxed{S_4 = \langle ?, \text{Cool}, \text{Normal}, \text{Strong} \rangle, \quad G_7 = \{ \langle ?, \text{Cool}, ?, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, \text{Normal}, ? \rangle, \langle ?, ?, ?, \text{Strong} \rangle \}}
$$

**(b)** Write a complete **Python program** that reads training data from a `.csv` file and implements the Candidate-Elimination algorithm, then display the final $S$ and $G$ sets. Use the dataset above. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Python Code):**

```python
import csv
import copy

def g_0(n):
    """Most general hypothesis"""
    return ['?'] * n

def s_0(n):
    """Most specific hypothesis"""
    return ['\u00d8'] * n

def is_consistent(hypothesis, example):
    """Check if hypothesis is consistent with example"""
    for i in range(len(hypothesis)):
        if hypothesis[i] != '?' and hypothesis[i] != example[i]:
            return False
    return True

def is_more_general(h1, h2):
    """Check if h1 is more general than h2"""
    for i in range(len(h1)):
        if h2[i] != '\u00d8' and h1[i] != h2[i] and h1[i] != '?':
            return False
    return True

def generalize_S(s, example):
    """Generalize S to cover positive example"""
    new_s = copy.deepcopy(s)
    for i in range(len(new_s)):
        if new_s[i] == '\u00d8':
            new_s[i] = example[i]
        elif new_s[i] != example[i] and new_s[i] != '?':
            new_s[i] = '?'
    return new_s

def specialize_G(g, example, attributes, domains):
    """Specialize G to exclude negative example"""
    specializations = []
    for i in range(len(g)):
        if g[i] == '?':
            for val in domains[attributes[i]]:
                if val != example[i]:
                    new_g = copy.deepcopy(g)
                    new_g[i] = val
                    specializations.append(new_g)
    return specializations

def candidate_elimination(data, attributes, domains):
    n = len(attributes)
    S = [s_0(n)]
    G = [g_0(n)]

    for example in data:
        x = example[:-1]
        label = example[-1]

        if label == 'Yes':
            G = [g for g in G if is_consistent(g, x)]
            S_new = []
            for s in S:
                if not is_consistent(s, x):
                    s_new = generalize_S(s, x)
                    if s_new not in S_new:
                        S_new.append(s_new)
                else:
                    S_new.append(s)
            S = S_new
        else:
            S = [s for s in S if not is_consistent(s, x)]
            G_new = []
            for g in G:
                if is_consistent(g, x):
                    specs = specialize_G(g, x, attributes, domains)
                    G_new.extend(specs)
                else:
                    G_new.append(g)
            G = G_new
    return S, G

# Read CSV
def load_csv(filename):
    with open(filename, 'r') as f:
        reader = csv.reader(f)
        data = [row for row in reader]
    return data

if __name__ == "__main__":
    attributes = ['Outlook', 'Temp', 'Humidity', 'Wind']
    domains = {
        'Outlook': ['Sunny', 'Overcast', 'Rain'],
        'Temp': ['Hot', 'Mild', 'Cool'],
        'Humidity': ['High', 'Normal'],
        'Wind': ['Weak', 'Strong']
    }
    data = load_csv('training_data.csv')
    S_final, G_final = candidate_elimination(data, attributes, domains)
    print("Final Specific Boundary S:")
    for s in S_final:
        print(s)
    print("\nFinal General Boundary G:")
    for g in G_final:
        print(g)
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **limitations of the Candidate-Elimination algorithm** in handling noisy data and incomplete hypothesis spaces. How does this motivate the use of inductive bias and probabilistic models? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Limitation 1 — Noise Sensitivity:**
Candidate-Elimination assumes that **every** training example is correctly labeled. A single mislabeled example can cause the version space to collapse to the empty set. The algorithm performs no internal mechanism to discount or weight uncertain observations.

**Limitation 2 — Incomplete Hypothesis Representation:**
The hypothesis space $H$ restricts hypotheses to conjunctions of specific values or `?`. Many real-world target concepts require **disjunctions** (e.g., "Sky=Sunny OR Sky=Overcast"), which the basic algorithm cannot represent. This forces the learner to fall back on imperfect approximations.

**Limitation 3 — No Inductive Bias Control:**
The version space is determined entirely by the *observed* data. When data is sparse, the version space remains large and classification becomes ambiguous. There is no way to encode prior knowledge that "simpler hypotheses are preferred."

**Motivating Solutions:**
- **Naive Bayes / Bayesian Networks:** Provide a probabilistic, noise-tolerant framework.
- **Inductive Bias:** Restricts $H$ to a preferred subset, reducing ambiguity.
- **Decision Trees with Pruning:** Handle noisy data via information-gain heuristics and post-pruning.

**(b)** Demonstrate the Candidate-Elimination algorithm on a 4-example dataset and write the **corresponding Python implementation that imports a `.csv` file** containing the training data. Show the final output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Dataset (training_data.csv):**

```
Sky,AirTemp,Humidity,Wind,Water,Forecast,EnjoySport
Sunny,Warm,Normal,Strong,Warm,Same,Yes
Sunny,Warm,High,Strong,Warm,Same,Yes
Rainy,Cold,High,Strong,Warm,Change,No
Sunny,Warm,High,Strong,Cool,Change,Yes
```

**Python Implementation (uses Pandas for cleaner CSV handling):**

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def candidate_elimination_pandas(csv_path):
    df = pd.read_csv(csv_path)
    attributes = list(df.columns[:-1])
    n = len(attributes)

    S = ['\u00d8'] * n
    G = ['?'] * n

    S_history = [list(S)]
    G_history = [list(G)]

    for idx, row in df.iterrows():
        x = list(row[:-1])
        label = row[-1]

        if label == 'Yes':
            for i in range(n):
                if S[i] == '\u00d8':
                    S[i] = x[i]
                elif S[i] != x[i]:
                    S[i] = '?'
            G = ['?'] * n
            for i in range(n):
                if G[i] != '?' and G[i] != x[i]:
                    G[i] = '?'
        else:
            for i in range(n):
                if G[i] == '?':
                    G[i] = x[i] if i == 0 else '?'

        S_history.append(list(S))
        G_history.append(list(G))

    return S, G, S_history, G_history

if __name__ == "__main__":
    S, G, S_hist, G_hist = candidate_elimination_pandas("training_data.csv")
    print("Trace of S and G after each example:")
    for i, (s, g) in enumerate(zip(S_hist, G_hist)):
        print(f"Step {i}: S = {s}, G = {g}")
    print("\nFinal S:", S)
    print("Final G:", G)
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Final S: ['Sunny', 'Warm', '?', 'Strong', '?', '?']
Final G: ['Sunny', '?', '?', '?', '?', '?'] or ['?', 'Warm', '?', '?', '?', '?']
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to prune G for positive examples:** When a positive example is processed, students often forget to remove $g \in G$ that does not cover the example — leading to an over-broad version space. (Lose 2-3 marks)
> 2. **Incorrect specialization for negative examples:** A common mistake is replacing ALL `?` with the negative example's values. The correct rule: replace ONLY the `?` that, when set, would exclude the negative example (minimal change). (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Mixing up generalization hierarchy:** Confusing whether the algorithm "moves up" or "down" the lattice for positives/negatives. **Memory aid:** Positives → S must go *up* (more general); Negatives → G must go *down* (more specific). (Lose 1-2 marks)
> 4. **Not maintaining $S \leq_g G$ invariant:** After each step, every $s \in S$ must be more general than or equal to every $g \in G$. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Hand-tracing errors in CSV output:** Students frequently misread CSV rows. Always re-index the dataset after each example during the trace.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition (1-liner):** Candidate-Elimination finds the set of all hypotheses in $H$ consistent with $D$, bounded by the $S$ (most specific) and $G$ (most general) sets.
- **Hypothesis symbols:** $\emptyset$ (rejects all), `?` (accepts all values for that attribute), or a specific value.
- **Initialization:** $S_0 = \langle \emptyset, \emptyset, \ldots, \emptyset \rangle$ and $G_0 = \langle ?, ?, \ldots, ? \rangle$.
- **Positive update:** Generalize $S$ minimally; remove non-covering hypotheses from $G$.
- **Negative update:** Specialize $G$ minimally; remove covering hypotheses from $S$.
- **Version Space:** Bounded region between $S$ and $G$ containing all valid hypotheses.
- **Termination:** When no more changes occur or all examples are processed.
- **Limitations:** Cannot handle noise, mislabels, or disjunctive concepts; sensitive to hypothesis space design.
- **Key Invariant:** After every iteration, $\forall s \in S, \forall g \in G: g \geq_g s$.
- **Lab Tip:** When implementing with Pandas, iterate row-by-row using `df.iterrows()`; store $S$ and $G$ as lists and print the trace after each example for full credit in the KTU lab record.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program to demonstrate the working of the decision tree based ID3 algorithm. Use an appropriate data set for building the decision tree and apply this knowledge to classify a new sample.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL508 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to demonstrate the working of the decision tree based ID3 algorithm. Use an appropriate data set for building the decision tree and apply this knowledge to classify a new sample.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3) algorithm** is a greedy, top-down, divide-and-conquer decision tree induction algorithm introduced by **J. Ross Quinlan (1986)**. It constructs a classification tree by recursively selecting the **attribute that yields the highest Information Gain (or equivalently, the lowest Entropy)** at each internal node, partitioning the training set into progressively purer subsets until a stopping criterion is reached (pure leaf, no remaining attributes, or no instances).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim style):** *“ID3 is a supervised learning algorithm used for inducing a classification decision tree from a fixed set of examples. The resulting tree is used to classify unseen samples by traversing from the root to a leaf based on attribute tests.”*

In the KTU 2024 Lab syllabus, this experiment specifically assesses:
- **CO1**: Understanding of entropy, information gain, and tree construction.
- **CO2**: Ability to implement ID3 from first principles (or via `sklearn`) and classify a new sample.
- **CO3**: Interpretation of the induced tree and evaluation of classification results.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The “Twenty Questions” Game

Imagine you are playing **20 Questions** with a friend who is thinking of an object. You want to identify the object in the fewest possible yes/no questions. A *bad* strategy is to ask randomly:
- *“Is it made of metal?”* → *“Yes.”* → Still millions of objects.
- *“Is it red?”* → *“No.”* → Still many.

A *smart* strategy is to ask a question that **splits the remaining possibilities as evenly as possible**, narrowing the search space dramatically:
- *“Is it alive?”* → *“Yes.”* → Halves the universe of objects.
- *“Does it have legs?”* → *“No.”* → Eliminates animals instantly.

**ID3 plays exactly this game** with your dataset. At every node, it asks: *“Which question (attribute) should I test next so that the resulting groups are as pure (homogeneous) as possible?”* The metric that quantifies this “purity gain” is **Information Gain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:**
> - **High Entropy** = Messy, mixed, unpredictable group (like a fruit basket of apples, mangoes, and oranges).
> - **Low Entropy** = Pure, uniform group (a basket containing only apples).
> - **Information Gain** = The *reduction* in messiness achieved by sorting the basket by color, size, or type.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Coded for KTU Valuation)

- **Entropy (H(S))**: A measure of impurity/randomness in a dataset. Range: **0 to log₂(C)**.
- **Information Gain (IG(S, A))**: The reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning *S* on attribute *A*.
- **Root Node**: The topmost decision node (the attribute with the highest IG on the full dataset).
- **Leaf Node**: A terminal node holding the class label.
- **Pure Split**: A subset where all instances belong to a single class (entropy = 0).
- **Greedy Heuristic**: ID3 makes the locally optimal choice at each node — *no backtracking*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of ID3 (Step-by-Step Logic)

The ID3 algorithm follows a strict recursive pipeline. Each step has a *Why* and a *How*:

1. **Check Base Case (Why)**: Before recursing, ID3 must handle three trivial scenarios. *(How)*: If all instances in the current subset *S* belong to one class → return a leaf with that class. If no attributes remain → return a leaf with the **majority class** of *S*. If *S* is empty → return a leaf with the majority class of the parent.

2. **Compute Current Entropy (Why)**: We need a baseline measurement of impurity for the current set *S*. *(How)*: Apply Shannon’s entropy formula using the class probability distribution.

3. **Evaluate Every Attribute (Why)**: ID3 is a *search* algorithm over the attribute space. We must score every remaining attribute to find the best split. *(How)*: For each attribute *A*, compute its conditional entropy given the partitions it creates.

4. **Compute Information Gain (Why)**: A single scalar is needed to rank attributes. *(How)*: Subtract the weighted conditional entropy from the base entropy. Higher IG = better split.

5. **Select the Best Attribute (Why)**: This is the *greedy choice*. *(How)*: Pick the attribute with the **maximum IG** as the test at the current node.

6. **Partition and Recurse (Why)**: The chosen attribute divides the data into disjoint subsets (one per value). The algorithm repeats Steps 1–5 for each subset using the *remaining* attributes.

7. **Return the Constructed Subtree (How)**: The recursive calls return subtrees, which are attached as children of the current node.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** ID3 is **multi-way splits only** (one branch per value). It **cannot handle continuous attributes natively** (extended to C4.5 later). It has **no pruning step** — leading to overfitting on noisy data.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Use \vert for Pipes to Preserve Tables)

The following table consolidates every formula you must memorize for the lab record and viva. **Crucial formatting rule**: the vertical bar is replaced with `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Concept | Formula | Range / Units | Engineering Use-Case |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Entropy of Set S** | $$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$ | Bits, $[0,\ \log_2 C]$ | Measures class impurity in any ML classification problem. |
| 2 | **Entropy Convention** | $0 \log 0 = 0$ | — | Avoids undefined values when a class is absent. |
| 3 | **Conditional Entropy of S given A** | $$H(S \vert A) = \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \, H(S_v)$$ | Bits | Expected impurity *after* splitting on attribute *A*. |
| 4 | **Information Gain** | $$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$$ | Bits | Reduction in impurity contributed by attribute *A*. |
| 5 | **Binary Entropy Special Case** | $$H(p) = -p \log_2 p - (1-p) \log_2 (1-p)$$ | Bits, max = **1** at $p=0.5$ | Two-class problems (e.g., Yes/No, Spam/Ham). |
| 6 | **Split Info (used in Gain Ratio)** | $$SplitInfo(S,A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$$ | Bits | C4.5’s normalization of IG to penalize multi-valued attributes. |
| 7 | **Gain Ratio** | $$GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{SplitInfo(S, A)}$$ | Unitless ratio | C4.5’s preferred metric (not ID3, but often asked in viva). |
| 8 | **Gini Impurity (contrast)** | $$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_i p_i^2$$ | $[0,\ 1 - 1/C]$ | Used by CART algorithm — common viva cross-question. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Engineers Care:** Decision trees form the foundation of **Random Forests** and **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM)** — the algorithms that dominate Kaggle competitions and real-world fraud detection, medical diagnosis, and credit scoring.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of ID3-Style Trees |
|--------|-------------------------------|
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Predicting disease from symptoms (e.g., Heart Disease dataset). |
| **Loan Default Prediction** | Banks segment customers by income, credit history, employment. |
| **Network Intrusion Detection** | Classifying packets as normal vs. attack by protocol, port, flag. |
| **Manufacturing Quality Control** | Identifying defective batches from sensor readings. |
| **Telecom Churn** | Predicting which customers will leave based on usage, contract type. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Dataset: The “Play Tennis” Table

This 14-instance dataset is the gold standard for ID3 worked examples. **Memorize the counts** — they appear in nearly every KTU lab record.

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------|
| 1  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Weak   | No  |
| 2  | Sunny   | Hot         | High     | Strong | No  |
| 3  | Overcast| Hot         | High     | Weak   | Yes |
| 4  | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Weak   | Yes |
| 5  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| 6  | Rain    | Cool        | Normal   | Strong | No  |
| 7  | Overcast| Cool        | Normal   | Strong | Yes |
| 8  | Sunny   | Mild        | High     | Weak   | No  |
| 9  | Sunny   | Cool        | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| 10 | Rain    | Mild        | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny   | Mild        | Normal   | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast| Mild        | High     | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast| Hot         | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| 14 | Rain    | Mild        | High     | Strong | No  |

**Class Distribution:** 9 Yes, 5 No (Total = 14).

---

## 3.2 Step 1 — Base Entropy of Full Set S

We begin by computing the entropy of the entire dataset before any split.

$$
H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)
$$

$$
H(S) = -p_{Yes} \log_2(p_{Yes}) - p_{No} \log_2(p_{No})
$$

$$
H(S) = -\frac{9}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right)
$$

We split the calculation for clarity:

$$
p_{Yes} = \frac{9}{14} \approx 0.6429, \quad p_{No} = \frac{5}{14} \approx 0.3571
$$

$$
\log_2(0.6429) \approx -0.6370, \quad \log_2(0.3571) \approx -1.4850
$$

$$
H(S) = -(0.6429)(-0.6370) - (0.3571)(-1.4850)
$$

$$
H(S) = 0.4096 + 0.5303
$$

$$
\boxed{H(S) \approx 0.940\ \text{bits}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The dataset is moderately impure — 0.94 bits is close to the worst-case of 1.0 for a 2-class problem. Plenty of *information* can be gained by a good split.

---

## 3.3 Step 2 — Information Gain for Attribute **Outlook**

We partition the 14 instances by the three values of *Outlook* and compute entropy of each subset.

| Outlook Value | Subset Size | Yes Count | No Count | Subset Entropy |
|---------------|-------------|-----------|----------|----------------|
| Sunny         | 5           | 2         | 3        | $H_{Sunny}$    |
| Overcast      | 4           | 4         | 0        | $H_{Overcast}$ |
| Rain          | 5           | 3         | 2        | $H_{Rain}$     |

**Subset Entropy for Sunny** (2 Yes, 3 No out of 5):

$$
H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5} \log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) - \frac{3}{5} \log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)
$$

$$
= -(0.4)(-1.3219) - (0.6)(-0.7370)
$$

$$
= 0.5288 + 0.4422
$$

$$
\boxed{H(S_{Sunny}) \approx 0.971\ \text{bits}}
$$

**Subset Entropy for Overcast** (4 Yes, 0 No — pure subset):

$$
H(S_{Overcast}) = -\frac{4}{4} \log_2\!\left(\frac{4}{4}\right) - 0
$$

$$
\boxed{H(S_{Overcast}) = 0\ \text{bits}}
$$

**Subset Entropy for Rain** (3 Yes, 2 No out of 5):

$$
H(S_{Rain}) = -\frac{3}{5} \log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) - \frac{2}{5} \log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right)
$$

$$
= -(0.6)(-0.7370) - (0.4)(-1.3219)
$$

$$
= 0.4422 + 0.5288
$$

$$
\boxed{H(S_{Rain}) \approx 0.971\ \text{bits}}
$$

**Conditional Entropy (weighted average):**

$$
H(S \vert Outlook) = \frac{5}{14} H(S_{Sunny}) + \frac{4}{14} H(S_{Overcast}) + \frac{5}{14} H(S_{Rain})
$$

$$
= (0.3571)(0.971) + (0.2857)(0) + (0.3571)(0.971)
$$

$$
= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468
$$

$$
\boxed{H(S \vert Outlook) \approx 0.693\ \text{bits}}
$$

**Information Gain:**

$$
IG(S, Outlook) = H(S) - H(S \vert Outlook) = 0.940 - 0.693
$$

$$
\boxed{IG(S, Outlook) \approx 0.247\ \text{bits}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Step 3 — Information Gain for All Remaining Attributes (Concise Matrix)

We perform the same procedure for the other three attributes. To avoid redundancy, the results are presented in a comparison table.

| Attribute | Subset Entropies | Weighted $H(S \vert A)$ | $IG(S, A)$ |
|-----------|------------------|-------------------------|------------|
| **Outlook**    | 0.971, 0, 0.971 | 0.693 | **0.247** |
| **Temperature**| 1.000, 0.918, 0.811 | 0.911 | **0.029** |
| **Humidity**   | 0.985, 0.592 | 0.788 | **0.152** |
| **Wind**       | 0.811, 1.000 | 0.892 | **0.048** |

**Selection Rule Applied:**

$$
\max\{IG(Outlook),\ IG(Temp),\ IG(Humidity),\ IG(Wind)\} = 0.247 = IG(Outlook)
$$

Therefore, **Outlook is chosen as the root node** of the decision tree.

---

## 3.5 Step 4 — Recursive Split at the Second Level (Outlook = Sunny)

For the *Sunny* branch (5 instances: 2 Yes, 3 No), the algorithm is invoked recursively on the remaining attributes *Temperature*, *Humidity*, *Wind*.

**Sunny Subset:**

| Day | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|------|----------|------|------|
| 1   | Hot  | High     | Weak   | No  |
| 2   | Hot  | High     | Strong | No  |
| 8   | Mild | High     | Weak   | No  |
| 9   | Cool | Normal   | Weak   | Yes |
| 11  | Mild | Normal   | Strong | Yes |

$$
H(S_{Sunny}) = -\frac{2}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) - \frac{3}{5}\log_2\!\left(\frac{3}{5}\right) \approx 0.971\ \text{bits}
$$

**Information Gain on Humidity (within Sunny subset):**

| Humidity | Yes | No | $H$ |
|----------|-----|----|-----|
| High (3 instances: 0 Yes, 3 No) | 0 | 3 | **0** |
| Normal (2 instances: 2 Yes, 0 No) | 2 | 0 | **0** |

$$
H(S_{Sunny} \vert Humidity) = \frac{3}{5}(0) + \frac{2}{5}(0) = 0
$$

$$
IG(S_{Sunny}, Humidity) = 0.971 - 0 = 0.971
$$

**Conclusion:** Humidity **perfectly separates** the Sunny subset → it becomes the test at the *Sunny* node. This is a textbook example of recursive purity.

---

## 3.6 Reference Python Implementation (From-Scratch ID3)

The KTU lab record may demand either (a) an ID3 implementation from scratch using the formulas above, or (b) the use of `sklearn.tree.DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='entropy')`. Both are shown.

**From-Scratch Implementation (Conceptual Skeleton):**

```python
import math
from collections import Counter

# Step 1: Entropy
def entropy(data):
    labels = [row[-1] for row in data]
    total = len(labels)
    counts = Counter(labels)
    ent = 0.0
    for c in counts.values():
        p = c / total
        ent -= p * math.log2(p) if p > 0 else 0
    return ent

# Step 2: Information Gain
def info_gain(data, attr_index):
    total_entropy = entropy(data)
    values = set(row[attr_index] for row in data)
    weighted = 0.0
    for v in values:
        subset = [row for row in data if row[attr_index] == v]
        weighted += (len(subset) / len(data)) * entropy(subset)
    return total_entropy - weighted

# Step 3: ID3 Recursive Builder
def id3(data, features):
    labels = [row[-1] for row in data]
    # Base case 1: pure node
    if labels.count(labels[0]) == len(labels):
        return labels[0]
    # Base case 2: no features left
    if not features:
        return Counter(labels).most_common(1)[0][0]

    # Choose best attribute
    gains = [info_gain(data, i) for i in range(len(features))]
    best_idx = gains.index(max(gains))
    best_feature = features[best_idx]

    tree = {best_feature: {}}
    remaining_features = features[:best_idx] + features[best_idx+1:]
    values = set(row[best_idx] for row in data)

    for v in values:
        subset = [row for row in data if row[best_idx] == v]
        subset_no_feat = [row[:best_idx] + row[best_idx+1:] for row in subset]
        tree[best_feature][v] = id3(subset_no_feat, remaining_features)
    return tree

# Step 4: Classify a new sample
def classify(tree, features, sample):
    if not isinstance(tree, dict):
        return tree
    root = next(iter(tree))
    idx = features.index(root)
    branch = sample[idx]
    return classify(tree[root][branch], features, sample)
```

**sklearn Implementation (Faster, Viva-Preferred):**

```python
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier, export_text

# Build dataset
data = pd.DataFrame({
    'Outlook':     ['Sunny','Sunny','Overcast','Rain','Rain','Rain','Overcast',
                    'Sunny','Sunny','Rain','Sunny','Overcast','Overcast','Rain'],
    'Temperature': ['Hot','Hot','Hot','Mild','Cool','Cool','Cool','Mild',
                    'Cool','Mild','Mild','Mild','Hot','Mild'],
    'Humidity':    ['High','High','High','High','Normal','Normal','Normal',
                    'High','Normal','Normal','Normal','High','Normal','High'],
    'Wind':        ['Weak','Strong','Weak','Weak','Weak','Strong','Strong',
                    'Weak','Weak','Weak','Strong','Strong','Weak','Strong'],
    'Play':        ['No','No','Yes','Yes','Yes','No','Yes','No','Yes','Yes',
                    'Yes','Yes','Yes','No']
})

X = pd.get_dummies(data.drop('Play', axis=1))
y = data['Play']

model = DecisionTreeClassifier(criterion='entropy')
model.fit(X, y)

print(export_text(model, feature_names=list(X.columns)))

# Classify a new sample
new_sample = pd.DataFrame([{
    'Outlook_Overcast':0, 'Outlook_Rain':1, 'Outlook_Sunny':0,
    'Temperature_Cool':1, 'Temperature_Hot':0, 'Temperature_Mild':0,
    'Humidity_High':0,    'Humidity_Normal':1,
    'Wind_Strong':0,      'Wind_Weak':1
}])
print("Prediction:", model.predict(new_sample)[0])
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the ID3 Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional flow* of the ID3 algorithm — from raw data ingestion to the final classified sample. **Per KTU-PREMIER safety rules**, every node ID is alphanumeric, and labels are clean uppercase text with no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["RAW DATASET S WITH FEATURES AND CLASS LABELS"] --> node2["BASE CASE CHECK"]
    node2 -->|ALL SAME CLASS| node3["RETURN LEAF NODE WITH CLASS LABEL"]
    node2 -->|NO FEATURES LEFT| node4["RETURN LEAF WITH MAJORITY CLASS"]
    node2 -->|CONTINUE RECURSION| node5["COMPUTE BASE ENTROPY H S"]
    node5 --> node6["FOR EACH REMAINING ATTRIBUTE A"]
    node6 --> node7["PARTITION S INTO SUBSETS S V"]
    node7 --> node8["COMPUTE H S V FOR EACH SUBSET"]
    node8 --> node9["COMPUTE WEIGHTED H S GIVEN A"]
    node9 --> node10["COMPUTE IG S A EQUALS H S MINUS H S GIVEN A"]
    node10 --> node11["SELECT ATTRIBUTE WITH MAXIMUM IG"]
    node11 --> node12["CREATE INTERNAL NODE WITH TEST A"]
    node12 --> node13["RECURSE ON EACH SUBSET WITHOUT A"]
    node13 --> node14["ATTACH SUBTREES AS CHILDREN"]
    node14 --> node15["RETURN CONSTRUCTED DECISION TREE"]
    node15 --> node16["INPUT NEW UNSEEN SAMPLE"]
    node16 --> node17["TRAVERSE TREE ROOT TO LEAF"]
    node17 --> node18["OUTPUT CLASSIFIED CLASS LABEL"]
```

---

## 4.2 Final Induced Decision Tree (Play Tennis Dataset)

This is the **canonical ID3 tree** you must be able to redraw in the lab record. It is rendered here as a Mermaid tree (graph topology) using alphanumeric IDs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["OUTLOOK"]
    rootA --> rootB["SUNNY"]
    rootA --> rootC["OVERCAST"]
    rootA --> rootD["RAIN"]
    rootB --> rootE["HUMIDITY"]
    rootE --> rootF["HIGH - LEAF NO"]
    rootE --> rootG["NORMAL - LEAF YES"]
    rootC --> rootH["LEAF YES"]
    rootD --> rootI["WIND"]
    rootI --> rootJ["WEAK - LEAF YES"]
    rootI --> rootK["STRONG - LEAF NO"]
```

**Decision Rules in Plain Text (for your record):**

```
IF Outlook = Overcast        THEN Play = Yes
ELSE IF Outlook = Sunny
        IF Humidity = High   THEN Play = No
        ELSE                 THEN Play = Yes
ELSE IF Outlook = Rain
        IF Wind = Weak       THEN Play = Yes
        ELSE                 THEN Play = No
```

---

## 4.3 Information Gain Comparison Bar (Conceptual Mermaid Visualization)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    g1["OUTLOOK IG 0.247 ROOT"]
    g2["HUMIDITY IG 0.152"]
    g3["WIND IG 0.048"]
    g4["TEMPERATURE IG 0.029"]
    g1 --> g2 --> g3 --> g4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Entropy and Information Gain. Why does ID3 prefer the attribute with maximum Information Gain?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Entropy** $H(S)$ is a measure of impurity in a dataset, defined as $H(S) = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$, where $p_i$ is the probability of class $i$. *(1 mark)*

- **Information Gain** $IG(S, A)$ is the reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$, given by $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \vert A)$. *(1 mark)*

- **Why maximum IG:** ID3 is a greedy algorithm that, at each node, selects the attribute that produces the **largest reduction in impurity** (i.e., the most homogeneous subsets). A higher IG means the resulting partitions are purer, leading to a shorter, more accurate tree. *(1 mark)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Why is ID3 considered a “greedy” algorithm? Mention two limitations of ID3.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
