### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

(a) **Discuss the role of Laplace smoothing in Naïve Bayes. Why is it necessary? Illustrate with an example.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of Laplace Smoothing:**
In real datasets, certain feature-class combinations may have **zero training occurrences**. Under the naïve independence assumption, a single zero in any $P(X_i \mid C)$ makes the **entire product zero**, causing the classifier to assign zero probability to that class for that test instance — even if other features strongly indicate that class. This is the **zero-frequency problem**. **(2 marks)**

**Mathematical Form:**
For a categorical feature with $k$ distinct values:

$$P(X_i = x \mid C = c) = \frac{\text{count}(X_i = x \text{ and } C = c) + 1}{\text{count}(C = c) + k}$$
**(2 marks)**

The "+1" in numerator and "+k" in denominator ensure no probability becomes zero while preserving the total probability sum = 1.

**Example:** From the weather dataset, $P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = 0/5 = 0$ (no "No" instance had Overcast outlook). Without smoothing, a test instance with "Overcast" would make $P(\text{No} \mid \mathbf{X}) = 0$ regardless of other features. With Laplace smoothing (k=3 outlook values):

$$P(\text{Overcast} \mid \text{No}) = \frac{0 + 1}{5 + 3} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.125$$
**(2 marks)**

**When to use:** Always recommended for categorical Naïve Bayes; not typically needed for Gaussian NB. **(1 mark)**

(b) **Explain the terms True Positive, False Positive, True Negative, and False Negative with reference to a confusion matrix. Using a 2×2 example confusion matrix, compute accuracy, precision, and recall.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions (from a binary classification viewpoint):**
- **True Positive (TP)**: Model predicted Positive, actual label is Positive. **Correctly detected positives.** **(0.5 mark)**
- **False Positive (FP)**: Model predicted Positive, actual label is Negative. **Type-I error (false alarm).** **(0.5 mark)**
- **True Negative (TN)**: Model predicted Negative, actual label is Negative. **Correctly detected negatives.** **(0.5 mark)**
- **False Negative (FN)**: Model predicted Negative, actual label is Positive. **Type-II error (missed detection).** **(0.5 mark)**

**Example Confusion Matrix:**

|  | Predicted: Yes | Predicted: No |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Yes** | TP = 50 | FN = 10 |
| **Actual: No**  | FP = 5  | TN = 35 |

**Total = 100 samples** **(1 mark)**

**Accuracy:**

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{50 + 35}{100} = \frac{85}{100} = 0.85 \;\; (85\%)$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Precision:**

$$\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{50}{50 + 5} = \frac{50}{55} \approx 0.909 \;\; (90.91\%)$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Recall:**

$$\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{50}{50 + 10} = \frac{50}{60} \approx 0.833 \;\; (83.33\%)$$
**(1 mark)**

> **Interpretation:** Precision of 90.91% means that when the model predicts "Yes", it is correct 91% of the time. Recall of 83.33% means it successfully identifies 83% of all actual "Yes" cases. The accuracy of 85% is the overall correctness across all classes. **(Remaining 0.5 mark for interpretation)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist as your final-day revision sheet before the lab exam/viva.**

- [x] **Bayes' Theorem** is the backbone: $P(C \mid X) = P(C) \cdot P(X \mid C) / P(X)$.
- [x] The **"naïve" assumption** = conditional independence of all features given the class.
- [x] The classifier uses the **MAP (Maximum A Posteriori)** rule to pick the most likely class.
- [x] **Priors** are estimated as class frequencies: $P(C) = \text{count}(C)/N$.
- [x] **Likelihoods** are computed per-feature: either frequency ratio (categorical) or Gaussian PDF (continuous).
- [x] **Laplace Smoothing** formula: $\frac{\text{count} + 1}{\text{class count} + k}$ — prevents zero-frequency collapse.
- [x] **Three NB variants** in scikit-learn: `GaussianNB`, `MultinomialNB`, `BernoulliNB`.
- [x] **Always encode** categorical features using `LabelEncoder` before training `GaussianNB`.
- [x] **Accuracy** = (TP + TN) / Total — best for balanced datasets.
- [x] **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP) — important when false positives are costly (e.g., spam).
- [x] **Recall** = TP / (TP + FN) — important when false negatives are costly (e.g., disease detection).
- [x] Use `train_test_split` with `random_state` for **reproducibility**.
- [x] Display metrics with `:.2f` format for **2-decimal-place precision** (KTU lab convention).
- [x] **Confusion Matrix** layout: rows = actual, columns = predicted.
- [x] **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of precision and recall: $F_1 = \frac{2PR}{P+R}$.
- [x] Naïve Bayes is **fast, low-memory, handles multi-class natively, and works well with small data**.
- [x] In scikit-learn, use `classification_report` for a one-line summary of all metrics.
- [x] For **viva**: be ready to explain (a) why it's called "naïve", (b) what happens with zero probabilities, and (c) when to use Gaussian vs Multinomial NB.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program to construct a Bayesian network considering medical data. Use this model to demonstrate the diagnosis of heart patients using standard Heart Disease Data Set.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL508 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to construct a Bayesian network considering medical data. Use this model to demonstrate the diagnosis of heart patients using standard Heart Disease Data Set.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Bayesian Network (BN)**, also known as a **Belief Network** or **Probabilistic Graphical Model (PGM)**, is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) in which each node represents a random variable, and each directed edge represents a conditional probabilistic dependency. Every node is associated with a **Conditional Probability Distribution (CPD)** — most commonly a **Conditional Probability Table (CPT)** — that quantifies the effect of the parent nodes on that variable.

For medical diagnosis, a Bayesian Network encodes the joint probability distribution over a set of disease variables $D$ and symptom/observation variables $S$ as:

$$
P(D, S) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))
$$

where $X_i \in \{D \cup S\}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A Bayesian Network for medical diagnosis is a *Naive Bayes generalization* — it relaxes the conditional independence assumption by allowing arbitrary directed dependencies between variables, while still factorizing the joint distribution efficiently.

### 1.2 The Heart Disease Data Set (UCI Cleveland Benchmark)

The standard **Heart Disease Data Set** (Cleveland Clinic Foundation subset) hosted at the UCI Machine Learning Repository contains **303 patient records** with **14 attributes**. This dataset is the de-facto benchmark for cardiac risk modeling in academic and KTU laboratory settings.

| Attribute | Description | Type | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| `age` | Patient age in years | Numeric | 29 – 77 |
| `sex` | Biological sex | Categorical | 1 = Male, 0 = Female |
| `cp` | Chest pain type | Ordinal | 0–3 |
| `trestbps` | Resting blood pressure (mm Hg) | Numeric | 94 – 200 |
| `chol` | Serum cholesterol (mg/dl) | Numeric | 126 – 564 |
| `fbs` | Fasting blood sugar > 120 mg/dl | Binary | 0 / 1 |
| `restecg` | Resting ECG result | Ordinal | 0–2 |
| `thalach` | Maximum heart rate achieved | Numeric | 71 – 202 |
| `exang` | Exercise-induced angina | Binary | 0 / 1 |
| `oldpeak` | ST depression vs rest | Numeric | 0 – 6.2 |
| `slope` | Slope of peak exercise ST | Ordinal | 0–2 |
| `ca` | Number of major vessels (fluoroscopy) | Numeric | 0–3 |
| `thal` | Thalassemia type | Ordinal | 0–3 |
| `target` | Diagnosis of heart disease | Binary | 0 = No, 1 = Yes |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Detective's Mind"

Imagine a **seasoned cardiologist** with 30 years of experience. When a patient walks in, the doctor doesn't think in rigid rules like *if-then*. Instead, they mentally juggle dozens of *likelihoods*:

> *"The patient is 58, male, with chest pain... Hmm, that nudges my belief toward heart disease upward. But his cholesterol is normal and ECG is clean — that pulls it down. Combined with exercise-induced angina, my final belief is around 75% chance of heart disease."*

This **probabilistic reasoning under uncertainty** is exactly what a Bayesian Network formalizes mathematically. Each symptom is a node; each causal link is an arrow; each node carries a small probability table — together forming a **computational replica of expert reasoning**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Summary:** A Bayesian Network = (Directed Graph Structure) + (Conditional Probability Tables). The graph encodes *qualitative* causal knowledge; the CPTs encode *quantitative* strength of influence. Inference = *updating beliefs when new evidence arrives*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

#### 2.1.1 Bayes' Theorem (Foundational Pillar)

The entire Bayesian Network architecture rests upon **Thomas Bayes' theorem of conditional probability**:

$$
P(H \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid H) \cdot P(H)}{P(E)}
$$

Where:
- $P(H \mid E)$ — **Posterior probability** (belief in hypothesis $H$ after observing evidence $E$)
- $P(E \mid H)$ — **Likelihood** (probability of evidence given hypothesis is true)
- $P(H)$ — **Prior probability** (initial belief before evidence)
- $P(E)$ — **Marginal likelihood / Evidence** (normalizing constant)

#### 2.1.2 Joint Probability Factorization

For a Bayesian Network with $n$ variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$, the **chain rule of probability** combined with the network's conditional independence assumptions yields the **factorized joint distribution**:

$$
P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))
$$

**Why this matters:** A naïve joint distribution over 14 binary medical variables would require $2^{14} - 1 = 16383$ independent parameters. With the Bayesian Network structure exploiting conditional independencies, the parameter count drops dramatically — typically to a few hundred entries across CPTs.

#### 2.1.3 Markov Condition & d-Separation

A node in a Bayesian Network is **conditionally independent** of its non-descendants given its parents. This is the **local Markov property**:

$$
X_i \perp\!\!\!\perp \text{NonDescendants}(X_i) \mid \text{Parents}(X_i)
$$

For *inference-time* independence queries, the **d-separation criterion** is used: two sets of nodes $A$ and $B$ are conditionally independent given evidence set $C$ if and only if every path between $A$ and $B$ is "blocked" by $C$.

#### 2.1.4 Inference Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable Elimination (VE)** | Exact | Exponential in treewidth | Small-to-medium networks |
| **Belief Propagation (BP)** | Exact (on trees/polytree) | Linear on trees | Tree-structured BNs |
| **Junction Tree** | Exact | Exponential in clique size | General BNs |
| **Gibbs Sampling** | Approximate (MCMC) | Iterative | Large-scale inference |
| **Variational Inference** | Approximate | Optimization-based | Continuous BNs |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Lab Focus:** The `pgmpy` library's `VariableElimination` class is the most common exact inference engine used in KTU laboratory examinations.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}$ | Foundation of all BN reasoning |
| 2 | Joint Factorization | $P(X) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$ | Compact storage of joint distribution |
| 3 | Chain Rule | $P(A, B, C) = P(A) P(B \mid A) P(C \mid A, B)$ | Building factorizations |
| 4 | Total Probability | $P(E) = \sum_{i} P(E \mid H_i) P(H_i)$ | Normalizing constant computation |
| 5 | MLE Parameter Estimate | $\hat{P}(X_i = x \mid \text{Parents}) = \dfrac{N(x, \text{Parents})}{N(\text{Parents})}$ | Learning CPTs from data |
| 6 | Bayesian Parameter Estimate (Laplace) | $\hat{P} = \dfrac{N + \alpha}{N + \alpha k}$ | Smoothing zero-frequency cells |
| 7 | Log-Likelihood | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{j} \log P(x_j \mid \theta)$ | Model selection metric |
| 8 | Entropy | $H(X) = -\sum_{x} P(x) \log P(x)$ | Information-theoretic measure |
| 9 | Mutual Information | $I(X; Y) = \sum_{x,y} P(x,y) \log \dfrac{P(x,y)}{P(x)P(y)}$ | Structure learning scoring |
| 10 | K2 Score (for BDeu) | $\text{Score} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \prod_{j=1}^{q_i} \dfrac{\Gamma(\alpha_{ij})}{\Gamma(\alpha_{ij} + m_{ij})} \prod_{k=1}^{r_i} \dfrac{\Gamma(\alpha_{ijk} + m_{ijk})}{\Gamma(\alpha_{ijk})}$ | Bayesian Dirichlet equivalence scoring |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Bayesian Networks are deployed in **medical diagnosis systems** (Quick Medical Reference QMR-DT), **fault diagnosis in industrial machinery**, **spam filtering**, **genetic regulatory network inference**, and **autonomous vehicle risk assessment**.

### 2.3 Why a Bayesian Network for Heart Disease Diagnosis?

1. **Handles Uncertainty Quantitatively:** Real medical data is noisy, incomplete, and probabilistic.
2. **Incorporates Prior Knowledge:** Domain experts can encode known causal relations (e.g., *age → heart disease*).
3. **Reasoning Under Missing Data:** Inference works even if some patient attributes are unknown.
4. **Explainability:** Unlike a "black-box" deep network, BNs provide clear causal pathways for every prediction — critical for medical regulatory approval.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Software Stack Required

| Library | Version | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `Python` | 3.8+ | Core language |
| `pandas` | 1.3+ | Data manipulation |
| `numpy` | 1.21+ | Numerical computation |
| `pgmpy` | 0.1.18+ | Bayesian Network modeling & inference |
| `scikit-learn` | 1.0+ | Train-test split, accuracy metrics |
| `matplotlib` | 3.5+ | Visualization of results |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Installation Command:** `pip install pgmpy pandas numpy scikit-learn matplotlib networkx`

### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Implementation

#### **STEP 1 — Import Required Libraries**

```python
# ===== IMPORTS =====
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
from pgmpy.models import BayesianNetwork
from pgmpy.estimators import MaximumLikelihoodEstimator, BayesianEstimator
from pgmpy.inference import VariableElimination
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report, confusion_matrix
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
```

#### **STEP 2 — Load and Preprocess the Heart Disease Dataset**

The dataset is typically downloaded from UCI ML Repository as `heart.csv` or `processed.cleveland.data`.

```python
# ===== DATA LOADING =====
# Option A: Load from CSV file
columns = ['age', 'sex', 'cp', 'trestbps', 'chol', 'fbs', 'restecg',
           'thalach', 'exang', 'oldpeak', 'slope', 'ca', 'thal', 'target']

df = pd.read_csv('heart.csv', names=columns, na_values='?')
print("Dataset shape:", df.shape)
print("Missing values:\n", df.isnull().sum())
print("First 5 rows:\n", df.head())
```

#### **STEP 3 — Discretize Continuous Variables**

**Why discretize?** Bayesian Networks in `pgmpy` operate on **discrete categorical variables** by default (CPTs are finite tables). Continuous variables like `age`, `chol`, `trestbps`, `thalach`, `oldpeak` must be binned into meaningful clinical categories.

```python
# ===== DISCRETIZATION FUNCTIONS =====
def discretize_age(age):
    if age < 40:    return 'Young'      # <40 years
    elif age < 55:  return 'Middle'     # 40-54 years
    elif age < 65:  return 'Senior'     # 55-64 years
    else:           return 'Elderly'    # >=65 years

def discretize_trestbps(bps):
    if bps < 120:    return 'Normal'      # <120 mmHg
    elif bps < 140:  return 'Elevated'    # 120-139 (Pre-hypertension)
    elif bps < 160:  return 'High'        # 140-159 (Stage 1 HTN)
    else:            return 'Very_High'   # >=160 (Stage 2 HTN)

def discretize_chol(chol):
    if chol < 200:    return 'Normal'      # <200 mg/dl (Desirable)
    elif chol < 240:  return 'Borderline'  # 200-239 (Borderline high)
    else:             return 'High'        # >=240 (High)

def discretize_thalach(hr):
    if hr < 120:    return 'Low'      # <120 bpm
    elif hr < 150:  return 'Normal'  # 120-149 bpm
    else:           return 'High'    # >=150 bpm

def discretize_oldpeak(op):
    if op < 1:    return 'Low'       # <1.0 mm
    elif op < 2:  return 'Medium'   # 1.0-1.9 mm
    else:         return 'High'     # >=2.0 mm

def discretize_ca(ca):
    if ca == 0: return 'Zero'    # No major vessels
    elif ca == 1: return 'One'   # 1 major vessel
    else:        return 'Many'   # 2 or more

# Apply discretizations
df['age_cat']      = df['age'].apply(discretize_age)
df['trestbps_cat'] = df['trestbps'].apply(discretize_trestbps)
df['chol_cat']     = df['chol'].apply(discretize_chol)
df['thalach_cat']  = df['thalach'].apply(discretize_thalach)
df['oldpeak_cat']  = df['oldpeak'].apply(discretize_oldpeak)
df['ca_cat']       = df['ca'].apply(discretize_ca)

# Binary target variable
df['heart_disease'] = df['target'].apply(lambda x: 'Yes' if x > 0 else 'No')
```

**Clinical Justification of Binning:** The age bins follow AHA cardiovascular risk stratification; the cholesterol bins follow NCEP ATP III guidelines; blood pressure bins follow JNC 7 classification. These thresholds are **standard medical benchmarks**, ensuring the discretization is not arbitrary but physiologically meaningful.

#### **STEP 4 — Construct the Bayesian Network Structure**

The DAG is constructed using domain knowledge — the relationships are based on established cardiac risk factors.

```python
# ===== BAYESIAN NETWORK STRUCTURE =====
model = BayesianNetwork([
    # Demographics -> Heart Disease
    ('age_cat',      'heart_disease'),
    ('sex',          'heart_disease'),

    # Clinical symptoms -> Heart Disease
    ('cp',           'heart_disease'),       # Chest pain type
    ('exang',        'heart_disease'),       # Exercise-induced angina

    # Diagnostic test results -> Heart Disease
    ('trestbps_cat', 'heart_disease'),       # Resting BP
    ('chol_cat',     'heart_disease'),       # Cholesterol
    ('fbs',          'heart_disease'),       # Fasting blood sugar
    ('restecg',      'heart_disease'),       # Resting ECG
    ('thalach_cat',  'heart_disease'),       # Max heart rate
    ('oldpeak_cat',  'heart_disease'),       # ST depression
    ('slope',        'heart_disease'),       # ST slope
    ('ca_cat',       'heart_disease'),       # Major vessels
    ('thal',         'heart_disease')        # Thalassemia type
])

print("Bayesian Network Structure (Edges):")
for edge in model.edges():
    print(f"  {edge[0]}  ->  {edge[1]}")
print(f"\nTotal edges: {len(model.edges())}")
print(f"Number of nodes: {len(model.nodes())}")
```

#### **STEP 5 — Prepare Final DataFrame and Split**

```python
# ===== FINAL DATASET =====
data_cols = ['age_cat', 'sex', 'cp', 'trestbps_cat', 'chol_cat', 'fbs',
             'restecg', 'thalach_cat', 'exang', 'oldpeak_cat', 'slope',
             'ca_cat', 'thal', 'heart_disease']

data = df[data_cols].dropna().copy()

# Convert all columns to string (pgmpy requirement for discrete variables)
for col in data.columns:
    data[col] = data[col].astype(str)

# Train-test split (80/20)
train_data, test_data = train_test_split(data, test_size=0.20, random_state=42, stratify=data['heart_disease'])

print(f"Training set size: {train_data.shape[0]}")
print(f"Testing set size:  {test_data.shape[0]}")
```

#### **STEP 6 — Parameter Learning (Fitting the CPTs)**

There are two principal estimators:

**(a) Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE):**

```python
# ===== MLE ESTIMATION =====
model.fit(data=train_data, estimator=MaximumLikelihoodEstimator)

# Display learned CPT for a sample node
cpd_heart = model.get_cpds('heart_disease')
print("Conditional Probability Table for 'heart_disease':")
print(cpd_heart)
```

**(b) Bayesian Estimation (with Laplace smoothing to avoid zero-probability cells):**

```python
# ===== BAYESIAN ESTIMATION (with prior pseudo-counts) =====
model_bayes = BayesianNetwork(model.edges())
model_bayes.fit(data=train_data,
                estimator=BayesianEstimator,
                prior_type='BDeu',
                equivalent_sample_size=10)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **MLE Formula (as shown in Section 2 Formula Sheet, Row 5):**
> $$\hat{P}(X_i = x \mid \text{Parents}) = \dfrac{\text{Count}(x, \text{Parents})}{\text{Count}(\text{Parents})}$$
> Each CPT entry is the *relative frequency* of the variable's value given its parent configuration in the training data.

#### **STEP 7 — Inspect All Learned CPTs**

```python
# ===== INSPECT ALL CPDs =====
print("=" * 60)
print("LEARNED CONDITIONAL PROBABILITY TABLES")
print("=" * 60)
for cpd in model.get_cpds():
    print(f"\nNode: {cpd.variable}")
    print(f"Cardinality: {cpd.cardinality}")
    print(cpd)
    print("-" * 60)
```

#### **STEP 8 — Initialize the Inference Engine**

```python
# ===== INFERENCE ENGINE =====
inference = VariableElimination(model)
```

#### **STEP 9 — Demonstration Queries (Diagnosis)**

**Query 1 — Single-symptom inference:**

```python
# What is the probability of heart disease given chest pain type = 2?
q1 = inference.query(variables=['heart_disease'],
                     evidence={'cp': '2'})
print("Query 1: P(Heart Disease | cp=2)")
print(q1)
print(f"\nP(Heart Disease = Yes | cp=2) = {q1.values[1]:.4f}")
```

**Query 2 — Multi-symptom clinical scenario:**

```python
# A 60-year-old male with chest pain type 3, exercise-induced angina,
# high cholesterol, and ST depression
q2 = inference.query(
    variables=['heart_disease'],
    evidence={
        'age_cat':     'Senior',
        'sex':         '1',
        'cp':          '3',
        'exang':       '1',
        'chol_cat':    'High',
        'oldpeak_cat': 'High'
    }
)
print("Query 2: P(Heart Disease | multi-symptom evidence)")
print(q2)
```

**Query 3 — Updating beliefs dynamically:**

```python
# Observe additional test result -> Update belief
q3 = inference.query(
    variables=['heart_disease'],
    evidence={
        'age_cat':     'Senior',
        'sex':         '1',
        'cp':          '3',
        'exang':       '1',
        'chol_cat':    'High',
        'oldpeak_cat': 'High',
        'thalach_cat': 'Low',          # NEW EVIDENCE
        'ca_cat':      'Many'          # NEW EVIDENCE
    }
)
print("Query 3: P(Heart Disease | full evidence set)")
print(q3)
```

#### **STEP 10 — Batch Diagnosis Function**

```python
# ===== DIAGNOSTIC FUNCTION FOR NEW PATIENTS =====
def diagnose_patient(evidence_dict, threshold=0.5):
    """
    Diagnose a patient given a dictionary of clinical evidence.
    Returns: (diagnosis, confidence, probability_of_disease)
    """
    result = inference.query(variables=['heart_disease'], evidence=evidence_dict)

    # Result values order: ['No', 'Yes']
    prob_no  = result.values[0]
    prob_yes = result.values[1]

    diagnosis   = "Heart Disease Detected" if prob_yes >= threshold else "No Heart Disease"
    confidence  = max(prob_yes, prob_no) * 100

    return diagnosis, confidence, prob_yes

# ---- Test Patient Cases ----

# Patient A: Low-risk profile
patient_A = {
    'age_cat': 'Young', 'sex': '0', 'cp': '0', 'exang': '0',
    'chol_cat': 'Normal', 'oldpeak_cat': 'Low', 'thalach_cat': 'High',
    'trestbps_cat': 'Normal', 'fbs': '0', 'restecg': '0',
    'slope': '2', 'ca_cat': 'Zero', 'thal': '3'
}
diag_A, conf_A, prob_A = diagnose_patient(patient_A)
print(f"\nPatient A: {diag_A}  |  Confidence: {conf_A:.2f}%  |  P(HD) = {prob_A:.4f}")

# Patient B: High-risk profile
patient_B = {
    'age_cat': 'Elderly', 'sex': '1', 'cp': '3', 'exang': '1',
    'chol_cat': 'High', 'oldpeak_cat': 'High', 'thalach_cat': 'Low',
    'trestbps_cat': 'Very_High', 'fbs': '1', 'restecg': '1',
    'slope': '0', 'ca_cat': 'Many', 'thal': '1'
}
diag_B, conf_B, prob_B = diagnose_patient(patient_B)
print(f"Patient B: {diag_B}  |  Confidence: {conf_B:.2f}%  |  P(HD) = {prob_B:.4f}")
```

#### **STEP 11 — Model Evaluation on Test Set**

```python
# ===== TEST-SET EVALUATION =====
y_true = []
y_pred = []

for idx, row in test_data.iterrows():
    evidence = {col: str(row[col]) for col in test_data.columns if col != 'heart_disease'}
    try:
        result = inference.query(variables=['heart_disease'], evidence=evidence)
        predicted = 'Yes' if result.values[1] >= 0.5 else 'No'
    except Exception:
        predicted = 'No'  # default for un-instantiable cases

    y_true.append(row['heart_disease'])
    y_pred.append(predicted)

# Performance metrics
accuracy = accuracy_score(y_true, y_pred)
print(f"\nModel Accuracy on Test Set: {accuracy * 100:.2f}%")
print("\nClassification Report:")
print(classification_report(y_true, y_pred, target_names=['No Heart Disease', 'Heart Disease']))

# Confusion Matrix
cm = confusion_matrix(y_true, y_pred, labels=['No', 'Yes'])
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 5))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues',
            xticklabels=['Predicted: No', 'Predicted: Yes'],
            yticklabels=['Actual: No', 'Actual: Yes'])
plt.title('Bayesian Network — Heart Disease Diagnosis')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('confusion_matrix.png', dpi=120)
plt.show()
```

#### **STEP 12 — Visualize the Network Graph**

```python
# ===== NETWORK GRAPH VISUALIZATION =====
import networkx as nx

G = nx.DiGraph()
G.add_edges_from(model.edges())

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
pos = nx.spring_layout(G, k=1.2, seed=42)
nx.draw(G, pos, with_labels=True, node_size=3000,
        node_color='lightblue', font_size=10,
        font_weight='bold', edge_color='darkred',
        arrows=True, arrowsize=20)
plt.title('Bayesian Network Structure — Heart Disease Diagnosis', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('bn_structure.png', dpi=120)
plt.show()
```

### 3.3 Expected Output Analysis (Sample)

```
Patient A: No Heart Disease   |  Confidence: 87.34%  |  P(HD) = 0.1266
Patient B: Heart Disease Detected |  Confidence: 94.21%  |  P(HD) = 0.9421

Model Accuracy on Test Set: 81.97%

              precision    recall  f1-score   support
No Heart Disease       0.79      0.85      0.82        28
Heart Disease          0.85      0.79      0.82        33
```

This demonstrates the Bayesian Network's ability to:
1. Quantify diagnostic uncertainty (probability output, not binary label)
2. Update beliefs dynamically as new evidence streams in
3. Provide clinically interpretable conditional probability tables

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bayesian Network DAG — Heart Disease Topology

The diagram below illustrates the directed acyclic graph (DAG) where **13 clinical features** act as parent nodes influencing the single **child node** `heart_disease`. This is known as a **converging connection** or **collider** at the `heart_disease` node.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[age_cat] --> HD
    n2[sex] --> HD
    n3[cp] --> HD
    n4[exang] --> HD
    n5[trestbps_cat] --> HD
    n6[chol_cat] --> HD
    n7[fbs] --> HD
    n8[restecg] --> HD
    n9[thalach_cat] --> HD
    n10[oldpeak_cat] --> HD
    n11[slope] --> HD
    n12[ca_cat] --> HD
    n13[thal] --> HD
    HD[heart_disease]

    classDef feature fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef target fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:3px,color:#000

    class n1,n2,n3,n4,n5,n6,n7,n8,n9,n10,n11,n12,n13 feature
    class HD target
```

**Interpretation:** Each clinical feature has a *direct probabilistic influence* on the final diagnosis. There are **no edges between feature nodes**, making this a **Naive Bayes-like structure** — but it generalizes NB by allowing arbitrary dependency structures if needed.

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The end-to-end computational pipeline of the experiment is depicted below. Each block represents a distinct processing stage in the Bayesian Network diagnosis workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    blockA[RAW DATASET<br/>UCI Cleveland 303 records<br/>14 attributes] --> blockB[DATA CLEANING<br/>Handle missing values<br/>Remove duplicates]
    blockB --> blockC[DISCRETIZATION LAYER<br/>Bin continuous vars<br/>age chol trestbps thalach oldpeak]
    blockC --> blockD[FEATURE SELECTION<br/>13 input attributes<br/>1 target attribute]
    blockD --> blockE[TRAIN-TEST SPLIT<br/>80 percent train<br/>20 percent test]
    blockE --> blockF[BN STRUCTURE DESIGN<br/>Define DAG edges<br/>Domain knowledge based]
    blockF --> blockG[PARAMETER LEARNING<br/>MLE or BayesianEstimator<br/>Fit CPTs from training data]
    blockG --> blockH[INFERENCE ENGINE<br/>VariableElimination<br/>Algorithm]
    blockH --> blockI[DIAGNOSIS QUERIES<br/>Single symptom<br/>Multi symptom<br/>Batch prediction]
    blockI --> blockJ[OUTPUT<br/>Probability of heart disease<br/>Binary classification<br/>Confidence score]
    blockJ --> blockK[PERFORMANCE METRICS<br/>Accuracy Precision Recall<br/>Confusion Matrix]

    classDef io fill:#E6F3FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef proc fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#996600,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px

    class blockA,blockB,blockD,blockK io
    class blockC,blockE,blockF,blockG,blockH,blockI proc
    class blockJ out
```

### 4.3 Conditional Probability Table (CPT) Anatomy — Sample Illustration

For the child node `heart_disease`, the CPT depends on **all 13 parent nodes**. In practice, due to the curse of dimensionality, BNs are usually restricted to a few parents per node. The diagram below shows how a CPT for `heart_disease` is constructed for a *simplified* 2-parent case (`cp` and `exang`):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    parentA[cp 0] --> cptnode[CPT for heart_disease]
    parentB[cp 1] --> cptnode
    parentC[cp 2] --> cptnode
    parentD[cp 3] --> cptnode
    parentE[exang 0] --> cptnode
    parentF[exang 1] --> cptnode

    cptnode --> outA[P_yes_cp0_exang0<br/>P_no_cp0_exang0]
    cptnode --> outB[P_yes_cp0_exang1<br/>P_no_cp0_exang1]
    cptnode --> outC[P_yes_cp3_exang0<br/>P_no_cp3_exang0]
    cptnode --> outD[P_yes_cp3_exang1<br/>P_no_cp3_exang1]

    classDef parent fill:#FCE4D6,stroke:#C00000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef cpt fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#DAEEF3,stroke:#6C8EBF,stroke-width:1px

    class parentA,parentB,parentC,parentD,parentE,parentF parent
    class cptnode cpt
    class outA,outB,outC,outD output
```

**Reading the CPT:** Each row corresponds to a unique combination of parent states. The columns give the conditional probabilities of `heart_disease` = Yes / No, which sum to 1.0 per row.

### 4.4 Inference Workflow — Sequential Belief Updating

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Doc as Cardiologist / User
    participant UI as Diagnosis Interface
    participant BN as Bayesian Network Engine
    participant VE as Variable Elimination
    participant DB as Trained CPT Database

    Doc->>UI: Submit patient evidence<br/>e.g. age_cat=Senior, cp=3
    UI->>BN: Forward evidence dict
    BN->>VE: Initiate Variable Elimination
    VE->>DB: Query CPTs for relevant nodes
    DB-->>VE: Return conditional probabilities
    VE->>VE: Marginalize non-query variables
    VE-->>BN: Return P(heart_disease | evidence)
    BN-->>UI: Posterior probability
    UI-->>Doc: Diagnosis + Confidence Score

    Note over Doc,DB: Loop continues as more test results arrive<br/>Probability updates each iteration
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Define a Bayesian Network. What are its two principal components?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> A **Bayesian Network (BN)** is a probabilistic graphical model that represents a set of random variables and their conditional dependencies via a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**.
>
> The two principal components are:
> 1. **Qualitative Component — DAG Structure:** Nodes represent random variables; directed edges encode conditional (often causal) dependencies between them. The graph must be acyclic.
> 2. **Quantitative Component — Conditional Probability Tables (CPTs):** Each node $X_i$ has an associated CPT specifying $P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$. Together, the DAG and CPTs uniquely define the joint probability distribution via the chain rule:
>
> $$
> P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))
> $$
>
> *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for DAG component, 1 mark for CPT + formula.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] List any three advantages of using Bayesian Networks for medical diagnosis over traditional rule-based expert systems.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> 1. **Probabilistic Reasoning under Uncertainty:** BNs compute posterior probabilities $P(\text{Disease} \mid \text{Evidence})$ rather than binary yes/no outputs, allowing quantification of diagnostic confidence. *(1 mark)*
>
> 2. **Graceful Degradation with Missing Data:** Inference proceeds even when some patient test results are unavailable — BNs marginalize over unobserved variables, unlike rule-based systems that fail on incomplete inputs. *(1 mark)*
>
> 3. **Causal & Explainable Reasoning:** The DAG structure provides transparent causal pathways (e.g., *age → chest pain → heart disease*), supporting regulatory compliance and clinician trust. *(1 mark)*
>
> *(Bonus acceptable answers: incorporation of prior knowledge, ability to combine expert input with data-driven learning, support for both diagnostic and predictive inference.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the mathematical foundations of Bayesian Networks with reference to Bayes' theorem and the chain rule of probability. Derive the joint distribution factorization formula and discuss how it reduces parameter complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Python program to construct a Bayesian Network for heart disease diagnosis using the UCI Cleveland dataset. Define the DAG structure with at least 5 clinical attributes as parent nodes and `heart_disease` as the target. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Mathematical Foundations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Bayes' Theorem Foundation (2 marks):**

The conditional probability of event $A$ given $B$ is:

$$
P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(B \mid A) \cdot P(A)}{P(B)}
$$

In medical diagnosis terminology, $A$ = disease hypothesis, $B$ = observed symptom/evidence.

**Step 2 — Chain Rule Application (2 marks):**

For $n$ random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$, the chain rule expresses the joint distribution as:

$$
P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = P(X_1) \cdot P(X_2 \mid X_1) \cdot P(X_3 \mid X_1, X_2) \cdots P(X_n \mid X_1, \ldots, X_{n-1})
$$

**Step 3 — BN Factorization (2 marks):**

The DAG's local Markov property asserts that each variable is conditionally independent of its non-descendants given its parents. Applying this to the chain rule:

$$
P(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))
$$

**Step 4 — Parameter Reduction (1 mark):**

For 14 binary variables: naïve joint distribution needs $2^{14} - 1 = 16{,}383$ parameters. With 13 parents each having only 2 states, each node's CPT needs $2 \times 2^{13} = 16{,}384$ entries — but only those with reasonable conditional independencies survive, drastically reducing the effective count.

**(b) Python Program (7 marks):**

```python
import pandas as pd
from pgmpy.models import BayesianNetwork
from pgmpy.estimators import MaximumLikelihoodEstimator
from pgmpy.inference import VariableElimination

# Step 1: Load dataset
df = pd.read_csv('heart.csv')
df['hd'] = df['target'].apply(lambda x: 'Yes' if x > 0 else 'No')

# Step 2: Discretize age and cholesterol
df['age_cat']  = pd.cut(df['age'],   bins=[0, 40, 55, 65, 100],
                         labels=['Young', 'Middle', 'Senior', 'Elderly'])
df['chol_cat'] = pd.cut(df['chol'],  bins=[0, 200, 240, 600],
                         labels=['Normal', 'Borderline', 'High'])

# Step 3: Define DAG structure (5 parent nodes minimum)
model = BayesianNetwork([
    ('age_cat',  'hd'),
    ('sex',      'hd'),
    ('cp',       'hd'),
    ('chol_cat', 'hd'),
    ('thalach',  'hd'),
    ('exang',    'hd')
])

# Step 4: Fit CPTs
data = df[['age_cat', 'sex', 'cp', 'chol_cat', 'thalach', 'exang', 'hd']].dropna()
for col in data.columns:
    data[col] = data[col].astype(str)
model.fit(data, estimator=MaximumLikelihoodEstimator)

# Step 5: Inference and diagnosis
inference = VariableElimination(model)
result = inference.query(variables=['hd'], evidence={'cp': 2, 'exang': 1})
print(result)
```

*(Award marks: 1 for data loading, 1 for discretization, 2 for DAG definition, 1 for fitting, 2 for inference demonstration.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - June 2024]*

**(a)** Describe the structure of the UCI Cleveland Heart Disease dataset. Explain why continuous variables must be discretized before being used in a Bayesian Network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Demonstrate the inference process for diagnosing a new patient using the trained Bayesian Network. Show how the posterior probability $P(\text{heart\_disease} \mid \text{evidence})$ is computed when multiple symptoms are observed. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution:**

**(a) Dataset Structure & Discretization Rationale (7 marks):**

**Dataset Description (3 marks):**

The UCI Cleveland Heart Disease dataset contains **303 patient records** with **14 attributes**:

| Type | Attributes |
|---|---|
| **Demographics** | `age`, `sex` |
| **Symptoms** | `cp` (chest pain type), `exang` (exercise angina) |
| **Vital Signs** | `trestbps` (resting BP), `thalach` (max heart rate) |
| **Lab Results** | `chol` (cholesterol), `fbs` (fasting blood sugar) |
| **Diagnostic Tests** | `restecg`, `oldpeak`, `slope`, `ca`, `thal` |
| **Target** | `target` (0 = No disease, 1 = Disease) |

**Why Discretization is Necessary (4 marks):**

1. **CPTs are Finite Tables:** Conditional Probability Tables require discrete parent configurations. With continuous variables, the joint space is infinite. *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Statistical Sufficiency:** Discretization into clinically meaningful bins (e.g., cholesterol: Normal <200, Borderline 200-240, High ≥240) aligns with medical risk categories, preserving diagnostic information. *(1 mark)*

3. **Avoiding Sparse Cell Counts:** Continuous variables produce many unique combinations with few or zero samples, leading to unreliable MLE estimates. Binning concentrates probability mass into well-populated cells. *(1 mark)*

4. **Algorithmic Compatibility:** The `pgmpy` library's CPT-based inference assumes discrete variables; continuous BNs require parametric forms (e.g., Gaussian BNs) which are harder to learn. *(0.5 mark)*

**(b) Multi-Symptom Inference Demonstration (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Evidence Vector (1 mark):**

For a new patient with symptoms: senior male, chest pain type 3, exercise-induced angina, high cholesterol:

```python
evidence = {
    'age_cat':  'Senior',
    'sex':      '1',
    'cp':       '3',
    'exang':    '1',
    'chol_cat': 'High'
}
```

**Step 2 — Posterior Computation via Bayes' Theorem (3 marks):**

The posterior is computed via Variable Elimination which marginalizes over hidden variables:

$$
P(\text{hd} \mid \text{evidence}) = \frac{P(\text{evidence} \mid \text{hd}) \cdot P(\text{hd})}{P(\text{evidence})}
$$

Numerator expansion using BN factorization:

$$
P(\text{hd}, \text{evidence}) = P(\text{hd} \mid \text{age\_cat}, \text{sex}, \text{cp}, \text{exang}, \text{chol\_cat}) \cdot \prod_{i} P(\text{parent}_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Code Implementation (2 marks):**

```python
from pgmpy.inference import VariableElimination
inference = VariableElimination(model)
result = inference.query(variables=['hd'], evidence=evidence)
print(f"P(Heart Disease | evidence) = {result.values[1]:.4f}")
# Expected: probability around 0.85-0.95 for high-risk patient
```

**Step 4 — Dynamic Belief Updating (1 mark):**

If a subsequent test (e.g., `thalach_cat = Low`) becomes available, re-query with extended evidence:

```python
evidence['thalach_cat'] = 'Low'
result_updated = inference.query(variables=['hd'], evidence=evidence)
print(f"Updated P(Heart Disease | extended evidence) = {result_updated.values[1]:.4f}")
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Discretization:** Students frequently pass continuous `age`, `chol`, etc. directly to `model.fit()` — `pgmpy` throws an error or produces NaN CPTs. **Always discretize first.** *(−2 marks)*
>
> 2. **Inconsistent Train/Test Categories:** If a test-set category (e.g., `chol = 280`) was never seen during training, inference raises `KeyError`. Always use `pd.cut()` or `astype(str)` consistently across both sets. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 3. **Missing Train-Test Split:** Students often evaluate on the training data, inflating accuracy to 99%+. **Always split before fitting.** *(−1 mark)*
>
> 4. **Cyclic Graphs:** Defining an edge like `heart_disease → cp` creates a cycle — `pgmpy` rejects it. **Always ensure DAG is acyclic.** *(−2 marks)*
>
> 5. **Confusing MLE with MAP:** MLE gives raw frequency estimates; MAP / Bayesian estimation applies priors. Specify which you used. *(−1 mark)*
>
> 6. **No Threshold Justification:** Predicting 'Yes' for every probability ≥ 0.5 is conventional but should be **explicitly stated** with clinical reasoning. *(−0.5 mark)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** A Bayesian Network = DAG (structure) + CPTs (parameters). Together, they uniquely specify the joint distribution.
- **Factorization Theorem:** $P(X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \prod_i P(X_i \mid \text{Parents}(X_i))$ — *memorize this equation*.
- **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(H \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid H)\,P(H)}{P(E)}$ — foundational formula for all inference.
- **Local Markov Property:** Each node is conditionally independent of non-descendants given its parents.
- **MLE Formula:** $\hat{P}(X_i = x \mid \text{Parents}) = \dfrac{N(x, \text{Parents})}{N(\text{Parents})}$
- **Discretization is Mandatory:** Bin continuous variables (`age`, `chol`, `trestbps`, `thalach`, `oldpeak`) into clinically meaningful categories before fitting.
- **Standard Bins:**
  - Age: `<40` Young / `40-54` Middle / `55-64` Senior / `≥65` Elderly
  - Cholesterol: `<200` Normal / `200-239` Borderline / `≥240` High
  - Blood Pressure: `<120` Normal / `120-139` Elevated / `140-159` High / `≥160` Very High
- **Library:** `pgmpy` is the standard Python library for BN modeling. Use `BayesianNetwork`, `MaximumLikelihoodEstimator`, `BayesianEstimator`, and `VariableElimination`.
- **Inference Output:** Variable Elimination returns a `Factor` object with `.values[0]` = P(No) and `.values[1]` = P(Yes).
- **Heart Disease Dataset:** 303 records × 14 attributes from UCI Cleveland — the canonical KTU lab benchmark.
- **Target Variable:** `heart_disease` ∈ {`Yes`, `No`} (derived from `target` column).
- **Expected Accuracy:** Typically **78% – 85%** on the Cleveland test set with naive BN structure.
- **Pipeline Order:** Load → Clean → Discretize → Define DAG → Split → Fit → Inspect CPTs → Query → Evaluate.
- **Confusion Matrix:** Use `sklearn.metrics.confusion_matrix` and visualize with `seaborn.heatmap`.
- **Why BNs over Naive Bayes:** Naive Bayes assumes feature independence; BNs allow arbitrary directed dependencies — more expressive but computationally heavier.
- **Real-World Applications:** QMR-DT medical expert system, Microsoft's Office Assistant, spam filters, fault diagnosis in aviation/automotive, gene regulatory networks.
- **Examiner Loves:** Asking for the **factorization equation**, **Bayes' theorem derivation**, and a **complete code walkthrough** with **expected output**.
- **Common Pitfall:** Forgetting to convert all columns to `str` before calling `model.fit()` — this is the **#1 cause** of pgmpy runtime errors in KTU labs.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module Experiment 2: EM Algorithm vs K-Means Clustering

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clustering** is an unsupervised learning technique that groups a set of data points into clusters such that data points in the same cluster are more similar to each other than to those in other clusters, based on a defined similarity measure.

**K-Means Algorithm (Hard Clustering):**
K-Means is a centroid-based partitioning algorithm that aims to partition *n* data points into *k* clusters by minimizing the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS). Each data point belongs **exclusively** to one cluster — this is called *hard assignment*.

**Expectation-Maximization (EM) Algorithm (Soft Clustering):**
EM is an iterative algorithm for finding Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE) of parameters in probabilistic models that depend on unobserved latent variables. In clustering, EM uses **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)** where each data point has a *probability* of belonging to each cluster — this is *soft assignment*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Classroom Grouping Problem**

Imagine a teacher (the algorithm) who wants to divide **50 students** into **3 study groups** based on their performance in Mathematics and Programming.

- **K-Means Approach (Hard Clustering):** The teacher measures each student once and *firmly assigns* them to one group: "You are in Group A, Period." If a student is borderline (70% like Group A, 30% like Group B), they are still forced into one group. The teacher then calculates the *average* score of each group and reassigns students to the nearest group. This repeats until no one moves groups. **Each student is in exactly ONE group.**

- **EM Approach (Soft Clustering):** The teacher says, "You are **40% in Group A, 35% in Group B, and 25% in Group C**." The teacher considers the *shape* of each group's performance distribution (some groups have wide variability, some are tight). The teacher then recalculates group characteristics and updates the probability of membership. **Every student has a probability of being in ALL groups simultaneously.**

### 1.3 Key Comparison Metrics (Standard Metrics in Bold)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are commonly used to evaluate clustering quality in KTU lab evaluations:
> - **Silhouette Score**: Ranges from **-1 to +1**; values closer to **+1** indicate well-defined clusters.
> - **Davies-Bouldin Index (DBI)**: Lower values indicate **better separation** between clusters.
> - **Inertia (WCSS)**: Within-Cluster Sum of Squares — **lower is better** for K-Means.
> - **Log-Likelihood**: Used for GMM/EM — **higher is better**.

### 1.4 Practical CSV Dataset Used

For this experiment, we use the well-known **Iris dataset** (or a custom 2D synthetic CSV like `Mall_Customers.csv` / `blobs.csv`) since it provides clear visualization and ground truth labels for comparison.

| Dataset Property | Description |
|---|---|
| File Format | `.csv` (Comma-Separated Values) |
| Recommended Dataset | `Iris.csv` (150 samples, 4 features) or `customer_data.csv` |
| Features Used (Example) | `Annual Income`, `Spending Score` (2D for visualization) |
| Number of Clusters (k) | 3 (matching the 3 species of Iris) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 K-Means Algorithm — Operational Steps

K-Means follows a **Lloyd's Algorithm** iterative procedure:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always state that K-Means minimizes the objective function **J = Σ ||x_i - μ_k||²** using the Expectation-Maximization framework, but with **hard assignments**.

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Initialization:** Randomly select *k* initial centroids μ₁, μ₂, ..., μ_k from the dataset.
2. **Assignment Step (E-Step equivalent):** For each data point x_i, assign it to the nearest centroid based on Euclidean distance.
3. **Update Step (M-Step equivalent):** Recalculate each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to its cluster.
4. **Convergence Check:** Repeat Steps 2–3 until centroids no longer change OR a maximum iteration count is reached.
5. **Output:** Final cluster centroids and cluster labels for all data points.

## 2.2 EM Algorithm for Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM) — Operational Steps

The EM algorithm alternates between two steps to maximize the log-likelihood of the data given the model parameters.

### Mathematical Foundation:

A **Gaussian Mixture Model** assumes the data is generated from a mixture of *k* Gaussian distributions:

$$P(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \cdot \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$$

Where:
- **π_k** = mixing coefficient (weight) of the k-th Gaussian (Σ π_k = 1)
- **μ_k** = mean vector of the k-th Gaussian
- **Σ_k** = covariance matrix of the k-th Gaussian
- **𝒩(x | μ_k, Σ_k)** = multivariate Gaussian probability density function

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Initialization:** Initialize parameters θ⁽⁰⁾ = {π_k, μ_k, Σ_k} for k = 1, ..., K.
2. **E-Step (Expectation):** Calculate the *responsibility* (posterior probability) that component k takes for data point x_i.
3. **M-Step (Maximization):** Update the parameters θ to maximize the expected log-likelihood.
4. **Convergence Check:** Repeat E-Step and M-Step until the change in log-likelihood < threshold (e.g., 1e-6) OR maximum iterations reached.
5. **Output:** Final parameters and the cluster assignment (typically the component with the highest posterior probability).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **J (WCSS)** | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$ | Minimized by K-Means |
| **d(x, μ)** | Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n}(x_j - \mu_j)^2}$ | Used in K-Means assignment |
| **μ_k (new)** | Centroid Update | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | M-Step of K-Means |
| **GMM Density** | Mixture Density | $P(x) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ | GMM model definition |
| **𝒩(x μ, Σ)** | Multivariate Gaussian | $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu, \Sigma) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\pi)^d \vert \Sigma \vert}} \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)\right)$ | Component density |
| **γ(z_nk)** | Responsibility (E-Step) | $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$ | Soft assignment |
| **N_k** | Effective Member Count | $N_k = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})$ | Used in M-Step |
| **μ_k (EM)** | Mean Update (M-Step) | $\mu_k^{new} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n$ | EM M-Step |
| **Σ_k (EM)** | Covariance Update (M-Step) | $\Sigma_k^{new} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})(x_n - \mu_k)(x_n - \mu_k)^T$ | EM M-Step |
| **π_k (EM)** | Mixing Coeff Update | $\pi_k^{new} = \frac{N_k}{N}$ | EM M-Step |
| **log L** | Log-Likelihood | $\log L(\theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \log \sum_{k=1}^{K} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$ | Convergence metric |
| **Silhouette** | Quality Score | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | Range: [-1, +1] |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Mathematical Derivation of the EM Algorithm

### Derivation 1: E-Step — Computing the Posterior Responsibilities

Starting from the latent variable model, we want to compute the posterior probability of the latent variable z_nk (which indicates that x_n was generated by component k) given the current parameters θ^old.

$$P(z_{nk} = 1 \mid x_n, \theta^{old}) = \frac{P(z_{nk}=1) \cdot P(x_n \mid z_{nk}=1, \theta^{old})}{P(x_n \mid \theta^{old})}$$

Using Bayes' theorem and the law of total probability, the denominator expands to the sum over all K components:

$$P(x_n \mid \theta^{old}) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} P(z_{nj}=1) \cdot P(x_n \mid z_{nj}=1, \theta^{old}) = \sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)$$

Substituting back, we get the **responsibility** function:

$$\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$$

> **Logic Note:** This represents the *soft assignment* — the probability that data point x_n belongs to cluster k under the current parameter estimates.

### Derivation 2: M-Step — Parameter Updates

The expected value of the complete-data log-likelihood with respect to the posterior distribution of the latent variables (computed in E-Step) is:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{old}) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \log \pi_k + \log \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

**Maximizing with respect to μ_k** by setting ∂Q/∂μ_k = 0:

$$\frac{\partial Q}{\partial \mu_k} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) \Sigma_k^{-1}(x_n - \mu_k) = 0$$

Solving for μ_k:

$$\mu_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n}{\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk})} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n$$

**Maximizing with respect to Σ_k** by setting ∂Q/∂Σ_k = 0 (subject to Lagrange multiplier constraint):

$$\Sigma_k^{new} = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{nk}) (x_n - \mu_k^{new})(x_n - \mu_k^{new})^T$$

**Maximizing with respect to π_k** subject to Σ π_k = 1 (using Lagrange multiplier λ):

$$\pi_k^{new} = \frac{N_k}{N}$$

### Derivation 3: Convergence Guarantee

The EM algorithm **monotonically increases** the log-likelihood at each iteration:

$$\log L(\theta^{new}) \geq \log L(\theta^{old})$$

This is guaranteed by the **Jensen's Inequality** applied to the concave log function:

$$\log \sum_{k} \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n) \geq \sum_{k} \gamma(z_{nk}) \log \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n)}{\gamma(z_{nk})}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | K-Means | EM (GMM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Clustering Type** | Hard (each point → 1 cluster) | Soft (probability of belonging to each cluster) |
| **Cluster Shape Assumption** | Spherical (isotropic) | Ellipsoidal (full covariance) |
| **Cluster Size Assumption** | Equal variance | Different variances allowed |
| **Assignment Output** | Integer label (0, 1, ..., K-1) | Probability matrix [N × K] |
| **Objective Function** | Minimize WCSS | Maximize Log-Likelihood |
| **Computational Complexity** | O(N·K·I·d) | O(N·K·I·d²) — more expensive |
| **Initialization Sensitivity** | High (use K-Means++) | High (use K-Means to init EM) |
| **Convergence** | Local minimum | Local maximum of likelihood |
| **Number of Parameters** | K means (K·d) | K means, K covariances, K weights |
| **Handles Overlap** | Poorly | Excellently |
| **Sklearn Class** | `KMeans` | `GaussianMixture` |
| **Quality Score (Silhouette, Iris)** | ~0.55 | ~0.50–0.58 (comparable) |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Small Dataset)

Consider a 2D dataset with **N = 4** points and **K = 2** clusters:

| Point | x₁ | x₂ |
|---|---|---|
| x₁ | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| x₂ | 1.5 | 2.0 |
| x₃ | 5.0 | 5.0 |
| x₄ | 6.0 | 5.5 |

### K-Means Iteration 1:

**Step A — Initialize Centroids** (assume μ₁ = (1, 1), μ₂ = (5, 5)):

**Step B — Compute Distances:**

$$d(x_1, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (1-1)^2} = 0.00$$
$$d(x_1, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(1-5)^2 + (1-5)^2} = 5.66$$

**Step C — Assignment:** x₁ → Cluster 1; x₂ → Cluster 1; x₃ → Cluster 2; x₄ → Cluster 2.

**Step D — Update Centroids:**

$$\mu_1 = \frac{(1,1) + (1.5, 2)}{2} = (1.25, 1.5)$$
$$\mu_2 = \frac{(5,5) + (6, 5.5)}{2} = (5.5, 5.25)$$

**Convergence** achieved in 2 iterations for this clean data.

### EM (GMM) Iteration 1:

**Step A — E-Step:** Calculate γ(z_nk) for each point. For x₁, assuming σ² = 1:

$$\mathcal{N}(x_1 \mid \mu_1, I) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{0}{2}\right) = 1.000$$
$$\mathcal{N}(x_1 \mid \mu_2, I) \propto \exp\left(-\frac{25}{2}\right) = 0.00000$$

$$\gamma(z_{11}) = \frac{0.5 \times 1.000}{0.5 \times 1.000 + 0.5 \times 0.00000} \approx 1.0$$

This shows the **soft** nature: x₁ has probability ≈1.0 of being in Cluster 1 and ≈0.0 of being in Cluster 2.

**Step B — M-Step:** Recompute means weighted by γ values (effectively identical to K-Means for this clean case).

> **Key Insight:** For well-separated, equal-variance, spherical clusters, K-Means and EM produce **nearly identical results**. The differences emerge when clusters have different sizes, shapes, or overlap.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**Differentiate between hard clustering and soft clustering. Give one example algorithm for each.**

**Model Answer:**

Hard clustering assigns each data point to **exactly one cluster** exclusively, with no ambiguity. Example: **K-Means** algorithm where each point x_i belongs to one cluster C_k and the assignment is binary (0 or 1).

Soft clustering assigns each data point a **probability distribution** over all clusters, indicating the likelihood of membership in each. Example: **Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm with Gaussian Mixture Models** where the output is a matrix of responsibilities γ(z_nk) ∈ [0, 1] with the constraint that Σ_k γ(z_nk) = 1 for each point.

> [!NOTE]
> The key difference is that K-Means produces a *deterministic* label, while EM produces a *probabilistic* assignment that captures uncertainty.

---

### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam - Model Question]

**What is the role of the E-Step and M-Step in the EM algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

The **E-Step (Expectation Step)** computes the posterior probability (called the *responsibility*) that each latent variable z_nk = 1 given the observed data x_n and the current parameter estimates θ^old. The formula is:

$$\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$$

The **M-Step (Maximization Step)** updates the model parameters θ = {π_k, μ_k, Σ_k} to maximize the expected complete-data log-likelihood computed in the E-Step. The update equations are:

$$\mu_k^{new} = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n, \quad \Sigma_k^{new} = \frac{1}{N_k}\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})(x_n - \mu_k)(x_n - \mu_k)^T, \quad \pi_k^{new} = \frac{N_k}{N}$$

The E-Step "guesses" the hidden cluster assignments, while the M-Step "refines" the cluster parameters to better fit the data.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Model Question Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm in detail. Derive the E-Step and M-Step update equations for a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) with K components. Clearly state the role of latent variables in the formulation.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A 2D dataset is given in `cluster_data.csv` with two features. Implement the EM algorithm using `sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture` to cluster the data into 3 groups. Write the complete Python code with proper initialization, fitting, and visualization using a scatter plot. Compute and report the Silhouette Score.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The EM algorithm is used to find **Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE)** in the presence of **latent (hidden) variables**. In the GMM context, the latent variable z_n is a K-dimensional binary vector where z_nk = 1 means x_n was generated by the k-th Gaussian.

**The Complete-Data Log-Likelihood:**

$$\log P(X, Z \mid \theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} z_{nk} \left[ \log \pi_k + \log \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

Since Z is unknown, we take the **expectation** of this quantity with respect to the posterior distribution of Z given X and θ^old:

$$Q(\theta, \theta^{old}) = \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{old}}[\log P(X, Z \mid \theta)] = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{nk}) \left[ \log \pi_k + \log \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k) \right]$$

**E-Step (using Bayes Theorem):**

$$\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k^{old} \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k^{old}, \Sigma_k^{old})}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j^{old} \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j^{old}, \Sigma_j^{old})}$$

**M-Step (maximize Q by setting partial derivatives to zero):**

$$\mu_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})}$$

$$\Sigma_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) (x_n - \mu_k^{new})(x_n - \mu_k^{new})^T}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})}$$

$$\pi_k^{new} = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})}{N}$$

**Convergence Guarantee:** EM monotonically increases the log-likelihood P(X | θ) at each iteration and converges to a local maximum.

#### Model Solution for (b):

```python
# Importing required libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

# Step 1: Load the CSV file
data = pd.read_csv('cluster_data.csv')
print("First 5 rows of dataset:")
print(data.head())

# Step 2: Extract features (assuming 2 numeric features for visualization)
X = data.iloc[:, [0, 1]].values

# Step 3: Standardize the features (zero mean, unit variance)
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Step 4: Apply EM Algorithm using Gaussian Mixture Model
gmm = GaussianMixture(
    n_components=3,        # K = 3 clusters
    covariance_type='full', # Full covariance matrix
    max_iter=200,          # Maximum iterations
    n_init=10,             # Run 10 times with different initializations
    random_state=42
)
gmm.fit(X_scaled)

# Step 5: Get the cluster labels (hard assignment from soft probabilities)
labels = gmm.predict(X_scaled)

# Step 6: Compute Silhouette Score
sil_score = silhouette_score(X_scaled, labels)
print(f"Silhouette Score (EM/GMM): {sil_score:.4f}")

# Step 7: Visualization
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
plt.scatter(X_scaled[:, 0], X_scaled[:, 1], c=labels, cmap='viridis', s=50, edgecolor='k')
plt.scatter(gmm.means_[:, 0], gmm.means_[:, 1], c='red', marker='X', s=200, label='GMM Means')
plt.title('EM Algorithm (GMM) Clustering Result')
plt.xlabel('Feature 1 (Standardized)')
plt.ylabel('Feature 2 (Standardized)')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
```

**Expected Output Interpretation:** A higher Silhouette Score (closer to +1) indicates better-defined clusters. The red X markers represent the Gaussian means (centroids in higher-dimensional space).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Model Question Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the K-Means clustering algorithm with its objective function. Discuss the limitations of K-Means that motivate the use of the EM algorithm.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the **same** CSV dataset from above, implement K-Means clustering with `sklearn.cluster.KMeans`. Compare the Silhouette Scores of K-Means and EM/GMM. Plot both clustering results side by side using subplots and comment on the quality of clustering.

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **K-Means algorithm** partitions N data points into K clusters by minimizing the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** objective function:

$$J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2$$

where μ_k is the centroid (mean) of cluster C_k.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize K centroids randomly from the data points.
2. **Assignment Step:** Assign each x_i to the cluster with the nearest centroid using Euclidean distance: $C_k = \{x_i : \Vert x_i - \mu_k \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_i - \mu_j \Vert^2 \, \forall j\}$
3. **Update Step:** Recompute centroids: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$
4. Repeat Steps 2–3 until convergence.

**Limitations of K-Means that Motivate EM:**

1. **Hard Assignment Issue:** K-Means forces each point into exactly one cluster, which is unrealistic for overlapping clusters. EM allows *probabilistic* membership.
2. **Spherical Cluster Assumption:** K-Means assumes clusters are isotropic (equal variance, spherical shape). EM with GMM can model **ellipsoidal clusters** of different sizes and orientations using the full covariance matrix Σ_k.
3. **Equal Cluster Size Bias:** K-Means tends to produce clusters of similar size. EM handles clusters of varying densities.
4. **Sensitivity to Outliers:** K-Means is highly sensitive to outliers since the centroid is the arithmetic mean. EM is more robust because it uses probabilistic weighting.
5. **No Probabilistic Output:** K-Means provides only labels, not confidence scores. EM provides the posterior probability distribution.

#### Model Solution for (b):

```python
# Importing required libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.cluster import KMeans
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.metrics import silhouette_score

# Step 1: Load the CSV file
data = pd.read_csv('cluster_data.csv')
X = data.iloc[:, [0, 1]].values

# Step 2: Standardize features
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)

# Step 3: Apply K-Means Algorithm
kmeans = KMeans(
    n_clusters=3,
    init='k-means++',   # Smart initialization
    n_init=10,
    max_iter=300,
    random_state=42
)
kmeans_labels = kmeans.fit_predict(X_scaled)
kmeans_sil = silhouette_score(X_scaled, kmeans_labels)
print(f"Silhouette Score (K-Means): {kmeans_sil:.4f}")

# Step 4: Apply EM/GMM Algorithm (same dataset)
gmm = GaussianMixture(n_components=3, covariance_type='full', n_init=10, random_state=42)
gmm.fit(X_scaled)
gmm_labels = gmm.predict(X_scaled)
gmm_sil = silhouette_score(X_scaled, gmm_labels)
print(f"Silhouette Score (EM/GMM): {gmm_sil:.4f}")

# Step 5: Side-by-side Visualization using Subplots
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 6))

# K-Means Plot
axes[0].scatter(X_scaled[:, 0], X_scaled[:, 1], c=kmeans_labels, cmap='viridis', s=50, edgecolor='k')
axes[0].scatter(kmeans.cluster_centers_[:, 0], kmeans.cluster_centers_[:, 1],
                c='red', marker='X', s=200, label='Centroids')
axes[0].set_title(f'K-Means Clustering\nSilhouette Score: {kmeans_sil:.4f}')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Feature 1')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Feature 2')
axes[0].legend()
axes[0].grid(True)

# EM/GMM Plot
axes[1].scatter(X_scaled[:, 0], X_scaled[:, 1], c=gmm_labels, cmap='viridis', s=50, edgecolor='k')
axes[1].scatter(gmm.means_[:, 0], gmm.means_[:, 1],
                c='red', marker='X', s=200, label='GMM Means')
axes[1].set_title(f'EM (GMM) Clustering\nSilhouette Score: {gmm_sil:.4f}')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Feature 1')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Feature 2')
axes[1].legend()
axes[1].grid(True)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('kmeans_vs_em_comparison.png', dpi=100)
plt.show()

# Step 6: Comparison Table
print("\n========== COMPARISON TABLE ==========")
print(f"{'Metric':<30}{'K-Means':<15}{'EM (GMM)':<15}")
print("-" * 60)
print(f"{'Silhouette Score':<30}{kmeans_sil:<15.4f}{gmm_sil:<15.4f}")
print(f"{'Clustering Type':<30}{'Hard':<15}{'Soft':<15}")
print(f"{'Cluster Shape':<30}{'Spherical':<15}{'Ellipsoidal':<15}")
print(f"{'Iterations Used':<30}{kmeans.n_iter_:<15}{gmm.n_iter_:<15}")
```

**Comments on Quality of Clustering:**

- If the **Silhouette Score of EM/GMM is higher**, it indicates that GMM better captures the **non-spherical shapes** and **varying densities** of the clusters in the dataset.
- If the scores are **similar**, the dataset has well-separated, roughly spherical clusters, and K-Means is computationally cheaper and sufficient.
- K-Means is **faster** and easier to interpret; EM/GMM is **more flexible** and provides probability outputs.
- For **overlapping clusters**, EM/GMM typically performs better due to soft assignments.
- For **clean, spherical data**, both produce nearly identical results.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT skip standardization** — clustering algorithms are distance-based and sensitive to feature scaling. Failing to apply `StandardScaler` will result in incorrect clusters and **deduct 2 marks**.
> 2. **Always mention `random_state`** for reproducibility — examiners will check for deterministic results.
> 3. **Confusing K-Means with EM** is a common error. Remember: K-Means is NOT the EM algorithm; it is a *special case* of EM with hard assignments and isotropic Gaussians.
> 4. **Forgetting the covariance_type='full'** in `GaussianMixture` will limit GMM to spherical clusters (similar to K-Means), defeating the purpose of comparison.
> 5. **Not using n_init ≥ 10** in both algorithms may lead to convergence to poor local optima.
> 6. **Computing Silhouette Score incorrectly** (e.g., on raw data instead of scaled data) will yield wrong results.
