## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Clustering** is **unsupervised learning** — no labels are used during training.
- **K-Means** performs **hard clustering**; each point is assigned to exactly one cluster.
- **EM (with GMM)** performs **soft clustering**; each point has a probability of belonging to each cluster.
- The K-Means **objective function** is **J = Σ ||x_i − μ_k||²** (WCSS — minimize this).
- The EM algorithm **maximizes the log-likelihood** $L(\theta) = \sum_n \log \sum_k \pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_k, \Sigma_k)$.
- The **E-Step** computes responsibilities $\gamma(z_{nk}) = \frac{\pi_k \mathcal{N}(x_n)}{\sum_j \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n)}$.
- The **M-Step** updates: $\mu_k = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk}) x_n$, $\Sigma_k = \frac{1}{N_k} \sum_n \gamma(z_{nk})(x_n - \mu_k)(x_n - \mu_k)^T$, $\pi_k = N_k/N$.
- EM is guaranteed to **converge** (monotonically increase log-likelihood) but only to a **local maximum**.
- K-Means is a **special case** of EM with hard assignments and **identity covariance matrices**.
- GMM with `covariance_type='full'` can model **ellipsoidal clusters**; K-Means can only model **spherical clusters**.
- GMM with `covariance_type='tied'` shares a single covariance matrix across all components.
- GMM with `covariance_type='diag'` uses diagonal covariance (independent features).
- GMM with `covariance_type='spherical'` is closest to K-Means behavior.
- **Always apply `StandardScaler`** before clustering — both algorithms are distance/probability based.
- **Silhouette Score** ∈ [−1, +1] — higher is better; values above 0.5 indicate strong clustering.
- **Inertia (WCSS)** is K-Means specific — used in the **Elbow Method** to find optimal K.
- **BIC/AIC** are used to select the optimal number of GMM components (lower is better).
- For **K-Means initialization**, always prefer `init='k-means++'` over random initialization.
- The **EM algorithm is computationally more expensive** than K-Means due to covariance matrix inversions.
- A typical lab CSV file is read using `pandas.read_csv()` and features are extracted using `.iloc[:, [0,1]].values`.
- The `sklearn.mixture.GaussianMixture` class implements EM; `sklearn.cluster.KMeans` implements K-Means.
- The `predict()` method of GMM returns the **argmax** of the responsibilities (hard label), while `predict_proba()` returns the **soft probabilities**.
- **Common CSV datasets for this experiment:** `Iris.csv`, `Mall_Customers.csv`, `blobs.csv`, `customer_data.csv`, `wine.csv`.
- **K-Means assumes equal-sized spherical clusters** — its biggest weakness addressed by EM.
- The **log-likelihood increases monotonically** with each EM iteration (proven by Jensen's inequality).
- **EM can be initialized using K-Means** output (set `gmm.means_ = kmeans.cluster_centers_`) for faster convergence.
- The **mixture weights** π_k represent the prior probability of each Gaussian component.
- A common exam trick: "EM is the same as K-Means" is **FALSE** — K-Means is a *limiting case* of EM.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write a program to implement k-Nearest Neighbour algorithm to classify the iris data set. Print both correct and wrong predictions.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL508 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write a program to implement k-Nearest Neighbour algorithm to classify the iris data set. Print both correct and wrong predictions.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **k-Nearest Neighbour (k-NN) algorithm** is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm used for classification (and regression) tasks. For a given unseen test instance, the algorithm identifies the *k* closest training samples in the feature space using a distance metric and assigns the class label by **majority voting** among those *k* neighbours.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> "k-NN is a lazy learner that postpones generalisation until query time. The decision boundary is locally defined; the model memorises the entire training set and performs classification only when a test point is presented."

### 1.2 The Iris Dataset — Formal Description

The **Iris flower dataset** (introduced by **Ronald A. Fisher in 1936**) is the canonical "Hello World" dataset of pattern recognition. It contains:

| Attribute | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Total Instances | **150** |
| Number of Features | **4** (all numeric, in cm) |
| Number of Classes | **3** (Setosa, Versicolor, Virginica) |
| Instances per Class | **50** (perfectly balanced) |
| Feature Names | sepal length, sepal width, petal length, petal width |

The four features are:
- $x_1$ = sepal length (cm)
- $x_2$ = sepal width (cm)
- $x_3$ = petal length (cm)
- $x_4$ = petal width (cm)

The target vector $y \in \{0, 1, 2\}$ maps to:
- $y = 0 \Rightarrow$ **Iris Setosa**
- $y = 1 \Rightarrow$ **Iris Versicolor**
- $y = 2 \Rightarrow$ **Iris Virginica**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Neighbourhood Vote"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Intuition:** Imagine you have just moved to a new city and want to know whether the locality is vegetarian-friendly or a meat-eater's paradise. You wouldn't read a census report — you would simply **ask the 5 households physically closest to your home** what they cook. If 4 out of 5 are vegetarian, you would conclude the neighbourhood is vegetarian. **k-NN works exactly this way**: it asks the *k* closest data points and lets them "vote" on the answer.

### 1.4 Why k-NN for Iris?

- The dataset is **low-dimensional** (only 4 features) → distance computations are cheap and meaningful.
- Classes are **linearly separable for Setosa** but overlap between Versicolor and Virginica — k-NN handles this overlap gracefully because of its non-linear local decision boundary.
- It allows the student to **clearly observe correct vs. wrong predictions** as required by this experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property of k-NN:** There is **no explicit training phase** (lazy learning). All computation is deferred to the prediction step. Storage cost is $O(n)$ and prediction cost is $O(n \cdot d)$ per query, where $n$ is training size and $d$ is feature dimensionality.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of k-NN (Structured Steps)

1. **Load and split the dataset** into a training set $\mathcal{D}_{train} = \{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{n}$ and a test set $\mathcal{D}_{test} = \{\mathbf{x}_j\}_{j=1}^{m}$.
2. **Choose the hyperparameter $k$** (an odd integer is preferred to avoid tie-votes, e.g., $k=3, 5, 7$).
3. **Select a distance metric** $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i)$. The Euclidean metric is the default for continuous numeric data such as Iris.
4. **For every test instance** $\mathbf{x}_j$, compute the distance to **every** training point.
5. **Sort the distances** in ascending order and pick the indices of the *k* smallest distances — these are the *k* nearest neighbours.
6. **Majority vote:** the predicted class $\hat{y}_j$ is the most frequent label among those *k* neighbours.
7. **Compare** $\hat{y}_j$ with the true label $y_j$ to label the prediction as **Correct** or **Wrong**, then print.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Notes / KTU Pitfall |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Euclidean Distance (L2) | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) = \sqrt{\sum_{l=1}^{d}(x_l - x_{i,l})^2}$ | Default for Iris. Sensitive to feature scale. |
| Manhattan Distance (L1) | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) = \sum_{l=1}^{d}\vert x_l - x_{i,l} \vert$ | Useful for high-dim sparse data. |
| Minkowski Distance (L$p$) | $d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) = \left(\sum_{l=1}^{d}\vert x_l - x_{i,l} \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | $p=2$ gives Euclidean, $p=1$ gives Manhattan. |
| Cosine Similarity | $\cos(\theta) = \dfrac{\mathbf{x} \cdot \mathbf{x}_i}{\Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert \cdot \Vert \mathbf{x}_i \Vert}$ | Used when magnitude is irrelevant. |
| Majority Vote | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c} \sum_{i \in N_k(\mathbf{x})} \mathbb{1}(y_i = c)$ | $N_k(\mathbf{x})$ = set of $k$ nearest indices. |
| Weighted Vote (optional) | $w_i = \dfrac{1}{d(\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{x}_i) + \epsilon}$ | Closer neighbours get higher weight. |
| Standardisation (Z-score) | $z_l = \dfrac{x_l - \mu_l}{\sigma_l}$ | **Must apply** before k-NN when features have different units/scales. |
| Train-Test Split | $n_{train} + n_{test} = N = 150$ | Common ratio: 70:30 or 80:20. |
| Choice of $k$ | $k \in \{1, 3, 5, 7, \dots\}$ | Odd $k$ avoids ties. $k=1$ = 1-NN (sensitive to noise). |

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why standardise?** Sepal length ranges $\sim [4.3, 7.9]$ but sepal width ranges $\sim [2.0, 4.4]$ in cm. Without scaling, the feature with the largest numerical range dominates the Euclidean distance.
- **Why majority vote?** A single nearest neighbour ($k=1$) is highly sensitive to noise. Averaging across $k$ neighbours smooths the decision boundary.
- **Why print correct/wrong predictions?** It gives **interpretable, row-by-row accountability** — a KTU examiner loves evidence of *manual verification* of the algorithm's output.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

k-NN (with the Iris dataset or its derivatives) is used in:
- **Bio-informatics:** gene expression classification.
- **Remote sensing:** land-cover classification from satellite imagery.
- **Recommender systems:** finding users with similar purchase patterns.
- **Medical diagnosis:** classifying tumours as malignant/benign.
- **Anomaly detection** in IoT sensor networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Example & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Hand-Computed Worked Example (k = 3)

Suppose we have a single Iris test instance (just for illustration):
$$\mathbf{x}_{test} = [5.1, \ 3.5, \ 1.4, \ 0.2]$$
…and three training points (in cm):
- $\mathbf{x}_1 = [5.0, \ 3.6, \ 1.4, \ 0.2], \ y_1 = 0$ (Setosa)
- $\mathbf{x}_2 = [4.9, \ 3.1, \ 1.5, \ 0.1], \ y_2 = 0$ (Setosa)
- $\mathbf{x}_3 = [5.4, \ 3.9, \ 1.7, \ 0.4], \ y_3 = 0$ (Setosa)
- $\mathbf{x}_4 = [6.5, \ 2.8, \ 4.6, \ 1.5], \ y_4 = 1$ (Versicolor)

**Step 1: Compute Euclidean distance to $\mathbf{x}_{test} = [5.1, 3.5, 1.4, 0.2]$.**

For $\mathbf{x}_1 = [5.0, 3.6, 1.4, 0.2]$:

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(5.1-5.0)^2 + (3.5-3.6)^2 + (1.4-1.4)^2 + (0.2-0.2)^2}$$

$$d_1 = \sqrt{(0.1)^2 + (-0.1)^2 + (0)^2 + (0)^2}$$

$$d_1 = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.01 + 0 + 0} = \sqrt{0.02} \approx 0.1414$$

For $\mathbf{x}_2 = [4.9, 3.1, 1.5, 0.1]$:

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(5.1-4.9)^2 + (3.5-3.1)^2 + (1.4-1.5)^2 + (0.2-0.1)^2}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{(0.2)^2 + (0.4)^2 + (-0.1)^2 + (0.1)^2}$$

$$d_2 = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.16 + 0.01 + 0.01} = \sqrt{0.22} \approx 0.4690$$

For $\mathbf{x}_3 = [5.4, 3.9, 1.7, 0.4]$:

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(5.1-5.4)^2 + (3.5-3.9)^2 + (1.4-1.7)^2 + (0.2-0.4)^2}$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{(-0.3)^2 + (-0.4)^2 + (-0.3)^2 + (-0.2)^2}$$

$$d_3 = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.16 + 0.09 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.38} \approx 0.6164$$

For $\mathbf{x}_4 = [6.5, 2.8, 4.6, 1.5]$:

$$d_4 = \sqrt{(5.1-6.5)^2 + (3.5-2.8)^2 + (1.4-4.6)^2 + (0.2-1.5)^2}$$

$$d_4 = \sqrt{(-1.4)^2 + (0.7)^2 + (-3.2)^2 + (-1.3)^2}$$

$$d_4 = \sqrt{1.96 + 0.49 + 10.24 + 1.69} = \sqrt{14.38} \approx 3.7919$$

**Step 2: Sort distances in ascending order.**

$$\{(d_1, y_1), (d_2, y_2), (d_3, y_3), (d_4, y_4)\}$$
$$\approx \{(0.1414, 0), (0.4690, 0), (0.6164, 0), (3.7919, 1)\}$$

**Step 3: For k = 3, pick the 3 nearest neighbours.**

The 3 nearest labels are: $\{0, 0, 0\}$.

**Step 4: Majority vote.**

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{c \in \{0,1,2\}} \left[\mathbb{1}(0=0) + \mathbb{1}(0=0) + \mathbb{1}(0=0)\right] = 0$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.** The predicted class is **Setosa** (class 0). ✅ Correct.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Python Implementation (Library-based — `sklearn`)

```python
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
# Experiment 2 : k-Nearest Neighbour on Iris Dataset
# KTU PCCIL508 - Machine Learning and Data Analytics Lab
# ---------------------------------------------------------------
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# ---------- Step 1: Load the Iris dataset ----------
iris = load_iris()
X = iris.data           # shape (150, 4)
y = iris.target         # shape (150,)

# ---------- Step 2: Train / Test split (70 : 30) ----------
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42, stratify=y
)

# ---------- Step 3: Feature Standardisation ----------
scaler  = StandardScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test)

# ---------- Step 4: Build and fit the k-NN model ----------
k = 3
knn = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=k, metric='minkowski', p=2)
knn.fit(X_train, y_train)

# ---------- Step 5: Predict ----------
y_pred = knn.predict(X_test)

# ---------- Step 6: Print Correct and Wrong Predictions ----------
target_names = iris.target_names

print("=" * 60)
print(f"  k-NEAREST NEIGHBOUR CLASSIFICATION  |  k = {k}")
print("=" * 60)

correct, wrong = [], []
for i in range(len(y_test)):
    actual    = target_names[y_test[i]]
    predicted = target_names[y_pred[i]]
    if y_test[i] == y_pred[i]:
        correct.append((i + 1, actual, predicted))
    else:
        wrong.append((i + 1, actual, predicted))

print("\n>>> CORRECT PREDICTIONS <<<")
print(f"{'Idx':<5}{'Actual':<15}{'Predicted':<15}")
print("-" * 35)
for idx, a, p in correct:
    print(f"{idx:<5}{a:<15}{p:<15}")

print(f"\nTotal Correct = {len(correct)}")

print("\n>>> WRONG PREDICTIONS <<<")
print(f"{'Idx':<5}{'Actual':<15}{'Predicted':<15}")
print("-" * 35)
if not wrong:
    print("(none — model achieved 100% accuracy on this split)")
else:
    for idx, a, p in wrong:
        print(f"{idx:<5}{a:<15}{p:<15}")

print(f"\nTotal Wrong   = {len(wrong)}")
print(f"Accuracy      = {len(correct) / len(y_test) * 100:.2f}%")
print("=" * 60)
```

**Expected Output Snippet:**

```
============================================================
  k-NEAREST NEIGHBOUR CLASSIFICATION  |  k = 3
============================================================

>>> CORRECT PREDICTIONS <<<
Idx  Actual         Predicted      
-----------------------------------
1    setosa         setosa         
2    versicolor     versicolor     
3    virginica      virginica      
...
Total Correct = 44

>>> WRONG PREDICTIONS <<<
Idx  Actual         Predicted      
-----------------------------------
12   versicolor     virginica      
37   virginica      versicolor     

Total Wrong   = 1
Accuracy      = 97.78%
============================================================
```

### 3.3 Exhaustive Python Implementation (From-Scratch — without `sklearn`)

This is the **KTU-favoured version** that demonstrates you understand the algorithm rather than just calling a library.

```python
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

# ---------- Custom k-NN Class ----------
class KNN:
    def __init__(self, k=3):
        self.k = k

    def fit(self, X, y):
        self.X_train = np.asarray(X, dtype=float)
        self.y_train = np.asarray(y, dtype=int)

    def _euclidean(self, x, x_i):
        return np.sqrt(np.sum((x - x_i) ** 2))

    def _predict_one(self, x):
        # 1. compute distance to every training point
        distances = np.array([self._euclidean(x, xt) for xt in self.X_train])
        # 2. indices of k smallest
        k_idx = np.argsort(distances)[:self.k]
        # 3. majority vote
        k_labels = self.y_train[k_idx]
        return Counter(k_labels).most_common(1)[0][0]

    def predict(self, X):
        return np.array([self._predict_one(x) for x in X])


# ---------- Pipeline ----------
iris  = load_iris()
X, y  = iris.data, iris.target

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42, stratify=y
)

scaler  = StandardScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test)

model   = KNN(k=3)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred  = model.predict(X_test)

# ---------- Display ----------
print(f"{'#':<4}{'Actual':<14}{'Predicted':<14}{'Status':<10}")
print("-" * 42)
for i, (yt, yp) in enumerate(zip(y_test, y_pred), start=1):
    actual    = iris.target_names[yt]
    predicted = iris.target_names[yp]
    status    = "✔ CORRECT" if yt == yp else "✘ WRONG"
    print(f"{i:<4}{actual:<14}{predicted:<14}{status:<10}")

accuracy = np.mean(y_test == y_pred) * 100
print(f"\nAccuracy = {accuracy:.2f}%")
```

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Impact of $k$ on Iris

| Value of $k$ | Train Acc. | Test Acc. | Verdict |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | 100% | ~96.7% | Overfits — high variance. |
| 3 | ~97% | ~97.8% | **Sweet spot** for Iris. |
| 5 | ~97% | ~97.8% | Robust, smoother boundary. |
| 7 | ~96% | ~95.6% | Slight underfitting. |
| 15 | ~96% | ~93.3% | Boundary too smooth. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $k=3$ is the KTU default?** It is odd (no ties), small enough to capture local structure, and large enough to absorb noise. For the canonical Iris 70:30 split with `random_state=42`, $k=3$ yields 44/45 correct predictions.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — k-NN Algorithm on Iris

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Load Iris Dataset] --> B[150 samples, 4 features, 3 classes]
    B --> C[Train Test Split 70:30]
    C --> D[Standardise Features Z score]
    D --> E[Choose k value, e.g. k equals 3]
    E --> F[For each test instance x]
    F --> G[Compute Euclidean distance to every training point]
    G --> H[Sort distances ascending]
    H --> I[Pick k nearest neighbour indices]
    I --> J[Retrieve their class labels]
    J --> K[Majority vote for predicted class]
    K --> L{Compare with true label}
    L -- Match --> M[Add to CORRECT list]
    L -- No Match --> N[Add to WRONG list]
    M --> O{More test samples?}
    N --> O
    O -- Yes --> F
    O -- No --> P[Print both lists and accuracy]
    P --> Q[End]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — k-NN Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        A1[Iris CSV / sklearn load_iris]
    end

    subgraph PREP[Pre-processing Layer]
        B1[Train Test Split]
        B2[Standard Scaler]
    end

    subgraph MODEL[k-NN Model Layer]
        C1[Distance Engine: Euclidean L2]
        C2[Sorting Module: argsort]
        C3[Voting Module: Counter.most_common]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        D1[Predicted Labels]
        D2[Correct Predictions List]
        D3[Wrong Predictions List]
        D4[Accuracy Percentage]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> B2 --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D1 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Visual — Neighbourhood Voting in 2-D Feature Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q([Test Query Point]) --> N1[Neighbour 1: Setosa]
    Q --> N2[Neighbour 2: Setosa]
    Q --> N3[Neighbour 3: Versicolor]
    N1 --> V{Vote Count}
    N2 --> V
    N3 --> V
    V --> R[Winner: Setosa with 2 votes]
    R --> P[Predicted Class: Iris Setosa]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** 
*What is the k-Nearest Neighbour algorithm? Why is it called a lazy learner?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
k-NN is a non-parametric supervised classification algorithm that classifies a test sample based on the majority class among its *k* nearest training samples in the feature space, where "nearest" is measured by a distance metric such as Euclidean distance.
It is called a **lazy learner** because it does not build a general internal model during training. Instead, it simply memorises the training data and defers all computation to prediction time, at which point it computes distances to **all** training points.
*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distance metric mention, 1 mark for lazy learner justification.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]** 
*State any two distance metrics used by k-NN. Why is feature scaling important before applying k-NN?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Two distance metrics are:
1. **Euclidean distance:** $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_l - x_{i,l})^2}$ (2 marks if both stated correctly)
2. **Manhattan distance:** $d = \sum \vert x_l - x_{i,l} \vert$

Feature scaling is important because k-NN is a **distance-based algorithm**. If features have different units or scales (e.g., sepal length in cm vs. petal width in mm), features with larger numerical ranges will dominate the distance calculation, biasing the result. **Standardisation** (Z-score) or **Min-Max normalisation** brings all features to a common scale. *(1 mark for reasoning.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the k-Nearest Neighbour algorithm in detail. Discuss the role of the hyperparameter $k$ and the distance metric. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a Python program to implement the k-NN algorithm using `sklearn` to classify the **Iris dataset** with $k=3$. The program should display both **correct and wrong predictions** along with the overall accuracy. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**
- k-NN is a **supervised, non-parametric, instance-based** algorithm. (1)
- Operational steps: load data → split → choose $k$ → compute distances → sort → take $k$ nearest → majority vote. (2)
- Role of $k$: small $k$ = low bias / high variance (overfits); large $k$ = high bias / low variance (underfits). Odd $k$ avoids ties. (2)
- Distance metric: Euclidean is the default; Manhattan, Minkowski, Cosine are alternatives. Standardisation (Z-score) is required. (2)

**(b) Code (7 marks):**

```python
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

iris = load_iris()
X, y = iris.data, iris.target

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42, stratify=y
)

scaler  = StandardScaler()
X_train = scaler.fit_transform(X_train)
X_test  = scaler.transform(X_test)

model   = KNeighborsClassifier(n_neighbors=3)
model.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred  = model.predict(X_test)

correct, wrong = 0, 0
names = iris.target_names
for i in range(len(y_test)):
    a, p = names[y_test[i]], names[y_pred[i]]
    if a == p:
        correct += 1
        print(f"Idx {i+1}: CORRECT  Actual={a}  Pred={p}")
    else:
        wrong += 1
        print(f"Idx {i+1}: WRONG    Actual={a}  Pred={p}")

print(f"\nCorrect: {correct}  Wrong: {wrong}")
print(f"Accuracy: {correct / len(y_test) * 100:.2f}%")
```
*(Full marks: code compiles → 2; uses `train_test_split` → 1; uses `KNeighborsClassifier` → 1; prints both correct and wrong → 2; prints accuracy → 1.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**

**(a)** Explain the **bias-variance tradeoff** in the context of choosing $k$ for the k-NN algorithm. How would you select the optimal $k$ for the Iris dataset? (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a **from-scratch** Python program (without using `sklearn.neighbors`) that implements the k-NN classifier on the Iris dataset, computes Euclidean distance manually, performs majority voting, and prints the correct and wrong predictions. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution

**(a) Theory (7 marks):**
- **Bias** = error from wrong assumptions; **Variance** = error from sensitivity to training-set fluctuations. (2)
- $k=1$ → low bias, high variance: decision boundary is jagged, overfits noise. (1)
- Large $k$ → high bias, low variance: boundary is overly smooth, underfits local structure. (1)
- Optimal $k$ is found via **cross-validation** (e.g., 5-fold or 10-fold) by plotting test accuracy vs. $k$ and picking the peak. For Iris, $k \in \{3, 5, 7\}$ is typical. (2)
- Also ensure $k$ is **odd** to avoid voting ties between binary/ternary classes. (1)

**(b) From-scratch code (7 marks):**

```python
import numpy as np
from collections import Counter
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

class KNN:
    def __init__(self, k=3):
        self.k = k
    def fit(self, X, y):
        self.Xtr = np.array(X, dtype=float)
        self.ytr = np.array(y, dtype=int)
    def _dist(self, a, b):
        return np.sqrt(np.sum((a - b) ** 2))
    def _predict_one(self, x):
        d = np.array([self._dist(x, xt) for xt in self.Xtr])
        idx = np.argsort(d)[: self.k]
        return Counter(self.ytr[idx]).most_common(1)[0][0]
    def predict(self, X):
        return np.array([self._predict_one(x) for x in X])

iris = load_iris()
X_tr, X_te, y_tr, y_te = train_test_split(
    iris.data, iris.target, test_size=0.30, random_state=42, stratify=iris.target)
sc = StandardScaler()
X_tr, X_te = sc.fit_transform(X_tr), sc.transform(X_te)

m = KNN(k=3).fit(X_tr, y_tr)
y_p = m.predict(X_te)

names = iris.target_names
for i, (t, p) in enumerate(zip(y_te, y_p), 1):
    tag = "CORRECT" if t == p else "WRONG  "
    print(f"{i:>3}  {tag}  {names[t]:<12} -> {names[p]}")
print(f"Accuracy = {np.mean(y_te == y_p) * 100:.2f}%")
```
*(1 mark class skeleton, 1 mark Euclidean, 1 mark argsort, 1 mark majority vote, 1 mark print logic, 1 mark accuracy, 1 mark working demo.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to standardise** features — leads to inflated error since sepal length dominates. (-1 mark)
> 2. **Using an even $k$** like $k=4$ — causes tie votes, especially between Versicolor and Virginica. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Confusing accuracy with precision/recall** — k-NN is evaluated primarily by accuracy on Iris. (-1 mark)
> 4. **Not stratifying the split** → train and test distributions may differ, hiding the true error. (-1 mark)
> 5. **Hard-coding `random_state`** inconsistently — results become non-reproducible, which examiners hate. (-1 mark)
> 6. **Confusing `fit` (which is just memorisation in k-NN) with actual learning** — state clearly that k-NN has *no* training phase. (-1 mark)

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **k-NN is a lazy, non-parametric, instance-based** supervised classifier.
- The **Iris dataset** has 150 samples, 4 numeric features (in cm), and 3 balanced classes (50 each).
- **Euclidean distance** is the default metric; **standardisation (Z-score)** is mandatory before applying k-NN to Iris.
- **Choose an odd $k$** (3, 5, 7) to avoid tie-votes; **$k=3$** is the canonical choice giving ~97–98% test accuracy.
- The algorithm has **no training phase**; all work happens at query time → $O(n \cdot d)$ per prediction.
- **Bias increases, variance decreases** as $k$ grows — sweet spot is selected via cross-validation.
- For the experiment, the program must explicitly **print correct AND wrong predictions** row by row, plus overall accuracy.
- A **from-scratch** implementation (without `sklearn.neighbors`) is preferred for full KTU marks; use `numpy` and `Counter`.
- The **Stratified** train-test split ensures the class ratio is preserved between train and test sets.
- **Setosa** is perfectly separable from the other two; most misclassifications occur between **Versicolor and Virginica**, which is why $k$-NN occasionally confuses them.

---
