topics.title: Implement the non-parametric Locally Weighted Regression algorithm in order to fit data points. Select appropriate data set for your experiment and draw graphs.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL508 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement the non-parametric Locally Weighted Regression algorithm in order to fit data points. Select appropriate data set for your experiment and draw graphs.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Locally Weighted Regression (LWR)**, also known as **Loess** (in its smoothened form) or **Locally Weighted Scatterplot Smoothing (LOWESS)**, is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm used primarily for regression. Unlike global regression models (e.g., Ordinary Least Squares) that learn a single set of parameters valid across the entire input space, LWR constructs a *local* linear model around each query point $\mathbf{x}_q$ by assigning a weight $w^{(i)}$ to every training instance $\mathbf{x}^{(i)}$ based on its proximity to $\mathbf{x}_q$. The parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ are then recomputed at every prediction step, making the model **memory-based** and **lazy**.

Mathematically, given a query point $\mathbf{x}_q$, LWR solves:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W}(\mathbf{x}_q) \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W}(\mathbf{x}_q) \mathbf{y}$$

where $\mathbf{W}(\mathbf{x}_q)$ is a diagonal matrix of weights derived from a kernel function centered at $\mathbf{x}_q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** LWR is the canonical example of a *non-parametric* regression algorithm in the ML syllabus. The key distinguishing property is that the model **does not retain learned parameters** after training — predictions are computed *on the fly* using the entire training set at query time.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **temperature predictor** in Kerala. Instead of learning a single equation for the whole state, you reason as follows:

- A person asks you: *"What is the likely temperature in **Kozhikode** today?"*
- You do **not** use a single rule for all of Kerala. You instead collect temperature readings from places **close to Kozhikode** (e.g., Kannur, Malappuram, Wayanad) and assign **higher importance** to readings from Kozhikode itself, slightly less to Malappuram, and almost zero to Thiruvananthapuram (because it is geographically and climatologically distant).
- You then fit a *local* straight line through this weighted subset and return the predicted temperature at Kozhikode.
- If another person asks about **Thiruvananthapuram**, you repeat the entire process — different weights, different local line.

This is exactly what LWR does. The "closeness" is quantified by a **kernel function** (typically a Gaussian), and the bandwidth parameter $\tau$ controls how far the locality extends.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical / Heuristic Constants Used in LWR:**
> - **Bandwidth parameter ($\tau$ or $\sigma$):** Controls the width of the local neighborhood. Small $\tau$ → high variance, low bias (wobbly curve). Large $\tau$ → low variance, high bias (smooth curve approaching global OLS).
> - **Kernel function (commonly Gaussian):** $w^{(i)} = \exp\left(-\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{x}_q \Vert^2}{2\tau^2}\right)$
> - **Cutoff:** For numerical efficiency, weights below a threshold (e.g., $10^{-6}$) are typically set to zero.

---

## 1.3 Why LWR is Non-Parametric

A model is called **non-parametric** when the number of effective parameters grows with the training set size, rather than being fixed a priori. LWR satisfies this because:

| Property | Parametric (OLS) | Non-Parametric (LWR) |
|---|---|---|
| Parameters after training | Fixed $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ | None retained |
| Memory required post-training | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(nd)$ (must store $X, y$) |
| Inference cost | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ per query | $\mathcal{O}(n d^2 + d^3)$ per query |
| Global vs Local | Global fit | Local fit per query |
| Hyperparameter | None (or $\lambda$ for ridge) | Bandwidth $\tau$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Term to Remember:** "Memory-based learning" and "lazy learning" are **synonyms** for the non-parametric nature of LWR. Use these exact phrases in your KTU viva.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Pipeline of LWR

LWR is best understood as a **five-stage pipeline** executed at every prediction step:

1. **Accept the query point** $\mathbf{x}_q \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
2. **Compute weights** $w^{(i)}$ for all $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$ training points using a kernel function centered at $\mathbf{x}_q$.
3. **Form the diagonal weight matrix** $\mathbf{W}(\mathbf{x}_q) = \mathrm{diag}\left(w^{(1)}, w^{(2)}, \dots, w^{(n)}\right)$.
4. **Solve the weighted normal equation** for the local parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_q)$.
5. **Return the prediction** $\hat{y}_q = \boldsymbol{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_q)^T \mathbf{x}_q$.

### Step 1 & 2 — Weight Computation (The Heart of LWR)

The most common weight function is the **Gaussian kernel**, motivated by the desire to penalize distant points exponentially:

$$w^{(i)} = \exp\left(-\frac{\left(x^{(i)} - x_q\right)^2}{2\tau^2}\right)$$

For multivariate input, the squared Euclidean distance is used:

$$w^{(i)} = \exp\left(-\frac{\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{x}_q \Vert_2^2}{2\tau^2}\right)$$

Alternative kernels include the **Tricube** (used in LOWESS), **Epanechnikov**, and **Rectangular (uniform)** kernels. The Gaussian is preferred in KTU lab experiments for its smooth, differentiable form.

### Step 3 — Weight Matrix

The weight matrix is diagonal:

$$\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} w^{(1)} & 0 & \cdots & 0 \\ 0 & w^{(2)} & \cdots & 0 \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & 0 & \cdots & w^{(n)} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$$

### Step 4 — Weighted Normal Equation

Unlike OLS which minimizes $\sum_i (h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$, LWR minimizes a **weighted** sum-of-squared-errors:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w^{(i)} \left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2 = (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T \mathbf{W} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

Setting $\nabla_\theta J = 0$ yields the **Weighted Normal Equation**:

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$$

### Step 5 — Prediction

$$\hat{y}_q = \boldsymbol{\theta}(\mathbf{x}_q)^T \mathbf{x}_q$$

Note that the prediction is the value of the locally fitted line **at the query point itself**.

---

## 2.2 The Bias-Variance Tradeoff in $\tau$

The bandwidth $\tau$ is the **single most important hyperparameter** in LWR. Its effect:

| Bandwidth $\tau$ | Bias | Variance | Curve Appearance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Very small (e.g., $0.01$) | Low | High | Wobbly, passes through all points (overfitting) |
| Moderate (e.g., $0.1$–$0.3$) | Balanced | Balanced | Smooth, captures trends |
| Very large (e.g., $\to \infty$) | High | Low | Approaches global OLS line (underfitting) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Viva Trap:** Students often confuse LWR with **K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)**. The key difference is that LWR produces a *continuous, locally linear* prediction, while KNN produces a *discrete, locally constant* (averaged) prediction. LWR is to linear regression what KNN is to a lookup table.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Number of training samples | Integer | dimensionless |
| $d$ | Dimensionality of input | Integer | dimensionless |
| $\mathbf{x}_q$ | Query point (scalar or vector) | $\in \mathbb{R}^d$ | feature units |
| $\tau$ | Bandwidth (kernel width) | Hyperparameter, $\tau > 0$ | same as $x$ |
| $w^{(i)}$ | Weight of training point $i$ | $\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{x}_q \Vert^2}{2\tau^2}\right)$ | dimensionless, $\in (0,1]$ |
| $\mathbf{W}$ | Diagonal weight matrix | $\mathrm{diag}(w^{(1)},\dots,w^{(n)})$ | dimensionless |
| $J(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ | Weighted SSE loss | $\sum_i w^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$ | $(y\text{-units})^2$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ | Local parameter vector | $\left(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}\right)^{-1}\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{y}$ | depends on $y$ |
| $\hat{y}_q$ | Predicted output at $\mathbf{x}_q$ | $\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}_q$ | same as $y$ |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LWR (and its smoothed sibling LOESS) is used extensively in:

- **Time-series forecasting** for non-stationary signals (e.g., sensor drift in IoT devices).
- **Control systems** where the plant dynamics are locally linear but globally nonlinear (gain scheduling).
- **Signal processing** for smoothing noisy experimental data in materials testing.
- **Robotics** for learning inverse dynamics from demonstrations (a precursor to Gaussian Process Regression).
- **Econometrics** for visualizing trends in noisy financial data (Cleveland's original 1979 application).

> [!NOTE]
> LWR can be viewed as a **simplified precursor to Gaussian Process Regression (GPR)**. GPR replaces the pointwise weight matrix with a covariance kernel over the entire input space, giving Bayesian uncertainty estimates. KTU students should be aware of this connection for higher-semester courses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Weighted Normal Equation

We begin with the weighted cost function:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w^{(i)} \left( \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)} \right)^2$$

### Step 1 — Vectorize the cost

Define the design matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times (d+1)}$ (with a column of ones for the bias), target vector $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, and weight matrix $\mathbf{W} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T \mathbf{W} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

**Logic:** Each term $\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2$ is the squared error for the $i$-th sample; multiplying by $w^{(i)}$ scales it by its proximity. The matrix form compactly represents the entire weighted sum.

### Step 2 — Expand the quadratic form

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T - \mathbf{y}^T\right) \mathbf{W} \left(\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y}\right)$$

$$= \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} - \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} + \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$$

**Logic:** $(\mathbf{AB})^T = \mathbf{B}^T \mathbf{A}^T$ was applied to the first transpose, and the scalar was expanded into a sum of four quadratic/scalar products.

### Step 3 — Note that the cross terms are scalar (and therefore equal to their own transpose)

The two middle terms are scalars, so:

$$\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} = \left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}\right)^T = \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{W}^T \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta}$$

Since $\mathbf{W}$ is symmetric (diagonal) and $n \times n$, this simplifies to:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\, \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$$

**Logic:** Because the cross term is a $1 \times 1$ scalar, $\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{a}^T \mathbf{b})^T = \mathbf{b}^T \mathbf{a}$. This lets us combine the two cross terms into $-2\boldsymbol{\theta}^T\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{y}$.

### Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $\boldsymbol{\theta}$

Using the standard matrix calculus identities:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \left( \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{A} \boldsymbol{\theta} \right) = 2\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\theta} \quad \text{(when } \mathbf{A} \text{ is symmetric)}$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \left( \mathbf{b}^T \boldsymbol{\theta} \right) = \mathbf{b}$$

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial \boldsymbol{\theta}} \left( \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} \right) = \mathbf{0} \quad \text{(no dependence on } \boldsymbol{\theta}\text{)}$$

Therefore:

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$$

### Step 5 — Set the gradient to zero

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \mathbf{0}$$

$$2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2 \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{0}$$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} = \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$$

### Step 6 — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ (assuming $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}$ is invertible)

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta} = \left( \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}}$$

**Logic:** This is the **closed-form Weighted Least Squares (WLS)** solution. It is mathematically identical to ridge regression in form, except $\mathbf{W}$ is a sample-weighting matrix, not a regularizer on $\boldsymbol{\theta}$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem Setup:** Consider 3 training points with 1-D input (with bias term $x_0 = 1$):

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{x}_q = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \tau = 1.0$$

### Step A — Compute weights (Gaussian kernel)

$$w^{(1)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(1-2)^2}{2(1)^2}\right) = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

$$w^{(2)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(3-2)^2}{2(1)^2}\right) = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065$$

$$w^{(3)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(5-2)^2}{2(1)^2}\right) = e^{-4.5} \approx 0.0111$$

**Logic:** Distance from $x_q = 2$: points 1 and 3 are 1 unit away (equal weight); point 5 is 3 units away (much smaller weight).

### Step B — Construct $\mathbf{W}$

$$\mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6065 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.6065 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0.0111 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step C — Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X}$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6065 & 0.6065 & 0.0111 \\ 0.6065 & 1.8195 & 0.0555 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6065 & 0.6065 & 0.0111 \\ 0.6065 & 1.8195 & 0.0555 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 3 \\ 1 & 5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing entry-by-entry:

- $(1,1)$: $0.6065(1) + 0.6065(1) + 0.0111(1) = 1.2241$
- $(1,2)$: $0.6065(1) + 0.6065(3) + 0.0111(5) = 2.4815$
- $(2,1)$: $0.6065(1) + 1.8195(1) + 0.0555(1) = 2.4815$
- $(2,2)$: $0.6065(1) + 1.8195(3) + 0.0555(5) = 6.4900$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.2241 & 2.4815 \\ 2.4815 & 6.4900 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step D — Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6065 & 0.6065 & 0.0111 \\ 0.6065 & 1.8195 & 0.0555 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

- Row 1: $0.6065(2) + 0.6065(4) + 0.0111(1) = 3.6496$
- Row 2: $0.6065(2) + 1.8195(4) + 0.0555(1) = 8.5147$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.6496 \\ 8.5147 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step E — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$

Determinant: $\det = (1.2241)(6.4900) - (2.4815)^2 = 7.9444 - 6.1578 = 1.7866$

Inverse:

$$\left(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X}\right)^{-1} = \frac{1}{1.7866} \begin{bmatrix} 6.4900 & -2.4815 \\ -2.4815 & 1.2241 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.6326 & -1.3890 \\ -1.3890 & 0.6851 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.6326 & -1.3890 \\ -1.3890 & 0.6851 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3.6496 \\ 8.5147 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.6326(3.6496) - 1.3890(8.5147) \\ -1.3890(3.6496) + 0.6851(8.5147) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 13.2588 - 11.8269 \\ -5.0693 + 5.8334 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.4319 \\ 0.7641 \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step F — Predict

$$\hat{y}_q = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}_q = 1.4319(1) + 0.7641(2) = 2.9601$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The local model is $\hat{y} = 1.4319 + 0.7641 x$. At $x_q = 2$, the predicted value is $\hat{y}_q \approx 2.96$. For comparison, a global OLS fit on the same data would have given a different slope, ignoring local curvature.

---

## 3.3 Reference Python Implementation (Lab-Ready)

The following Python code is the **expected KTU lab implementation**. It is provided in a non-CS lab context specifically because this is a *Programming Lab* course (PCCIL508).

```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# ---------- 1. Generate Synthetic Non-Linear Dataset ----------
np.random.seed(42)
n = 100
X = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, n).reshape(-1, 1)
y = np.sin(X).ravel() + 0.3 * np.random.randn(n)

# ---------- 2. Locally Weighted Regression Function ----------
def lwr(x_query, X, y, tau=0.3):
    m = X.shape[0]
    # Compute weights using Gaussian kernel
    diffs = X - x_query
    weights = np.exp(-(diffs ** 2) / (2.0 * tau ** 2)).ravel()
    W = np.diag(weights)

    # Add bias column
    X_b = np.hstack([np.ones((m, 1)), X])

    # Closed-form weighted least squares
    XtWX = X_b.T @ W @ X_b
    XtWy = X_b.T @ W @ y
    theta = np.linalg.solve(XtWX, XtWy)

    # Predict at query point
    x_q_b = np.array([1.0, x_query[0]])
    return theta @ x_q_b

# ---------- 3. Predict over a Dense Grid ----------
X_test = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 300).reshape(-1, 1)
y_pred = np.array([lwr(x, X, y, tau=0.3) for x in X_test])

# ---------- 4. Plot ----------
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.scatter(X, y, s=20, c='steelblue', alpha=0.5, label='Training Data (Noisy Sine)')
plt.plot(X_test, np.sin(X_test), 'g--', lw=2, label='True Function (sin x)')
plt.plot(X_test, y_pred, 'r-', lw=2.5, label='LWR Fit (τ=0.3)')
plt.xlabel('x'); plt.ylabel('y')
plt.title('Locally Weighted Regression on Noisy Sine Data')
plt.legend(); plt.grid(alpha=0.3)
plt.show()
```

> [!TIP]
> **Experiment Variation:** Try changing $\tau$ to $\{0.05, 0.3, 1.0, 3.0\}$ and observe the bias-variance tradeoff visually. This is a frequently asked **observation** in the KTU lab record.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LWR Algorithm Flowchart (Query-Time Pipeline)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the operational pipeline of LWR **at prediction time** for a single query point.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([Start: Receive Query Point x_q]) --> B[Loop over all n training points]
    B --> C[Compute distance d_i = norm of x_i minus x_q]
    C --> D[Compute weight w_i = exp of minus d_i squared over 2 tau squared]
    D --> E{All n weights computed?}
    E -- No --> B
    E -- Yes --> F[Construct diagonal weight matrix W of size n by n]
    F --> G[Augment X with bias column: X_b]
    G --> H[Compute A = X_b transpose times W times X_b]
    H --> I[Compute b = X_b transpose times W times y]
    I --> J[Solve linear system A times theta = b for theta]
    J --> K[Form query vector x_q_b with leading 1]
    K --> L[Predict y_hat = theta transpose times x_q_b]
    L --> M([Return y_hat to caller])
    M --> N{More queries pending?}
    N -- Yes --> A
    N -- No --> O([End])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two decision diamonds (E and N) emphasize that LWR must re-execute the entire pipeline for **every new query point**. There is no training phase — only a "fitting-on-demand" phase. This is the defining characteristic of a lazy learner.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following block diagram maps the functional interactions between the modules of the LWR system, useful for the KTU lab record's "block diagram" section.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        X[Training Features X]
        Y[Training Targets y]
        XQ[Query Point x_q]
    end

    subgraph KERNEL[Kernel Weight Module]
        D[Distance Calculator]
        W[Weight Generator: Gaussian]
        WM[Diagonal Weight Matrix Builder]
    end

    subgraph SOLVER[Weighted LS Solver]
        XTX[X transpose W X]
        XTY[X transpose W y]
        INV[Matrix Inverse]
        THETA[Local Parameter Vector theta]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Prediction Output]
        PRED[Predicted Value y_hat]
        PLOT[Matplotlib Visualizer]
    end

    X --> D
    XQ --> D
    D --> W --> WM
    X --> XTX
    WM --> XTX
    Y --> XTY
    WM --> XTY
    XTX --> INV
    INV --> THETA
    XTY --> THETA
    THETA --> PRED
    X --> PLOT
    Y --> PLOT
    PRED --> PLOT
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for the lab record:** The KTU lab manual often requires a "block diagram of the algorithm." A block diagram distinguishes the **kernel module** (which is query-dependent) from the **solver module** (which is a generic linear-algebra routine). This separation is also what makes LWR a natural template for **Gaussian Process Regression** in advanced courses.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Diagram: LWR vs Global OLS

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GLOBAL[Global OLS — Single Fit]
        GO1[All training data] --> GO2[One theta = XtX inverse Xty]
        GO2 --> GO3[Single straight line for entire domain]
    end

    subgraph LOCAL[LWR — Query-Dependent Fits]
        LO1[All training data] --> LO2[Query x_q]
        LO2 --> LO3[Compute weights W of x_q]
        LO3 --> LO4[theta of x_q = XtWX inverse XtWy]
        LO4 --> LO5[Local line at x_q]
    end

    GLOBAL --> COMPARE{Compare Fits}
    LOCAL --> COMPARE
    COMPARE --> RESULT[Global = 1 fit, Local = n_query fits. LWR can curve; OLS cannot.]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Locally Weighted Regression. Why is it classified as a non-parametric algorithm?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Locally Weighted Regression (LWR) is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm that fits a separate linear regression model for each query point by assigning weights to training instances based on their proximity to that query.
>
> **Non-parametric justification (3 key points):**
>
> 1. **No fixed parameter set is learned** during a "training phase." The model retains the entire training dataset and computes parameters on-demand.
> 2. **The effective number of parameters grows with the dataset size** $n$, violating the parametric assumption of a fixed-dimensional parameter vector.
> 3. **Predictions at each query point** are produced by solving a fresh weighted normal equation $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$, where the weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ depends on the query.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the three justification points.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Explain the role of the bandwidth parameter $\tau$ in Locally Weighted Regression. What happens when $\tau$ is very small or very large?**

**Model Answer:**

> The bandwidth parameter $\tau$ controls the **width of the kernel** and therefore the size of the local neighborhood that influences each prediction.
>
> - **Very small $\tau$ (e.g., 0.01):** Only training points extremely close to the query receive appreciable weight. The fit becomes **highly wobbly** and tends to pass through every point — high variance, low bias (**overfitting**).
> - **Very large $\tau$ (e.g., 10 or $\to \infty$):** All training points receive nearly equal weights, the local model approaches the **global OLS solution** — low variance, high bias (**underfitting**).
> - **Moderate $\tau$ (tuned by cross-validation):** Achieves a balance and produces a smooth, accurate non-linear fit.
>
> *(3 marks: 1 for defining $\tau$, 1 for small-$\tau$ consequence, 1 for large-$\tau$ consequence.)*

---

## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the closed-form solution for the parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ of Locally Weighted Regression starting from the weighted cost function. Clearly state the role of the weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following 4 training points in 1-D: $X = \{0, 1, 2, 4\}$, $Y = \{1, 2, 4, 3\}$. For a query point $x_q = 1.5$ and bandwidth $\tau = 1.0$, compute the prediction $\hat{y}_q$ using a Gaussian kernel. Show all matrix computations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Start with the weighted cost:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w^{(i)}\left(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\right)^2$$

**Step 1 — Matrix form:** With design matrix $\mathbf{X}$, target $\mathbf{y}$, and diagonal weight matrix $\mathbf{W} = \mathrm{diag}(w^{(1)}, \dots, w^{(n)})$:

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})^T \mathbf{W} (\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y})$$

**Step 2 — Expand:** The diagonal $\mathbf{W}$ scales the residual vector $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\theta} - \mathbf{y}$ elementwise, so distant points contribute less to the loss.

$$J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero:**

$$\nabla_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} J = 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X} \boldsymbol{\theta} - 2\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 4 — Solve:**

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\theta} = \left(\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X}\right)^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}}$$

**Role of $\mathbf{W}$:** $\mathbf{W}$ is a **diagonal matrix whose entries are the kernel-derived weights** for each training point relative to the current query. It quantifies the relative influence of each sample in the local fit. It is recomputed for every new query, which is the key feature that makes LWR query-adaptive.

*(7 marks: 2 for cost formulation, 2 for differentiation, 1 for setting gradient to zero, 1 for the closed-form solution, 1 for explaining $\mathbf{W}$.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Form the augmented design matrix** (with bias column):

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \\ 4 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Gaussian weights** with $\tau = 1.0$:

$$w^{(1)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(0 - 1.5)^2}{2}\right) = e^{-1.125} \approx 0.3247$$

$$w^{(2)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(1 - 1.5)^2}{2}\right) = e^{-0.125} \approx 0.8825$$

$$w^{(3)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(2 - 1.5)^2}{2}\right) = e^{-0.125} \approx 0.8825$$

$$w^{(4)} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(4 - 1.5)^2}{2}\right) = e^{-3.125} \approx 0.0439$$

**Step 3 — Form $\mathbf{W}$:**

$$\mathbf{W} = \mathrm{diag}(0.3247,\ 0.8825,\ 0.8825,\ 0.0439)$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X}$:**

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3247 & 0.8825 & 0.8825 & 0.0439 \\ 0 & 0.8825 & 1.7650 & 0.1756 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W}\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3247+0.8825+0.8825+0.0439 & 0+0.8825+1.7650+0.1756 \\ 0+0.8825+1.7650+0.1756 & 0+0.8825+3.5300+0.7024 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W}\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1336 & 2.8231 \\ 2.8231 & 5.1149 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$:**

Row 1: $0.3247(1) + 0.8825(2) + 0.8825(4) + 0.0439(3) = 0.3247 + 1.7650 + 3.5300 + 0.1317 = 5.7514$

Row 2: $0(1) + 0.8825(2) + 1.7650(4) + 0.1756(3) = 0 + 1.7650 + 7.0600 + 0.5268 = 9.3518$

$$\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W}\mathbf{y} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.7514 \\ 9.3518 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$:**

Determinant: $\det = (2.1336)(5.1149) - (2.8231)^2 = 10.9132 - 7.9699 = 2.9433$

Inverse: $\dfrac{1}{2.9433}\begin{bmatrix} 5.1149 & -2.8231 \\ -2.8231 & 2.1336 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.7378 & -0.9592 \\ -0.9592 & 0.7249 \end{bmatrix}$

$$\boldsymbol{\theta} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.7378(5.7514) - 0.9592(9.3518) \\ -0.9592(5.7514) + 0.7249(9.3518) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 9.9951 - 8.9702 \\ -5.5167 + 6.7790 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0249 \\ 1.2623 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Predict at $x_q = 1.5$:**

$$\hat{y}_q = \theta_0 + \theta_1 \cdot x_q = 1.0249 + 1.2623(1.5) = 1.0249 + 1.8935 = \mathbf{2.9184}$$

*(7 marks: 1 for weights, 1 for $\mathbf{W}$, 2 for $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}$, 1 for $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{y}$, 1 for $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, 1 for final prediction.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Compare Locally Weighted Regression (LWR) with K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) and global Ordinary Least Squares (OLS). Construct a comparison table with at least 5 differentiating parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the effect of the bandwidth parameter $\tau$ on the bias-variance tradeoff. Using a noisy sine dataset $y = \sin(x) + \epsilon$ with $x \in [0, 2\pi]$, sketch (describe qualitatively) the LWR fit for (i) $\tau = 0.05$, (ii) $\tau = 0.5$, (iii) $\tau = 2.0$. Which choice is most appropriate and why? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | Global OLS | KNN | **LWR** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Learning type | Parametric | Non-parametric (instance-based) | **Non-parametric (instance-based)** |
| Parameters learned | Single $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ | None (stores all data) | **None (stores all data)** |
| Prediction rule | $\hat{y} = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}$ | Average of $k$ nearest $y$ | **Local weighted linear fit** |
| Effect of distant points | Equal influence | No influence (excluded) | **Exponentially decaying influence** |
| Output surface | Single global hyperplane | Step-like (piecewise constant) | **Smooth non-linear curve** |
| Hyperparameter | None | $k$ (number of neighbors) | **$\tau$ (bandwidth)** |
| Computational cost at inference | $\mathcal{O}(d)$ | $\mathcal{O}(nd)$ | **$\mathcal{O}(nd^2 + d^3)$** |
| Best suited for | Linear relationships | Discontinuous patterns | **Smooth non-linear patterns** |

*(7 marks: 1 per row, with weighting toward the most important 5.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Bias-Variance Tradeoff:** The Mean Squared Error of any predictor can be decomposed as $\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{Bias}^2 + \mathrm{Variance} + \sigma^2_{\text{noise}}$. As $\tau$ changes, these two components trade off:

- **Small $\tau$:** Only points very close to $x_q$ influence $\boldsymbol{\theta}$. The local fit is highly sensitive to noise in those few nearby points → **high variance**. However, the model is flexible enough to capture the true local trend → **low bias**.
- **Large $\tau$:** Many points influence $\boldsymbol{\theta}$, smoothing over local variation → **low variance**. But the model becomes too rigid to capture the curvature of $\sin(x)$ → **high bias**.

**Qualitative Sketches for the Noisy Sine Dataset:**

| $\tau$ value | LWR Fit Appearance | Bias | Variance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau = 0.05$ | Highly wobbly, jagged, passes through almost every noisy point; fails to recover the smooth sine wave | Low | **Very High** |
| $\tau = 0.5$ | Smooth curve that closely follows $\sin(x)$ while ignoring most of the noise | **Balanced** | **Balanced** |
| $\tau = 2.0$ | Almost a straight line; the curvature of the sine wave is washed out | **High** | Low |

**Most Appropriate Choice:** $\tau = 0.5$ is the best for a typical noisy sine dataset of this form. It provides the best **generalization** by capturing the underlying smooth trend without overfitting to noise. The optimal value is usually selected via **cross-validation** (e.g., leave-one-out CV on the training data) to minimize prediction error.

*(7 marks: 2 for tradeoff theory, 3 for the three $\tau$ sketches/descriptions, 2 for the selection justification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the bias column** $x_0 = 1$ in the design matrix. Without it, the local line is forced through the origin, which almost always gives wrong predictions. (–1 to –2 marks)
> 2. **Confusing $\tau^2$ with $\tau$** in the Gaussian kernel. The denominator is $2\tau^2$, not $2\tau$. (–1 mark)
> 3. **Solving the normal equation with the unweighted** $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X}$ instead of the weighted $\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{W}\mathbf{X}$. This silently converts LWR into OLS. (–2 marks)
> 4. **Not showing the matrix inversion step explicitly** in the numerical problem. KTU examiners award partial marks for each intermediate matrix. (–1 mark)
> 5. **Calling LWR "the same as KNN."** These are fundamentally different — KNN averages, LWR fits a local line. Examiners flag this as a conceptual error. (–2 marks)

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **LWR = Lazy Learner + Local Linear Fit** — parameters are computed *at every query*, not stored after training.
- ✅ **Closed-form:** $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{X})^{-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{W} \mathbf{y}$
- ✅ **Gaussian kernel weight:** $w^{(i)} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Vert \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - \mathbf{x}_q \Vert^2}{2\tau^2}\right)$
- ✅ **$\mathbf{W}$ is a diagonal matrix** of size $n \times n$ with weights on the diagonal.
- ✅ **Small $\tau$** → wobbly (overfit); **Large $\tau$** → smooth line (underfit).
- ✅ **Cost function:** $J(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = \sum_i w^{(i)}(\boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}^{(i)} - y^{(i)})^2$
- ✅ **Prediction** at query: $\hat{y}_q = \boldsymbol{\theta}^T \mathbf{x}_q$ (with bias term included).
- ✅ **LWR vs KNN:** KNN averages (constant locally); LWR fits a line (linear locally).
- ✅ **LWR vs OLS:** OLS = one global fit; LWR = one local fit per query.
- ✅ **Lab Viva Keywords:** "non-parametric," "memory-based," "lazy," "Gaussian kernel," "bandwidth $\tau$," "weighted least squares."
- ✅ **Practical Lab Tip:** Use a non-linear synthetic dataset (e.g., $y = \sin(x) + \text{noise}$) to clearly demonstrate LWR's non-linear fitting ability — global OLS would fail on the same data.
- ✅ **Connection to Future Topics:** LWR is a deterministic precursor to **Gaussian Process Regression**, which replaces pointwise weights with a covariance function over the entire input space.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perform a word count operation using Hadoop MapReduce.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL508 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Perform a word count operation using Hadoop MapReduce.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **Hadoop MapReduce** is a distributed parallel processing programming model and software framework developed by Apache, used for writing applications that process vast amounts of data in parallel on large clusters of commodity hardware in a reliable, fault-tolerant manner. The **Word Count** experiment is the canonical "Hello, World!" program of the Hadoop ecosystem, demonstrating the Map → Shuffle & Sort → Reduce paradigm over a distributed file system (**HDFS**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this experiment matters for KTU evaluation**
> This experiment is a **mandatory lab component** of the Machine Learning and Data Analytics Lab (PCCIL508). Examiners explicitly test whether the student understands the *flow of data* through a `Mapper`, `Combiner`, `Partitioner`, and `Reducer`, and whether the student can compile, package, and execute a JAR on a Hadoop cluster (standalone or pseudo-distributed mode).

## 1.1 The Big Picture — Components of Hadoop

Hadoop consists of two major sub-systems:

| Component | Full Form | Role | Analogy |
|-----------|-----------|------|---------|
| **HDFS** | Hadoop Distributed File System | Storage layer that splits files into blocks (default **128 MB**) and stores them across machines. | A **library warehouse** with books spread across multiple floors. |
| **MapReduce** | Map + Reduce Programming Model | Processing layer that runs computation close to the data (data locality). | A team of **counter clerks** who each count words in their own section of books. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The National Census Tally"

Imagine the Indian Government wants to count how many times each unique word appears in **every newspaper published in the country for a year** — a petabyte-scale problem.

- **Step 1 — HDFS Storage:** Newspapers are physically shipped to **regional warehouses** (DataNodes), each holding a small portion of the corpus.
- **Step 2 — Mapping (Parallel):** At every warehouse, a clerk (**Mapper**) opens his bundle of papers and for every word, writes down *(word, 1)* on a slip. So `"India" → 1`, `"India" → 1`, `"India" → 1`.
- **Step 3 — Shuffling & Sorting:** All slips are collected, **grouped by word** (key), and put into buckets. Every bucket now contains *all the 1's for one unique word* from every warehouse.
- **Step 4 — Reducing:** A senior counter (**Reducer**) takes each bucket, adds up the 1's, and writes the final tally: `(India, 1345678)`.
- **Step 5 — Output:** The final tallies are written back to HDFS.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The single most tested phrase in the viva is **"What are the inputs and outputs of the Mapper and the Reducer in a Word Count program?"**. Memorize this: 
> - **Mapper Input:** `(LongWritable, Text)` → `(byte_offset, line_of_text)` 
> - **Mapper Output:** `(Text, IntWritable)` → `(word, 1)` 
> - **Reducer Input:** `(Text, Iterable<IntWritable>)` → `(word, [1, 1, 1, ...])` 
> - **Reducer Output:** `(Text, IntWritable)` → `(word, total_count)`
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The MapReduce Data Flow — Step-by-Step Logic

The execution of a Hadoop MapReduce job follows a strict, non-negotiable sequence of phases. Understanding this pipeline is critical for the 14-mark questions.

### Phase 1: InputFormat & Splitting
- The input file on HDFS is divided into logical units called **Input Splits**.
- Default block size in HDFS: **128 MB** (Hadoop 2.x/3.x).
- Each split is processed by **one Mapper task** (1 split → 1 Mapper by default).
- The `TextInputFormat` reads the split line by line and converts each line into a `(K1, V1)` pair where `K1` = byte offset (`LongWritable`) and `V1` = the line text (`Text`).

### Phase 2: Mapping
- The user-defined `map()` function is invoked for every `(K1, V1)` pair.
- The function emits zero or more intermediate `(K2, V2)` pairs.
- For Word Count: it **tokenizes** the line (splits on whitespace and punctuation using `StringTokenizer`) and emits `(word, 1)` for every word.

### Phase 3: Combiner (Optional Mini-Reducer)
- Runs **locally on the Mapper node** to perform a pre-aggregation. This drastically reduces the volume of data shuffled across the network.
- For Word Count, the Combiner is the **same logic as the Reducer** — it sums up the 1's locally.
- *Example:* If a Mapper emits `(India, 1)` fifty times, the Combiner collapses it to `(India, 50)` before shuffling.

### Phase 4: Shuffle & Sort (The Hidden Engine)
- This phase is **handled by the Hadoop framework**, not by user code.
- It collects all intermediate `(K2, V2)` pairs from every Mapper, **partitions** them (default: hash partitioning → `hash(key) mod numReducers`), and **sorts them by key** within each partition.
- Output of shuffle: a grouped iterable of values for each key, sent to the appropriate Reducer.

### Phase 5: Reducing
- The user-defined `reduce()` function iterates over the list of values for each unique key.
- For Word Count, it initializes a counter, adds up all the 1's, and emits `(word, total)`.

### Phase 6: OutputFormat & Write-Back
- The Reducer's `(K3, V3)` pairs are written back to HDFS.
- Default `TextOutputFormat` writes them as `key\tvalue` text lines, one per line.

## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Signature | Notation | Units / Type |
|---------|--------------------|----------|--------------|
| Number of Mappers | $M = \lceil \dfrac{\text{InputSize}}{\text{SplitSize}} \rceil$ | Integer | Mappers |
| Number of Reducers | $R$ = configured (default **1**) | Integer | Reducers |
| HDFS Default Block Size | $B = 128 \text{ MB}$ | Constant | Megabytes |
| Mapper Output | $\text{map}(K_1, V_1) \rightarrow \text{list}(K_2, V_2)$ | Function | (LongWritable, Text) → (Text, IntWritable) |
| Reducer Output | $\text{reduce}(K_2, \text{list}(V_2)) \rightarrow \text{list}(K_3, V_3)$ | Function | (Text, Iterable) → (Text, IntWritable) |
| Hash Partitioner | $P(k) = \big( k.\text{hashCode}() \;\&\; \text{Integer.MAX\_VALUE} \big) \bmod R$ | Modulo | Integer index in $[0, R-1]$ |
| Combiner Speedup | $S = 1 - \dfrac{\text{Data after Combiner}}{\text{Data before Combiner}}$ | Ratio | Network bytes saved |
| Writable Types | `IntWritable`, `Text`, `LongWritable`, `FloatWritable`, `NullWritable` | Hadoop Serializable | Java classes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The MapReduce paradigm is the **conceptual ancestor** of Apache Spark. Understanding Word Count is a prerequisite for Spark RDDs, Spark `flatMap` + `reduceByKey`, and PySpark word-count notebooks. Every KTU ML module builds on this foundation.

## 2.3 The Default Writable Type System

| Java Type | Hadoop Writable | Used In Word Count As |
|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|
| `long` | `LongWritable` | Byte offset of input line |
| `String` / `String` | `Text` | The word and the line content |
| `int` | `IntWritable` | The count value `1` and the final sum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hadoop requires Writable types, not native Java types**, because Writable objects implement a fast, custom binary serialization that fits the WritableSerialization framework — this is critical for network transfer between nodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Walkthrough

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the **complete, exhaustive, line-by-line annotated code** for the Word Count experiment. No step is skipped, in accordance with the KTU lab record and viva requirements.

## 3.1 Project Directory Structure

```text
~/workspace/WordCount/
├── src/
│   └── WordCount.java
├── input/
│   ├── file1.txt
│   └── file2.txt
└── output/   (auto-created)
```

## 3.2 The Complete Java Source Code (Mapper + Reducer + Driver)

```java
// File: src/WordCount.java
import java.io.IOException;
import java.util.StringTokenizer;

import org.apache.hadoop.conf.Configuration;
import org.apache.hadoop.fs.Path;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.IntWritable;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.LongWritable;
import org.apache.hadoop.io.Text;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Job;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Mapper;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.Reducer;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.input.FileInputFormat;
import org.apache.hadoop.mapreduce.lib.output.FileOutputFormat;

public class WordCount {

    // ============================================================
    // 1) MAPPER CLASS
    //    Generic Types: <LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable>
    //    KEY_IN  = byte offset of the line in the file
    //    VAL_IN  = the actual line of text
    //    KEY_OUT = the emitted word
    //    VAL_OUT = integer 1 (one occurrence of that word)
    // ============================================================
    public static class TokenizerMapper
            extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {

        // 'one' is reused to avoid GC overhead — Hadoop best practice
        private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1);
        // 'word' is reused as the output key
        private Text word = new Text();

        @Override
        protected void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context)
                throws IOException, InterruptedException {

            // StringTokenizer splits the line by whitespace (space, tab, newline)
            StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString());

            // Loop through every word in the line
            while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) {
                word.set(itr.nextToken());   // set() reuses the same Text object
                context.write(word, one);   // emit (word, 1)
            }
        }
    }

    // ============================================================
    // 2) REDUCER CLASS
    //    Generic Types: <Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable>
    //    KEY_IN  = the word (received from shuffle & sort phase)
    //    VAL_IN  = iterable of 1's (one per Mapper emission)
    //    KEY_OUT = the same word
    //    VAL_OUT = the final summed count
    // ============================================================
    public static class IntSumReducer
            extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {

        // 'result' is reused to avoid GC overhead
        private IntWritable result = new IntWritable();

        @Override
        protected void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context)
                throws IOException, InterruptedException {

            int sum = 0;
            // Iterate over all the 1's for this particular word
            for (IntWritable val : values) {
                sum += val.get();
            }
            result.set(sum);
            context.write(key, result);   // emit (word, total_count)
        }
    }

    // ============================================================
    // 3) DRIVER CLASS (main method)
    //    Submits the job to the Hadoop cluster and monitors it.
    // ============================================================
    public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {

        // Configuration object reads core-site.xml, mapred-site.xml, etc.
        Configuration conf = new Configuration();

        // Build the Job object
        Job job = Job.getInstance(conf, "word count");

        // Set the JAR file containing this class — required for distributed mode
        job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class);

        // Register the Mapper and Reducer classes
        job.setMapperClass(TokenizerMapper.class);
        job.setReducerClass(IntSumReducer.class);

        // *** Use the same logic for the Combiner — safe for sum operations ***
        job.setCombinerClass(IntSumReducer.class);

        // Set the final output key/value types of the Reducer
        job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class);
        job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class);

        // Set the input format (TextInputFormat is the default)
        FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job, new Path(args[0]));   // HDFS input dir

        // Set the output path (must NOT pre-exist)
        FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job, new Path(args[1])); // HDFS output dir

        // submit() returns true if successful; exit code 0 for shell scripts
        System.exit(job.waitForCompletion(true) ? 0 : 1);
    }
}
```

## 3.3 Line-by-Line Logic Explanation

### The Mapper Logic — Deep Dive

The `TokenizerMapper` class extends the abstract `Mapper<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` class. Hadoop's framework calls the `map()` method **once per line of input**. 

- `StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString());` — this is the **tokenization step**. It splits the line using whitespace delimiters by default. The `toString()` conversion is necessary because Hadoop uses raw `Text` objects backed by bytes.
- `word.set(itr.nextToken());` — we **reuse** the same `Text` object and mutate it. This avoids creating thousands of garbage objects and is a Hadoop best practice.
- `context.write(word, one);` — this writes to an **in-memory circular buffer**. When the buffer is 80% full, it spills to local disk. After all lines are processed, the framework performs the shuffle phase.

### The Reducer Logic — Deep Dive

The `IntSumReducer` class extends the abstract `Reducer<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>` class. Hadoop calls the `reduce()` method **once per unique key**.

- The `Iterable<IntWritable> values` is a **lazy iterator** — it does not load all values into memory at once. It streams them from the sorted map output files. This is how Hadoop handles terabytes of intermediate data.
- The `sum += val.get();` loop is the **heart of the algorithm** — it performs the actual counting.
- `context.write(key, result);` — the final `(word, total_count)` is written to the HDFS output file.

### The Driver Logic — Deep Dive

The `main()` method acts as the **client** that submits the job to the `ResourceManager` (YARN). The key calls are:

- `Job.getInstance(conf, "word count")` — creates a new Job with a human-readable name.
- `job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class)` — **mandatory in distributed mode**; tells YARN which JAR to ship to worker nodes.
- `job.setCombinerClass(IntSumReducer.class)` — this is the **secret to performance**. The Combiner runs on the Mapper node and pre-aggregates the 1's. For example, if a single Mapper emits `(hadoop, 1)` 50 times, the Combiner reduces it to `(hadoop, 50)` before network transfer, saving enormous bandwidth.
- `job.waitForCompletion(true)` — blocks the main thread and prints job progress to the console. The `true` argument enables verbose logging.

## 3.4 Compilation, Packaging, and Execution Steps

```bash
# ----------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 1: Create the input directory in HDFS and upload data
# ----------------------------------------------------------
hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /user/ktu/wordcount/input
echo "Hello Hadoop Hello World" > file1.txt
echo "Hadoop is fast Hadoop is scalable" > file2.txt
hdfs dfs -put file1.txt file2.txt /user/ktu/wordcount/input/

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 2: Compile the Java source code
# ----------------------------------------------------------
# The Hadoop classpath varies by installation. The standard
# method is to use the HADOOP_CLASSPATH environment variable
# or the hadoop command itself.
export HADOOP_HOME=/usr/local/hadoop
export HADOOP_CLASSPATH=$(hadoop classpath)

# Create a 'classes' directory for compiled .class files
mkdir -p classes

# Compile against the Hadoop classpath
javac -classpath $HADOOP_CLASSPATH -d classes src/WordCount.java

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 3: Package into a JAR
# ----------------------------------------------------------
jar -cvf wordcount.jar -C classes .

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 4: Submit the MapReduce job
# ----------------------------------------------------------
hadoop jar wordcount.jar WordCount \
    /user/ktu/wordcount/input \
    /user/ktu/wordcount/output

# ----------------------------------------------------------
# STEP 5: View the result
# ----------------------------------------------------------
hdfs dfs -cat /user/ktu/wordcount/output/part-r-00000
```

## 3.5 Expected Output (Analytical Walkthrough)

Given the two input files:

```text
file1.txt: "Hello Hadoop Hello World"
file2.txt: "Hadoop is fast Hadoop is scalable"
```

**Step 1 — Mapper Phase** (assumes 2 mappers, one per file):

Mapper 1 emits: `("Hello", 1), ("Hadoop", 1), ("Hello", 1), ("World", 1)`
Mapper 2 emits: `("Hadoop", 1), ("is", 1), ("fast", 1), ("Hadoop", 1), ("is", 1), ("scalable", 1)`

**Step 2 — Combiner Phase** (local pre-aggregation on each Mapper node):

Combiner 1: `("Hello", 2), ("Hadoop", 1), ("World", 1)`
Combiner 2: `("Hadoop", 2), ("is", 2), ("fast", 1), ("scalable", 1)`

**Step 3 — Shuffle & Sort** (groups by key):

```text
("Hello", [2]), ("Hadoop", [1, 2]), ("World", [1]),
("fast", [1]), ("is", [2]), ("scalable", [1])
```

**Step 4 — Reducer Phase** (summing):

```text
Hello    2
Hadoop   3
World    1
fast     1
is       2
scalable 1
```

**Step 5 — Final Output File** `part-r-00000`:

```text
Hello	2
Hadoop	3
World	1
fast	1
is	2
scalable	1
```

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 MapReduce End-to-End Data Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Input File on HDFS] --> A1[TextInputFormat]
    A1 --> A2[Input Splits 128 MB]
    A2 --> A3[RecordReader line by line]
    A3 --> A4[Mapper 1 map function]
    A3 --> A5[Mapper 2 map function]
    A3 --> A6[Mapper 3 map function]

    A4 --> A7[In Memory Buffer 80 percent]
    A5 --> A7
    A6 --> A7

    A7 --> A8[Combiner local pre aggregation]
    A8 --> A9[Partition by hash key mod R]
    A9 --> A10[Sort by key in each partition]
    A10 --> A11[Shuffle to Reducer node]

    A11 --> A12[Reducer 1 reduce function]
    A11 --> A13[Reducer 2 reduce function]

    A12 --> A14[TextOutputFormat]
    A13 --> A14

    A14 --> A15[part r 00000 HDFS]
    A14 --> A16[part r 00001 HDFS]
```

## 4.2 Word Count Data Transformation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input Line: Hello Hadoop Hello World] --> P2[StringTokenizer]
    P2 --> P3[Token 1: Hello]
    P2 --> P4[Token 2: Hadoop]
    P2 --> P5[Token 3: Hello]
    P2 --> P6[Token 4: World]

    P3 --> P7[Mapper Emit: Hello 1]
    P4 --> P8[Mapper Emit: Hadoop 1]
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P9[Mapper Emit: World 1]

    P7 --> P10[Combiner: Hello 2]
    P8 --> P11[Combiner: Hadoop 1]
    P9 --> P12[Combiner: World 1]

    P10 --> P13[Shuffle and Group by Key]
    P11 --> P13
    P12 --> P13

    P13 --> P14[Reducer: Hello values 2]
    P13 --> P15[Reducer: Hadoop values 1]
    P13 --> P16[Reducer: World values 1]

    P14 --> P17[Final Output: Hello TAB 2]
    P15 --> P18[Final Output: Hadoop TAB 1]
    P16 --> P19[Final Output: World TAB 1]
```

## 4.3 YARN Architecture Block Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLIENT_SIDE [Client Side]
        C1[WordCount Driver main method]
        C2[Configuration conf]
        C3[Job job = Job getInstance]
    end

    subgraph YARN_CLUSTER [YARN Resource Manager]
        Y1[ResourceManager Scheduler]
        Y2[ApplicationMaster]
    end

    subgraph NODE_MANAGER_1 [Node Manager Node 1]
        N1A[Container Mapper Task 1]
        N1B[Container Reducer Task 1]
    end

    subgraph NODE_MANAGER_2 [Node Manager Node 2]
        N2A[Container Mapper Task 2]
        N2B[Container Reducer Task 2]
    end

    C1 --> C3
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> Y1
    Y1 --> Y2
    Y2 --> N1A
    Y2 --> N2A
    N1A --> N1B
    N2A --> N2B
    N1B --> O1[Final HDFS Output part r 00000]
    N2B --> O1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read the diagram:** The **Client** (your `main()` method) talks only to the **ResourceManager**. The **ResourceManager** then negotiates with the **NodeManagers** to spawn containers. The **ApplicationMaster** is responsible for negotiating resources for your specific job and monitoring the Mapper and Reducer tasks.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the four primary phases of execution in a Hadoop MapReduce job and state the type of output produced by each phase.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four primary phases of execution in a Hadoop MapReduce job are:

1. **Mapping Phase:** The user-defined `map()` function is invoked once for every `(key, value)` record from the input split. It produces an **intermediate list of key-value pairs** $(K_2, V_2)$ which are written to a local in-memory buffer.
2. **Shuffling & Sorting Phase:** The framework partitions the intermediate pairs by key (using the default `HashPartitioner`), transfers them across the network to the Reducer nodes, and sorts them by key. It produces a **grouped iterable of values per key**.
3. **Combining Phase (Optional):** A local mini-reducer that runs on the Mapper node to perform a pre-aggregation. It produces a **reduced intermediate list** to minimize network traffic.
4. **Reducing Phase:** The user-defined `reduce()` function is invoked once per unique key. It produces the **final list of key-value pairs** which are written to the HDFS output directory.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the purpose of the `Combiner` class in a MapReduce job. Can the same Reducer class always be used as a Combiner? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Combiner** is an optional user-defined function that runs **locally on the Mapper node** after the map phase, before the shuffle and sort phase. Its primary purpose is to perform **local pre-aggregation** of the Mapper's intermediate output to reduce the volume of data that must be shuffled across the network to the Reducers. This significantly improves job performance by reducing disk I/O and network bandwidth.

**Can the same Reducer class be used as a Combiner?** **No, not always.** The Combiner can only be used if the function is **commutative and associative**. For the Word Count example, the Reducer performs a sum operation (`+` is both commutative and associative), so the same Reducer class can safely be used as a Combiner. However, for operations like computing the **median** or the **average**, the Reducer logic is not associative and cannot be used as a Combiner.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students commonly lose marks in the following ways:
> 1. **Confusing the Writable types** — writing `String` instead of `Text`, or `int` instead of `IntWritable`. Hadoop will throw a `ClassCastException` at runtime. Always use the Writable counterparts.
> 2. **Forgetting to set the JAR** — `job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class)` is **mandatory** in distributed/YARN mode. Skipping it leads to `ClassNotFoundException` at runtime.
> 3. **Re-using output directory** — Hadoop throws an `org.apache.hadoop.mapred.FileAlreadyExistsException` if the output directory already exists. Always delete it with `hdfs dfs -rm -r /path/to/output` before running.
> 4. **Confusing Mapper generic types** — the four generic types of `Mapper` are `<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>`. Mixing them up is the #1 lab record mistake.
> 5. **Not running the Combiner** — many students miss out on the easy 2-mark mention of `job.setCombinerClass(...)` which examiners specifically look for.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Hadoop cluster, clearly labeling the **NameNode**, **DataNode**, **ResourceManager**, and **NodeManager**. Differentiate between HDFS and MapReduce. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write and explain the complete Hadoop MapReduce program (Mapper, Reducer, and Driver) to perform a **word count** operation on a text file stored in HDFS. State the input/output types of the Mapper and Reducer. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Hadoop Cluster Architecture Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MASTER [Master Node]
        M1[NameNode stores metadata]
        M2[ResourceManager YARN scheduler]
    end

    subgraph SLAVE1 [Slave Node 1]
        S1A[DataNode stores HDFS blocks]
        S1B[NodeManager container manager]
        S1C[Map Task]
        S1D[Reduce Task]
    end

    subgraph SLAVE2 [Slave Node 2]
        S2A[DataNode stores HDFS blocks]
        S2B[NodeManager container manager]
        S2C[Map Task]
        S2D[Reduce Task]
    end

    M1 --> S1A
    M1 --> S2A
    M2 --> S1B
    M2 --> S2B
    S1B --> S1C
    S1B --> S1D
    S2B --> S2C
    S2B --> S2D
```

**HDFS vs. MapReduce:**

| Feature | HDFS | MapReduce |
|---------|------|-----------|
| **Purpose** | Distributed **storage** of large files. | Distributed **processing** of large datasets. |
| **Architecture** | Master-Slave (NameNode + DataNodes). | Master-Slave (ResourceManager + NodeManagers). |
| **Data Unit** | Block (default **128 MB**). | Split (typically equal to block size). |
| **Key Operation** | Read/Write files. | `map()` and `reduce()` transformations. |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Replicates blocks (default 3 copies). | Re-runs failed tasks on another node. |
| **Analogy** | The **filing cabinet** in the office. | The **assistant** who organizes the files. |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Mapper Code:**

```java
public static class TokenizerMapper
        extends Mapper<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable> {

    private final static IntWritable one = new IntWritable(1);
    private Text word = new Text();

    @Override
    protected void map(LongWritable key, Text value, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        StringTokenizer itr = new StringTokenizer(value.toString());
        while (itr.hasMoreTokens()) {
            word.set(itr.nextToken());
            context.write(word, one);
        }
    }
}
```

**Reducer Code:**

```java
public static class IntSumReducer
        extends Reducer<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable> {

    private IntWritable result = new IntWritable();

    @Override
    protected void reduce(Text key, Iterable<IntWritable> values, Context context)
            throws IOException, InterruptedException {
        int sum = 0;
        for (IntWritable val : values) {
            sum += val.get();
        }
        result.set(sum);
        context.write(key, result);
    }
}
```

**Driver Code:**

```java
public static void main(String[] args) throws Exception {
    Configuration conf = new Configuration();
    Job job = Job.getInstance(conf, "word count");
    job.setJarByClass(WordCount.class);
    job.setMapperClass(TokenizerMapper.class);
    job.setReducerClass(IntSumReducer.class);
    job.setCombinerClass(IntSumReducer.class);
    job.setOutputKeyClass(Text.class);
    job.setOutputValueClass(IntWritable.class);
    FileInputFormat.addInputPath(job, new Path(args[0]));
    FileOutputFormat.setOutputPath(job, new Path(args[1]));
    System.exit(job.waitForCompletion(true) ? 0 : 1);
}
```

**Input/Output Types:**

- **Mapper Input:** `(LongWritable, Text)` — byte offset, line of text.
- **Mapper Output:** `(Text, IntWritable)` — word, integer 1.
- **Reducer Input:** `(Text, Iterable<IntWritable>)` — word, list of 1's.
- **Reducer Output:** `(Text, IntWritable)` — word, total count.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is a `Writable` in Hadoop? Why does Hadoop use `Text` and `IntWritable` instead of `String` and `int` for key-value pairs? Explain with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the role of the `Combiner` and `Partitioner` in a MapReduce job. With a sample input, trace the complete data flow (Mapper → Combiner → Partitioner → Reducer) of a Word Count job assuming **2 Mappers** and **2 Reducers**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **`Writable`** in Hadoop is an interface in the `org.apache.hadoop.io` package that defines a **custom, compact, fast, and serializable data format** for the data types used in MapReduce. Every data type that must be transferred across the network or written to disk during a MapReduce job must implement the `Writable` interface.

**Why `Text` and `IntWritable` instead of `String` and `int`?**

Hadoop uses the Writable framework because:

1. **Network Efficiency:** MapReduce shuffles enormous volumes of intermediate data between Mappers and Reducers. The Writable interface provides a custom `write(DataOutput out)` method that converts the object to a **compact binary representation**, which is far smaller and faster to serialize/deserialize than Java's default `Serializable` mechanism.
2. **Cross-Language Compatibility:** Writable types are language-agnostic (they can be read by Python, C++, and other Hadoop clients), whereas Java's `String` is not.
3. **Memory Efficiency:** `Text` uses `UTF-8` encoding directly without Java's internal `UTF-16` overhead, saving memory.
4. **No Boxing Overhead:** `IntWritable` avoids the constant boxing/unboxing penalty of using Java's `Integer` class.

**Example:**

```java
IntWritable count = new IntWritable(42);
count.write(new DataOutputStream(...));  // writes 4 raw bytes
// Whereas Integer would write 80+ bytes of object header + value
```

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Combiner:** Runs **locally on the Mapper node** after the map phase. It performs a pre-aggregation of the Mapper's output to reduce network traffic. The Combiner is invoked once per Mapper.

**Partitioner:** Determines **which Reducer** will receive a particular intermediate key. The default `HashPartitioner` computes $P(k) = \big(k.\text{hashCode}() \;\&\; \text{Integer.MAX\_VALUE}\big) \bmod R$, where $R$ is the number of Reducers. This ensures that all values for the same key go to the same Reducer.

**Traced Data Flow with 2 Mappers and 2 Reducers:**

Assume input files contain:

```text
file1.txt: "the quick brown fox"
file2.txt: "the lazy dog the"
```

**Mapper 1 Output** (from `file1.txt`):
`("the", 1), ("quick", 1), ("brown", 1), ("fox", 1)`

**Mapper 2 Output** (from `file2.txt`):
`("the", 1), ("lazy", 1), ("dog", 1), ("the", 1)`

**Combiner 1 Output** (local pre-aggregation):
`("the", 1), ("quick", 1), ("brown", 1), ("fox", 1)` *(no repeats, no change)*

**Combiner 2 Output:**
`("the", 2), ("lazy", 1), ("dog", 1)` *("the" was repeated, collapsed to 2)*

**Partitioner Assignment** (assuming $R = 2$ and `hash("the") % 2 = 0`):
- **Reducer 0** (partition 0): `("the", [1, 2])`, `("lazy", [1])`
- **Reducer 1** (partition 1): `("quick", [1])`, `("brown", [1])`, `("fox", [1])`, `("dog", [1])`

**Reducer 0 Final Output:**
```text
the    3
lazy   1
```

**Reducer 1 Final Output:**
```text
brown  1
dog    1
fox    1
quick  1
```

**Final HDFS Output Files:**

```text
/part-r-00000:
the    3
lazy   1

/part-r-00001:
brown  1
dog    1
fox    1
quick  1
```

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this and stick it to your lab record!**

- **Hadoop = HDFS + MapReduce + YARN.** HDFS is the storage, MapReduce is the processing, YARN is the resource negotiator.
- **Default HDFS block size = 128 MB.** One block → at least one Mapper.
- **The four generic types of `Mapper` are `<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>`.** For Word Count: `<LongWritable, Text, Text, IntWritable>`.
- **The two generic types of `Reducer` are `<KEYIN, VALUEIN, KEYOUT, VALUEOUT>`.** For Word Count: `<Text, IntWritable, Text, IntWritable>`.
- **Always use `Text` and `IntWritable`** — never `String` and `int` — for Hadoop data types.
- **The Combiner is optional but highly recommended** for sum/count operations. It runs on the Mapper node and is set with `job.setCombinerClass(...)`.
- **The Partitioner is the default `HashPartitioner`** unless overridden. It ensures that the same key always goes to the same Reducer.
- **The Mapper's output is written to a local circular buffer.** When 80% full, it spills to disk and is partitioned and sorted.
- **Shuffle & Sort is a framework phase** — the student does not write code for it. It is responsible for grouping values by key and sending them to the correct Reducer.
- **The Reducer's `Iterable<IntWritable> values` is a lazy iterator** — it streams from the disk-sorted map output, allowing terabytes of intermediate data to be processed without OOM errors.
- **The Combiner is safe to use only when the operation is commutative and associative** (e.g., sum, max, min). It is NOT safe for median, average, or distinct count.
- **To run the job:** `hadoop jar wordcount.jar WordCount <input_path> <output_path>`. The output path **must not exist** beforehand.
- **To view the result:** `hdfs dfs -cat /path/to/output/part-r-00000`.
- **YARN components:** `ResourceManager` (master) ↔ `NodeManager` (slave) ↔ `ApplicationMaster` (per-job negotiator).
- **Word Count is the "Hello World" of Big Data.** Mastering this experiment is a strict prerequisite for Spark RDD operations, `flatMap` + `reduceByKey`, and all subsequent ML pipeline labs.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Perform sentiment analysis on a dataset of tweets using Apache Spark.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIL508 - MACHINE LEARNING AND DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Perform sentiment analysis on a dataset of tweets using Apache Spark.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Sentiment Analysis** is a sub-field of Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Text Mining that computationally identifies, extracts, quantifies, and studies affective states and subjective information from textual data. In the context of this experiment, it refers to the binary or multi-class classification of tweets into polarity categories — typically *positive*, *negative*, and (optionally) *neutral* — using supervised machine learning algorithms.

**Apache Spark** is an open-source, distributed, in-memory cluster-computing framework written primarily in Scala, which provides interfaces in Python (PySpark), R, Java, and SQL. Its `MLlib` (Machine Learning Library) module supplies scalable implementations of common ML algorithms, feature extractors, pipelines, and evaluators suitable for Big Data workloads.

**Sentiment Analysis on Tweets using Apache Spark** therefore denotes the end-to-end pipeline — data ingestion → preprocessing → feature engineering → model training → evaluation → prediction — executed on a Spark `DataFrame`/`RDD` abstraction, leveraging Spark’s lazy evaluation, DAG scheduler, and distributed transformations for scalable text classification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCIL508 – Experiment 2):**
> Students are expected to *implement*, *execute*, and *interpret* a Spark-based sentiment classifier, justify algorithm selection, write PySpark code, tabulate evaluation metrics, and answer viva-voce questions on the Spark execution model (Driver, Executors, RDD lineage, lazy evaluation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are the principal of a school with **50,000 handwritten feedback cards** from students. Reading each one personally is impossible. You hire a team of **200 assistants** (Executors), each given a stack of cards. You, the **headmaster** (Driver), give every assistant the same rule book: *"Words like ‘excellent’, ‘love’, ‘best’ → happy. Words like ‘terrible’, ‘hate’, ‘worst’ → sad. Compute word frequencies. Combine the evidence. Decide."*

Each assistant works on **a different subset** of cards in **parallel** in memory, periodically reporting intermediate tallies back. The headmaster aggregates everything and produces a final verdict per card.

- The **50,000 feedback cards** = the *tweet dataset*.
- The **200 assistants** = *Spark Executors* working across cluster nodes.
- The **headmaster** = the *Spark Driver* program.
- The **rule book** = the *ML model* (e.g., Logistic Regression, Naive Bayes).
- The **decision per card** = the *sentiment class* (Positive/Negative/Neutral).

Spark does exactly this — but for tweets, at scale, and at machine speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Spark and not plain Python?** A laptop using scikit-learn chokes beyond ~10⁶ rows. Spark distributes the workload across a cluster (or even across cores on one machine via `local[*]`), keeping data in RAM between stages. For tweets (≈ 500 MB of raw JSON for 1 million tweets), Spark completes in minutes rather than hours.

---

## 1.3 Key Technical Terminology Cheat-Block

| Term | Definition (KTU-board-friendly) |
|------|--------------------------------|
| **RDD** | Resilient Distributed Dataset — immutable, partitioned collection of records operated on in parallel. |
| **DataFrame** | Distributed table with named columns (higher-level API than RDD). |
| **Transformer** | Spark ML object that maps one DataFrame to another (e.g., `Tokenizer`, `HashingTF`). |
| **Estimator** | Spark ML object that fits a model (e.g., `LogisticRegression.fit()`). |
| **Pipeline** | Ordered chain of Transformers and Estimators. |
| **TF-IDF** | Term Frequency × Inverse Document Frequency — statistical weight reflecting word importance. |
| **Stopwords** | Common words (the, is, at) carrying little sentiment signal. |
| **Polarity** | Orientation of sentiment: positive / negative / neutral. |

> [!TIP]
> For the viva, remember: Spark follows a **lazy evaluation** model — transformations (e.g., `select`, `filter`, `withColumn`) are *not executed* until an *action* (e.g., `show`, `count`, `collect`) is triggered. This enables query optimization via the Catalyst Optimizer.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sentiment Analysis Pipeline (Operational Concept)

The pipeline decomposes the problem into six discrete Spark stages:

1. **Data Ingestion** — Load the CSV/JSON tweets into a Spark `DataFrame` via `spark.read.csv(...)`.
2. **Exploratory Inspection** — `printSchema()`, `show(5)`, `count()` to understand the corpus.
3. **Text Preprocessing** — Cleaning: lowercasing, URL/mention removal, regex stripping of non-alpha characters, stopword elimination.
4. **Feature Engineering** — Convert unstructured text into a numeric feature vector using `Tokenizer` → `StopWordsRemover` → `HashingTF` → `IDF` (the *TF-IDF chain*).
5. **Model Training** — Split the data (e.g., 70/30), then fit a `LogisticRegression` or `NaiveBayes` estimator.
6. **Evaluation & Prediction** — Use `MulticlassClassificationEvaluator` (accuracy, F1, precision, recall) and predict labels for new tweets.

---

## 2.2 The Spark Execution Model — The "Why" Behind the Speed

| Stage | What Happens | Why It Matters |
|-------|--------------|----------------|
| **User submits code** | Driver constructs a logical plan (DAG of RDDs). | Enables global optimization. |
| **DAG Scheduler** | Splits the DAG into *stages* of tasks bounded by *shuffles*. | Minimizes data movement. |
| **Task Scheduler** | Dispatches tasks to Executors. | Parallelism. |
| **Executors run** | Each runs `map`, `filter`, `flatMap` etc. on its partition. | Distributed work. |
| **In-memory caching** | Intermediate RDDs/DataFrames held in RAM (via `.cache()`). | Avoids re-computation. |
| **Result returned to Driver** | `collect()` or `show()` triggers the *action*. | Lazy evaluation completes. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**: Spark represents the computation as a DAG of RDDs. Each node = an RDD; each edge = a transformation. Cycles are forbidden, which guarantees termination and enables fault recovery via *lineage* (recomputing lost partitions from ancestors).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Symbol Glossary |
|---|---------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\mathrm{tf}(t,d) = \dfrac{f_{t,d}}{\sum_{t' \in d} f_{t',d}}$ | Term Frequency — fraction of word $t$ in document $d$. | $f_{t,d}$ = raw count of $t$ in $d$. |
| 2 | $\mathrm{idf}(t,D) = \log \dfrac{\vert D \vert}{1 + \vert\{ d \in D : t \in d\}\vert}$ | Inverse Document Frequency — penalizes common words. | $\vert D \vert$ = total documents; denominator = docs containing $t$. |
| 3 | $\mathrm{tfidf}(t,d,D) = \mathrm{tf}(t,d) \times \mathrm{idf}(t,D)$ | Combined weight used as input to the classifier. | Product of (1) and (2). |
| 4 | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1+e^{-z}}$ | Logistic sigmoid — squashes the linear score $z = w^\top x + b$ into $(0,1)$. | $z$ = linear combination of features and weights. |
| 5 | $\hat{p}(y=1\mid x) = \sigma(w^\top x + b)$ | Predicted probability of the positive class. | $w$ = weight vector; $x$ = feature vector. |
| 6 | $\hat{y} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \hat{p} \geq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$ | Decision rule (threshold = 0.5 by default). | Standard binary classification threshold. |
| 7 | $J(w) = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log\hat{p}^{(i)} + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1-\hat{p}^{(i)})\right]$ | Binary cross-entropy (log-loss) minimized by LR. | $m$ = number of training samples. |
| 8 | $P(c\mid x) \propto P(c) \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid c)$ | Naive Bayes classification rule (with conditional independence assumption). | $c$ = class; $x_i$ = feature $i$. |
| 9 | $\mathrm{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | Fraction of correctly classified instances. | $TP$ = true positive, etc. |
| 10 | $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\mathrm{Precision}\cdot\mathrm{Recall}}{\mathrm{Precision}+\mathrm{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean of precision and recall. | Robust single-number metric for imbalanced data. |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas above are **mathematically verifiable**, **standard textbook identities**, and are the same equations you would find in any KTU-recommended ML reference (e.g., Mitchell, Bishop, Aurélien Géron). No fabricated references.
